topics.title: Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **HISTOGRAM — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **histogram** is a graphical representation of a **frequency distribution** of continuous numerical data, organized into a series of adjacent, non-overlapping intervals (called *bins* or *class intervals*). The horizontal axis represents the **measured variable (class interval)** and the vertical axis represents the **frequency (count), relative frequency, or frequency density** of observations falling within each bin. The adjacent rectangles share a common boundary, distinguishing it from a bar chart.

In the discipline of **Measurements and Industrial Instrumentation**, the histogram is one of the seven basic tools of quality control (Ishikawa's 7 QC Tools). It is used to:
- Visualize the **statistical distribution** of a measured process variable.
- Identify the **central tendency (mean)**, **spread (variance)**, and **shape (skewness/kurtosis)** of measurement data.
- Detect **outliers**, **bimodality**, and **process shifts** in industrial measurement systems.
- Provide empirical validation of the **Probability Density Function (PDF)** that the data is assumed to follow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PERUT527 – Module 1):** Histogram is a foundational statistical visualization topic within the introductory module on measurement fundamentals. It links the abstract notion of a *population* with the practical reality of *sampled measurement data*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 🍎 The Apple Basket Analogy

Imagine a fruit vendor has **200 apples** of slightly different sizes (say, diameter between **6 cm** and **10 cm**). He wants to know the most common apple size he is selling. So he does the following:

1. He sorts the apples into **boxes (bins)** labelled *6–6.5 cm*, *6.5–7 cm*, *7–7.5 cm*, ..., *9.5–10 cm*.
2. He **counts** how many apples fall into each box (frequency).
3. He draws **adjacent rectangles** (no gaps!) whose height is proportional to the count in each box.

That drawing is a **histogram**! The tallest rectangle instantly tells the vendor: *"Most of my apples are around 7.5–8 cm."*

### 🔧 Engineering Analogy (Instrumentation)

Consider a **pressure transducer** in a process plant taking **500 readings** of steam pressure (in bar). A histogram of those 500 readings:
- Tells the engineer the **most likely operating pressure**.
- Reveals whether the pressure is **stable** (narrow, tall histogram) or **erratic** (wide, flat histogram).
- Shows if there is a **systematic offset** in the sensor (lopsided, skewed histogram).

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Histogram Matters in Instrumentation:**
> A single measurement is just a number. A **histogram turns a thousand numbers into a story** about the instrument's behaviour and the process's health.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bin / Class Interval** | A contiguous range of values on the x-axis | e.g., `[6.0, 6.5)` cm |
| **Bin Width (h)** | The width of one class interval | Same unit as variable |
| **Frequency (fᵢ)** | Count of observations in bin *i* | Dimensionless integer |
| **Relative Frequency** | $f_i / N$ where $N$ is total observations | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Frequency Density** | $f_i / (N \cdot h)$ — area becomes normalized | 1 / (unit of x) |
| **Class Mark / Midpoint** | $(L_i + L_{i+1})/2$ | Same unit as variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Histogram vs Bar Chart — A KTU favourite question!**
> - In a **bar chart**, the x-axis is *categorical* (e.g., cities, products) and the bars have **gaps**.
> - In a **histogram**, the x-axis is *continuous numerical* (e.g., pressure, temperature) and the bars are **touching (no gaps)** because the variable is continuous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Histogram Construction

A histogram is constructed by following a **systematic procedure**. Understanding the rationale behind each step is essential for KTU board-level answers.

### Step 1 — Data Collection
- Gather $N$ measured samples of a variable $x$.
- $N$ is the **sample size**. KTU convention: $N \geq 30$ for a meaningful histogram; for normal-distribution inference, $N \geq 50$.

### Step 2 — Determine the Range
- Compute $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$.
- This is the **total spread** of the data.

### Step 3 — Choose the Number of Bins (k)
- **Sturges' Rule** (classical KTU formula):
$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$$
- **Square-Root Rule** (simple, also accepted in KTU):
$$k = \sqrt{N}$$
- **Rice Rule** (used in quality engineering):
$$k = 2 \cdot N^{1/3}$$
- Trade-off: too few bins → loss of detail; too many bins → noisy, multi-modal false patterns.

### Step 4 — Compute the Bin Width (h)
$$h = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{k}$$
The bin width is **rounded up** to a "convenient" measurement value (e.g., 0.5, 1, 5, 10) to make the histogram readable.

### Step 5 — Establish the Bin Boundaries
- The **first lower boundary** is often taken slightly below $x_{min}$ (e.g., $x_{min} - h/2$).
- Subsequent boundaries are obtained by adding $h$ repeatedly.
- Each bin is **left-closed, right-open** by convention: $[L_i, L_{i+1})$.

### Step 6 — Tabulate Frequencies (Tally)
- Tally each $x_j$ into the correct bin.
- Sum of all frequencies must equal $N$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i = N$$

### Step 7 — Plot the Rectangles
- Draw a rectangle for each bin: width = $h$, height = $f_i$ (or relative frequency).
- **Adjacent rectangles share a common vertical edge** (no gap).

---

## 2.2 Shape Analysis — The "Story" the Histogram Tells

A histogram's silhouette gives immediate diagnostic information about a measurement process.

| Shape | Visual Cue | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric / Bell-shaped** | Mirror image about the centre | Process is stable; data approximates a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** |
| **Right-skewed (positive skew)** | Long tail to the right | Sensor saturation, upper specification limit hits, exponential-type process |
| **Left-skewed (negative skew)** | Long tail to the left | Lower measurement limit hits, e.g., zero-floored instruments |
| **Bimodal** | Two distinct peaks | Two different process states / two different populations mixed (e.g., day vs night shift) |
| **Uniform (rectangular)** | Flat top | Poor resolution, rounded values, or data drawn from a uniform distribution |
| **Peaked (leptokurtic)** | Very sharp central peak, thin tails | Over-controlled process, sensor with low repeatability variability |
| **Plateau / Truncated** | Flat top, sharp drop at ends | Sensor range limit; values clipped at min/max of instrument |

---

## 2.3 Measures of Central Tendency, Spread & Shape

For a histogram with $k$ bins, where $m_i$ is the midpoint of bin $i$ and $f_i$ is its frequency:

### 📊 Mean (estimated from histogram)
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i \cdot m_i$$

### 📊 Variance
$$s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i \cdot (m_i - \bar{x})^2$$

### 📊 Standard Deviation
$$s = \sqrt{s^2}$$

### 📊 Skewness (Pearson's second skewness coefficient)
$$Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$$

For grouped data using histogram midpoints:
$$\text{Median} \approx L_m + h \cdot \left( \frac{N/2 - F_{m-1}}{f_m} \right)$$
where $L_m$ = lower boundary of median class, $F_{m-1}$ = cumulative frequency before median class, $f_m$ = frequency of median class.

### 📊 Relative Frequency Form
$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{N}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i = 1$$
The **area** under a relative-frequency histogram equals **1**, making it an empirical estimate of the probability density function.

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All Histogram Formulas for KTU PERUT527**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of bins (Sturges) | $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$ | Dimensionless; $N$ = sample size |
| 2 | Number of bins (Square root) | $k = \sqrt{N}$ | Simpler alternative |
| 3 | Bin width | $h = R / k = (x_{max} - x_{min}) / k$ | Same unit as $x$ |
| 4 | Relative frequency | $p_i = f_i / N$ | $\sum p_i = 1$ |
| 5 | Frequency density | $fd_i = f_i / (N \cdot h)$ | Used when bin widths differ |
| 6 | Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum f_i m_i$ | $m_i$ = bin midpoint |
| 7 | Variance | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $N-1$ for unbiased estimate |
| 8 | Std. deviation | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Same unit as $x$ |
| 9 | Coefficient of variation | $CV = s / \bar{x}$ | Dimensionless; indicates sensor precision |
| 10 | Pearson's skewness | $Sk = 3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})/s$ | $Sk=0$: symmetric |
| 11 | Median (grouped) | $L_m + h \cdot \dfrac{N/2 - F_{m-1}}{f_m}$ | Linear interpolation |
| 12 | Mode (grouped) | $L_{mo} + h \cdot \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2}$ | $d_1, d_2$ = freq. diffs of mode bin |
| 13 | Total area (rel. freq. hist.) | $\sum p_i = 1$ | Empirical PDF property |
| 14 | Modal class | Bin with highest $f_i$ | Tallest bar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):**
> - **Calibration Labs:** Build histogram of repeated measurements of a known standard → estimate instrument **repeatability** and **bias**.
> - **Process Control:** Daily histogram of fill-volume in a bottling line → detect **drift** before it violates tolerances.
> - **Sensor Selection:** Compare histograms from two candidate flowmeters → the one with the **narrower, centred** histogram offers better precision and accuracy.
> - **Six-Sigma / SPC:** Histogram is the gateway to the **Normal Probability Plot** and the **Control Chart**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Pattern):**
> A digital temperature indicator in a chemical reactor is used to record the reactor wall temperature. The following **60 readings** (in °C) were obtained in a one-hour stability test:
>
> | Bin (°C) | Tally | Frequency $f_i$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | 99.5 – 100.5 | II | 2 |
> | 100.5 – 101.5 | IIIII IIIII IIIII III | 18 |
> | 101.5 – 102.5 | IIIII IIIII IIIII IIII | 24 |
> | 102.5 – 103.5 | IIIII IIIII I | 11 |
> | 103.5 – 104.5 | IIIII | 5 |
>
> **(a)** Plot the histogram. **(b)** Calculate the mean, variance, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. **(c)** Determine the median and Pearson's skewness coefficient. Comment on the distribution shape.

---

### Part (a) — Histogram Plot

The bin width is $h = 1.0$ °C, all bins are uniform, so the histogram is plotted with frequency $f_i$ on the y-axis directly. The tallest bar is the **101.5 – 102.5 °C** bin with $f = 24$.

**Mermaid block representation of the histogram structure** is given in SECTION 4. The actual plot is a 5-rectangle column graph with heights 2, 18, 24, 11, 5.

---

### Part (b) — Mean, Variance, Standard Deviation, CV

#### Step 1: Compute the midpoints $m_i$ of each bin

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 &= \frac{99.5 + 100.5}{2} = 100.0 \ ^\circ\text{C} \\
m_2 &= \frac{100.5 + 101.5}{2} = 101.0 \ ^\circ\text{C} \\
m_3 &= \frac{101.5 + 102.5}{2} = 102.0 \ ^\circ\text{C} \\
m_4 &= \frac{102.5 + 103.5}{2} = 103.0 \ ^\circ\text{C} \\
m_5 &= \frac{103.5 + 104.5}{2} = 104.0 \ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

The midpoints are perfectly spaced by 1 °C each because the bins are uniform.

#### Step 2: Verify the total sample size

$$
N = \sum f_i = 2 + 18 + 24 + 11 + 5 = 60
$$

This matches the given $N = 60$. ✓

#### Step 3: Compute the products $f_i \cdot m_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 \cdot m_1 &= 2 \times 100.0 = 200.0 \\
f_2 \cdot m_2 &= 18 \times 101.0 = 1818.0 \\
f_3 \cdot m_3 &= 24 \times 102.0 = 2448.0 \\
f_4 \cdot m_4 &= 11 \times 103.0 = 1133.0 \\
f_5 \cdot m_5 &= 5 \times 104.0 = 520.0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Sum and compute the mean

$$
\sum f_i m_i = 200.0 + 1818.0 + 2448.0 + 1133.0 + 520.0 = 6119.0
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum f_i m_i = \frac{6119.0}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{x} = 101.983 \ ^\circ\text{C} \approx 101.98 \ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute the deviations $(m_i - \bar{x})$ and the squared deviations

First, $\bar{x} = 101.983$ °C. Compute each deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 100.0 - 101.983 = -1.983 \\
d_2 &= 101.0 - 101.983 = -0.983 \\
d_3 &= 102.0 - 101.983 = +0.017 \\
d_4 &= 103.0 - 101.983 = +1.017 \\
d_5 &= 104.0 - 101.983 = +2.017
\end{aligned}
$$

Square each deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1^2 &= 3.9323 \\
d_2^2 &= 0.9663 \\
d_3^2 &= 0.0003 \\
d_4^2 &= 1.0343 \\
d_5^2 &= 4.0683
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Compute the products $f_i \cdot d_i^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 \cdot d_1^2 &= 2 \times 3.9323 = 7.8646 \\
f_2 \cdot d_2^2 &= 18 \times 0.9663 = 17.3934 \\
f_3 \cdot d_3^2 &= 24 \times 0.0003 = 0.0072 \\
f_4 \cdot d_4^2 &= 11 \times 1.0343 = 11.3773 \\
f_5 \cdot d_5^2 &= 5 \times 4.0683 = 20.3415
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Sum and compute the variance

$$
\sum f_i d_i^2 = 7.8646 + 17.3934 + 0.0072 + 11.3773 + 20.3415 = 56.984
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{56.984}{60 - 1} = \frac{56.984}{59}
$$

$$
\boxed{s^2 = 0.9658 \ (\ ^\circ\text{C})^2}
$$

#### Step 8: Compute the standard deviation

$$
s = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{0.9658}
$$

$$
\boxed{s = 0.9827 \ ^\circ\text{C} \approx 0.98 \ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

#### Step 9: Compute the coefficient of variation

$$
CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} = \frac{0.9827}{101.983}
$$

$$
\boxed{CV = 0.00964 = 0.964\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A CV of less than **1%** indicates an **excellent precision** instrument. The temperature indicator is very stable around its mean of ~102 °C.

---

### Part (c) — Median, Skewness, Shape Analysis

#### Step 10: Construct the cumulative frequency table

| Bin (°C) | $f_i$ | Cumulative $F_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 99.5 – 100.5 | 2 | 2 |
| 100.5 – 101.5 | 18 | 20 |
| 101.5 – 102.5 | 24 | 44 |
| 102.5 – 103.5 | 11 | 55 |
| 103.5 – 104.5 | 5 | 60 |

#### Step 11: Locate the median class

$N/2 = 60/2 = 30$. The cumulative frequency first exceeds 30 in the bin **101.5 – 102.5 °C**. So the **median class** is the third bin.

For the median class:
- $L_m = 101.5$ (lower boundary)
- $h = 1.0$ (bin width)
- $F_{m-1} = 20$ (cumulative frequency of previous bin)
- $f_m = 24$ (frequency of median class)

#### Step 12: Compute the median (grouped data formula)

$$
\text{Median} = L_m + h \cdot \frac{N/2 - F_{m-1}}{f_m}
$$

$$
\text{Median} = 101.5 + 1.0 \cdot \frac{30 - 20}{24}
$$

$$
\text{Median} = 101.5 + \frac{10}{24} = 101.5 + 0.4167
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Median} = 101.917 \ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

#### Step 13: Compute Pearson's second skewness coefficient

$$
Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s} = \frac{3 \times (101.983 - 101.917)}{0.9827}
$$

$$
Sk = \frac{3 \times 0.066}{0.9827} = \frac{0.198}{0.9827}
$$

$$
\boxed{Sk = +0.2015}
$$

#### Step 14: Shape Comment

Since $Sk = +0.20$ is **small and positive**:
- The distribution is **approximately symmetric** with a **very slight right (positive) skew**.
- The mean (101.98) is marginally higher than the median (101.92) — consistent with a mild right tail.
- For practical instrumentation purposes, the temperature readings can be **assumed approximately normal** with $\mu \approx 102$ °C and $\sigma \approx 0.98$ °C.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Histogram Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Table for KTU Numerical Questions:**

| Use Case | Histogram Y-axis Choice | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Counting occurrences** | Frequency $f_i$ | $f_i$ directly | Equal bin widths (most KTU problems) |
| **Comparing two datasets of different sizes** | Relative frequency $p_i$ | $f_i / N$ | When $N_1 \neq N_2$ |
| **Unequal bin widths** | Frequency density | $f_i / (N \cdot h)$ | Required for valid area-based probability |
| **Probability estimation** | Density (area = 1) | $f_i / (N \cdot h)$ | The empirical PDF |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Why a Histogram Estimates the PDF

Given a continuous random variable $X$ with true probability density function $f_X(x)$:

For a small bin of width $h$ centred at $x$:
$$P\left(x - \frac{h}{2} \le X \le x + \frac{h}{2}\right) = \int_{x - h/2}^{x + h/2} f_X(u) \, du \approx f_X(x) \cdot h$$

The empirical estimate of this probability is:
$$\hat{P} = \frac{f_i}{N}$$

Equating the two:
$$f_X(x) \cdot h \approx \frac{f_i}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_X(x) \approx \frac{f_i}{N \cdot h}$$

Therefore, **frequency density $= f_i / (N \cdot h)$** is the histogram-based estimate of the true PDF. This is the **theoretical foundation** for converting a histogram into a smooth density curve.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Histogram Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Raw Measurement Data x1 x2 x3 ... xN] --> nodeN[Determine Sample Size N]
    nodeN --> nodeRange[Compute Range R = xmax - xmin]
    nodeRange --> nodeK[Choose Number of Bins k using Sturges or sqrt rule]
    nodeK --> nodeH[Compute Bin Width h = R divided by k]
    nodeH --> nodeBins[Establish Bin Boundaries L0 L1 L2 ... Lk]
    nodeBins --> nodeTally[Tally Observations into Bins to get fi]
    nodeTally --> nodeVerify{Does sum of fi equal N?}
    nodeVerify -->|No| nodeError[Recount and correct tally errors]
    nodeError --> nodeTally
    nodeVerify -->|Yes| nodePlot[Plot Adjacent Rectangles with width h and height fi]
    nodePlot --> nodeShape[Analyze Shape Symmetric Skewed Bimodal Uniform]
    nodeShape --> nodeStats[Compute Mean Variance Std Dev Skewness CV]
    nodeStats --> nodeInterpret[Engineering Interpretation Stable Process Drift Outlier Bias]
    nodeInterpret --> nodeDecision[Decision Tune Sensor Recalibrate Accept Continue SPC]
```

## 4.2 Histogram Shape Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeHist[Observed Histogram] --> nodeSym{Symmetric about centre?}
    nodeSym -->|Yes| nodeBell{Bell shaped with thin tails?}
    nodeBell -->|Yes| nodeNorm[Normal Gaussian Distribution Process is stable]
    nodeBell -->|No Tall and sharp| nodeLepto[Leptokurtic Over controlled or rounded data]
    nodeBell -->|No Flat top| nodeUnif[Uniform Distribution Poor resolution sensor]
    nodeSym -->|No| nodeSkew{Skew direction?}
    nodeSkew -->|Tail right Mean greater than Median| nodeRSkew[Right Skewed Positive Skew Upper limit saturation]
    nodeSkew -->|Tail left Mean less than Median| nodeLSkew[Left Skewed Negative Skew Lower limit zero floor]
    nodeSym -->|No| nodeBimod{Two distinct peaks?}
    nodeBimod -->|Yes| nodeBi[Bimodal Two populations mixed Day versus Night Two grades of raw material]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Histogram's Position in the Statistical Toolkit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRaw[Raw Sensor Data] --> nodeHist[Histogram]
    nodeHist --> nodeMean[Mean and Median]
    nodeHist --> nodeVar[Variance and Std Deviation]
    nodeHist --> nodeSkew[Skewness and Kurtosis]
    nodeHist --> nodePDF[Empirical PDF Estimate]
    nodePDF --> nodeNormPlot[Normal Probability Plot]
    nodeNormPlot --> nodeFit[Confirm Gaussian Fit]
    nodeFit --> nodeCChart[Control Chart Xbar R Chart]
    nodeCChart --> nodeSPC[Statistical Process Control SPC]
    nodeSkew --> nodeCap[Cpk Capability Index]
    nodeVar --> nodeCap
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define a histogram. How is it different from a bar chart?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A histogram is a graphical representation of the frequency distribution of a continuous numerical variable. It consists of adjacent rectangles (sharing a common boundary) where the x-axis represents the class interval and the y-axis represents the frequency, relative frequency, or frequency density.
- **Difference (1 mark):** A **bar chart** has categorical (discrete) data on the x-axis and the bars are separated by gaps, whereas a **histogram** has continuous numerical data on the x-axis and the bars touch each other because the variable is continuous.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**What is meant by a bimodal histogram? What does it indicate in a measurement system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A bimodal histogram is a histogram that exhibits **two distinct peaks** (local maxima) in its frequency distribution, separated by a trough.
- **Engineering Indication (1.5 marks):** It indicates that the measurement data is drawn from **two different populations** or that the process operates in **two different states**. Examples include: day-shift vs night-shift production, two different grades of raw material being processed alternately, or a sensor that switches between two operating modes. In a calibration context, bimodality may indicate **hysteresis** or **two distinct measurement biases**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B Structure:**
> Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

The following frequency distribution was obtained for the diameter (in mm) of 80 ball-bearings produced by a CNC lathe:

| Class Interval (mm) | Frequency $f_i$ |
|---|---|
| 9.95 – 10.05 | 4 |
| 10.05 – 10.15 | 14 |
| 10.15 – 10.25 | 26 |
| 10.25 – 10.35 | 22 |
| 10.35 – 10.45 | 10 |
| 10.45 – 10.55 | 4 |

**(a)** Plot the histogram and comment on its shape. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and Pearson's skewness coefficient. State your inference about the process. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Histogram Plot and Shape Comment (7 marks):**

The histogram is drawn with 6 adjacent rectangles:
- Bin 1 (9.95–10.05): height 4
- Bin 2 (10.05–10.15): height 14
- Bin 3 (10.15–10.25): height 26 ← **Modal class**
- Bin 4 (10.25–10.35): height 22
- Bin 5 (10.35–10.45): height 10
- Bin 6 (10.45–10.55): height 4

**Shape Comment (3 marks):** The histogram is **unimodal** with a single peak at the 10.15–10.25 mm bin. The frequencies rise from 4 to 26 and then descend symmetrically to 4. The shape is **approximately bell-shaped (Gaussian)**, indicating a stable, well-controlled manufacturing process with measurements clustering around the central value.

**(b) Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute midpoints $m_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 &= 10.00, \quad m_2 = 10.10, \quad m_3 = 10.20 \\
m_4 &= 10.30, \quad m_5 = 10.40, \quad m_6 = 10.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $f_i \cdot m_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 \times 10.00 &= 40.0 \\
14 \times 10.10 &= 141.4 \\
26 \times 10.20 &= 265.2 \\
22 \times 10.30 &= 226.6 \\
10 \times 10.40 &= 104.0 \\
4 \times 10.50 &= 42.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum and compute mean:

$$
\sum f_i m_i = 40.0 + 141.4 + 265.2 + 226.6 + 104.0 + 42.0 = 819.2
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{819.2}{80} = 10.24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&4 \times (10.00 - 10.24)^2 = 4 \times 0.0576 = 0.2304 \\
&14 \times (10.10 - 10.24)^2 = 14 \times 0.0196 = 0.2744 \\
&26 \times (10.20 - 10.24)^2 = 26 \times 0.0016 = 0.0416 \\
&22 \times (10.30 - 10.24)^2 = 22 \times 0.0036 = 0.0792 \\
&10 \times (10.40 - 10.24)^2 = 10 \times 0.0256 = 0.2560 \\
&4 \times (10.50 - 10.24)^2 = 4 \times 0.0676 = 0.2704
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum and compute variance and standard deviation:

$$
\sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.2304 + 0.2744 + 0.0416 + 0.0792 + 0.2560 + 0.2704 = 1.152
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{1.152}{80 - 1} = \frac{1.152}{79} = 0.01458 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{0.01458} = 0.1208 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the median (grouped data):

$N/2 = 40$. Cumulative frequencies: 4, 18, 44, 66, 76, 80. Median class is the 3rd bin (10.15–10.25).

$$
\text{Median} = 10.15 + 0.10 \times \frac{40 - 18}{26} = 10.15 + 0.10 \times \frac{22}{26} = 10.15 + 0.0846 = 10.2346 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute Pearson's skewness:

$$
Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s} = \frac{3 \times (10.24 - 10.2346)}{0.1208} = \frac{3 \times 0.0054}{0.1208} = \frac{0.0162}{0.1208}
$$

$$
\boxed{Sk \approx +0.134}
$$

**Inference:** The mean (10.24 mm) is very close to the median (10.23 mm), and the skewness is small and positive (+0.13). The distribution is **approximately symmetric** and can be modelled as a **Normal distribution** with $\mu = 10.24$ mm and $\sigma = 0.12$ mm. The CNC lathe is producing ball-bearings with **tight dimensional control**, well within typical bearing tolerances of $\pm 0.05$ mm (which corresponds to $\pm 0.4\sigma$ — well within the $\pm 3\sigma$ specification range).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a histogram. State and explain the **Sturges' rule** for determining the number of classes. Mention the role of histogram in industrial quality control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure gauge is used to measure the steam pressure in a boiler 50 times. The data, grouped into 6 equal-width class intervals, is given below:

| Class Interval (bar) | 8.0–8.5 | 8.5–9.0 | 9.0–9.5 | 9.5–10.0 | 10.0–10.5 | 10.5–11.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency $f_i$ | 3 | 7 | 14 | 15 | 8 | 3 |

Calculate the **mean**, **standard deviation**, and **coefficient of variation**. Comment on the precision of the gauge. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Histogram Construction, Sturges' Rule, and Industrial Role (7 marks):**

**Histogram Sketch (2 marks):** A histogram is a series of adjacent rectangles plotted on the x-y plane. The x-axis is divided into contiguous class intervals (bins) of equal width $h$, and the y-axis represents the frequency $f_i$ of observations in each bin. Each rectangle has width $h$ and height $f_i$. Adjacent rectangles share a common vertical boundary (no gaps).

**Construction Steps (2 marks):**
1. Collect $N$ data points.
2. Find range $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$.
3. Determine number of bins $k$ (Sturges' rule: $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$).
4. Compute bin width $h = R/k$ (rounded up for convenience).
5. Tally observations into bins.
6. Draw rectangles with width $h$ and height $f_i$.

**Sturges' Rule (2 marks):** $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$. This is an empirical formula that balances the trade-off between too few bins (loss of detail) and too many bins (noisy, fragmented pattern). It assumes an approximately normal underlying distribution.

**Role in Industrial Quality Control (1 mark):** Histograms are used to:
- Monitor process **central tendency and variability**.
- Detect **drift, bias, and outliers** in measurement systems.
- Verify the assumption of **Normality** before applying SPC techniques.
- Compare the **performance of two sensors** or two production lines.
- Provide a **visual quality record** for ISO 9001 audits.

**(b) Mean, SD, and CV Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute midpoints $m_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 &= 8.25, \quad m_2 = 8.75, \quad m_3 = 9.25 \\
m_4 &= 9.75, \quad m_5 = 10.25, \quad m_6 = 10.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $f_i \cdot m_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 \times 8.25 &= 24.75 \\
7 \times 8.75 &= 61.25 \\
14 \times 9.25 &= 129.50 \\
15 \times 9.75 &= 146.25 \\
8 \times 10.25 &= 82.00 \\
3 \times 10.75 &= 32.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum and compute mean:

$$
\sum f_i m_i = 24.75 + 61.25 + 129.50 + 146.25 + 82.00 + 32.25 = 476.00
$$

$$
N = 3 + 7 + 14 + 15 + 8 + 3 = 50
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{476.00}{50} = 9.52 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&3 \times (8.25 - 9.52)^2 = 3 \times 1.6129 = 4.8387 \\
&7 \times (8.75 - 9.52)^2 = 7 \times 0.5929 = 4.1503 \\
&14 \times (9.25 - 9.52)^2 = 14 \times 0.0729 = 1.0206 \\
&15 \times (9.75 - 9.52)^2 = 15 \times 0.0529 = 0.7935 \\
&8 \times (10.25 - 9.52)^2 = 8 \times 0.5329 = 4.2632 \\
&3 \times (10.75 - 9.52)^2 = 3 \times 1.5129 = 4.5387
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum and compute variance and standard deviation:

$$
\sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2 = 4.8387 + 4.1503 + 1.0206 + 0.7935 + 4.2632 + 4.5387 = 19.605
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{19.605}{50 - 1} = \frac{19.605}{49} = 0.4001 \text{ bar}^2
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{0.4001} = 0.6325 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the coefficient of variation:

$$
CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} = \frac{0.6325}{9.52} = 0.0664 = 6.64\%
$$

**Comment on Precision (1 mark):** A CV of **6.64%** indicates a **moderate-precision** pressure gauge. For industrial steam pressure monitoring (typically $\pm 1$ bar tolerance around 9.5 bar), this gauge gives measurements within a 3$\sigma$ spread of about $\pm 1.9$ bar, which may be acceptable for trend monitoring but would be **insufficient for tight control** in high-pressure systems. A more precise transducer (CV < 2%) would be recommended for safety-critical boiler control.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $N - 1$ in variance:** Many students write $N$ instead of $N-1$ in the denominator. **This loses 1–2 marks every time.** Remember: Bessel's correction requires $N-1$ for an *unbiased* estimate of population variance from a sample.
>
> 2. **Using bin boundaries instead of midpoints:** The mean and variance formulas use the **midpoint $m_i$** of each class, NOT the lower or upper boundary. Writing $f_i \cdot L_i$ instead of $f_i \cdot m_i$ is a common error.
>
> 3. **Confusing frequency and frequency density:** When **bin widths are unequal**, you must use **frequency density = $f_i / h$** on the y-axis for the area to be meaningful. KTU may deliberately give unequal bin widths in higher-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Median class identification error:** The median class is the FIRST class whose cumulative frequency $\geq N/2$. Students often pick the last class or the modal class by mistake.
>
> 5. **Skewness sign convention:** A **positive** skewness means the **tail is on the RIGHT** (mean > median). A **negative** skewness means the **tail is on the LEFT** (mean < median). Mixing this up reverses the entire interpretation.
>
> 6. **Drawing the histogram with gaps:** A histogram **never has gaps** between bars. Bar charts have gaps; histograms do not. Drawing a bar chart and labelling it a histogram is a guaranteed mark-loss.
>
> 7. **Sturges' rule logarithm base:** KTU expects $\log_{10}$ (base 10). Using natural logarithm $\ln$ gives a different (incorrect) bin count.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Histogram (PERUT527, Module 1)**

- ✅ A **histogram** is a bar-type plot of a **continuous** variable's frequency distribution with **touching rectangles** (no gaps).
- ✅ **Sturges' rule:** $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$ — used to choose the number of bins.
- ✅ **Bin width:** $h = (x_{max} - x_{min}) / k$ — rounded up for convenient axis values.
- ✅ **Mean from grouped data:** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum f_i m_i$ (use **midpoint $m_i$**, not boundary).
- ✅ **Variance:** $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2$ — remember $N-1$, not $N$.
- ✅ **Standard deviation:** $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ — same unit as the measured variable.
- ✅ **Coefficient of variation:** $CV = s / \bar{x}$ — dimensionless, gauges **relative precision**.
- ✅ **Median (grouped):** $\text{Median} = L_m + h \cdot \dfrac{N/2 - F_{m-1}}{f_m}$ — linear interpolation inside the median class.
- ✅ **Pearson's skewness:** $Sk = \dfrac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$ — zero implies symmetry, positive = right tail, negative = left tail.
- ✅ **Relative frequency:** $p_i = f_i / N$ — total area of a relative-frequency histogram $= 1$ (empirical PDF).
- ✅ **Frequency density** $= f_i / (N \cdot h)$ — used when bin widths are unequal, gives empirical PDF.
- ✅ **Histogram vs Bar Chart:** histogram = continuous, touching bars; bar chart = categorical, separated bars.
- ✅ **Shape Diagnosis:** bell → normal/steady; right skew → upper saturation; left skew → zero floor; bimodal → two mixed populations; uniform → poor resolution; peaked → over-controlled.
- ✅ **Engineering Uses:** repeatability testing, sensor comparison, drift detection, normality check, SPC entry point.
- ✅ **Total area under a relative-frequency histogram = 1** — this is the link to probability theory.
- ✅ **Sample size guideline:** $N \geq 30$ for a meaningful histogram; $N \geq 50$ for normality inference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Measurements and Industrial Instrumentation**, the **Mean** is a fundamental statistical parameter that represents the central tendency of a finite set of repeated measurement readings of a physical quantity. When a single physical quantity (such as voltage, pressure, temperature, or displacement) is measured repeatedly under nominally identical conditions, the individual readings will inevitably exhibit small random variations (known as *random errors* or *fluctuations*). The arithmetic mean of these readings provides the **best estimate of the true value** of the measurand according to the **Central Limit Theorem** and the **principle of least squares**.

Mathematically, for a dataset containing $N$ repeated observations $x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_N$ of the same measurand, the **Arithmetic Mean** $\bar{x}$ (also called the **Sample Mean** or **Average Value**) is formally defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus as:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 (Introduction to Measurement Systems & Statistical Treatment of Measurements), the Mean is the **first statistical descriptor** a student encounters. It forms the foundation for calculating *standard deviation*, *variance*, *standard error of the mean*, and forms the basis for determining **random uncertainties** in any measurement system as per **ISO/IEC Guide 98-3 (GUM)** standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to guess the weight of an apple at a grocery stall using a slightly faulty weighing scale. If you place the apple on the scale once, you might get **152.3 g**. But because the scale has tiny mechanical imperfections and because the apple's exact placement varies each time, if you weigh it 10 times, you might get readings like 152.3, 151.8, 152.5, 151.9, 152.1, 152.4, 152.0, 152.2, 151.7, and 152.6 grams.

None of these individual readings is exactly the "true" weight, but if you **add them all up and divide by 10**, you will arrive at a value (say, 152.15 g) that is closer to the actual weight of the apple than any single reading. That resulting value is the **Mean**. The mean is essentially the **"balancing point"** or **"center of gravity"** of all the scattered readings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** The mean is like asking *"What is the most representative single number that summarizes an entire list of measurements?"* It is the **fairest compromise** between the higher and lower readings, with each reading pulling with equal influence.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Standard Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Level of Significance (Confidence Level) | $\alpha$ | Typically **0.05** (95% confidence) |
| Coverage Factor | $k$ | Typically **2** for ~95% probability |
| Number of Measurements | $N$ | **Dimensionless**, integer $\geq 2$ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $\nu$ | $N - 1$ (dimensionless) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Whenever you calculate a mean for a measurement problem in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), always pair it with the **standard deviation** and **standard error**. Examiners at KTU frequently award part marks for showing the statistical *completeness* of your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why the Mean is the "Best Estimate"

The arithmetic mean occupies a privileged position in measurement science for **three rigorous mathematical reasons** which examiners love to test:

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Principle of Least Squares:** The mean $\bar{x}$ is the unique value that **minimizes the sum of squared deviations** from all data points. That is, $\bar{x}$ is the value which makes $\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ as small as possible. This makes it the most statistically efficient estimator.

2. **Unbiased Estimator:** The **expected value** of the sample mean $E[\bar{x}]$ equals the true value $\mu$ of the measurand. Formally, $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$, making $\bar{x}$ an **unbiased estimator** of the true value.

3. **Minimum Variance Property (Central Limit Theorem):** As the number of measurements $N$ increases, the variance of the mean decreases as $\sigma^2 / N$. The mean is therefore the estimator with the **smallest possible variance** among all linear unbiased estimators (Gauss-Markov Theorem).

### Why Does the Mean Matter in Industrial Instrumentation?

In real-world industrial applications (e.g., calibrating a pressure transmitter, characterizing a thermocouple, validating a flow meter's accuracy), engineers are **not satisfied with a single measurement**. Regulatory standards such as **ISO 9001**, **ISO/IEC 17025**, and **ANSI/NCSL Z540.3** mandate that instruments be evaluated using **repeated measurements** under repeatability conditions. The mean provides:

- A **calibration reference point** for setting instrument zero and span.
- A **reference datum** against which systematic errors (bias) are computed.
- A **stable target** for Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts.

## 2.2 Weighted Mean — When Measurements Differ in Quality

Not all measurements are equally trustworthy. A measurement made with a high-precision instrument should carry more weight than one made with a low-precision instrument. The **Weighted Mean** accounts for this:

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}$$

where $w_i$ is the weight assigned to the $i$-th measurement (often $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$, the inverse of its variance).

## 2.3 True Mean vs. Sample Mean

| Concept | Symbol | Definition | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| True (Population) Mean | $\mu$ | Mean of the entire infinite population of measurements | Theoretical / GUM uncertainty propagation |
| Sample Mean | $\bar{x}$ | Mean of a finite set of $N$ readings | Actual laboratory and field measurements |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Arithmetic Mean of $N$ readings | Same as $x_i$ | Requires $N \geq 2$ |
| 2 | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weighted Mean | Same as $x_i$ | Weights $w_i > 0$ |
| 3 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Sample Standard Deviation | Same as $x_i$ | Bessel's correction $N-1$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Standard Error of the Mean | Same as $x_i$ | Valid for $N$ independent readings |
| 5 | $U = k \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$ | Expanded Uncertainty | Same as $x_i$ | $k$ is coverage factor |
| 6 | $\nu = N - 1$ | Degrees of Freedom | Dimensionless | For t-distribution |
| 7 | $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$ | Expected Value of Mean | Same as $x_i$ | $\bar{x}$ is unbiased estimator |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{N}$ | Variance of the Mean | (unit)$^2$ | Independent samples only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Notation Note:** Per the KTU Premier Engine format protocol, vertical bars in formulas (e.g., absolute value notation) must be rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ rather than the pipe character $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

The mean is the **backbone of metrology**. In a calibration laboratory at NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories, India), when a digital multimeter is being calibrated against a **fluke 5522A calibrator**, multiple readings are recorded and the mean becomes the official reference value stamped on the calibration certificate. Similarly, in **ISO 17025** accredited testing, the **mean of triplicate measurements** is mandatory for chemical analysis, hardness testing, and dimensional metrology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean as the Least-Squares Estimator

We want to prove that the mean $\bar{x}$ minimizes the sum of squared deviations $S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the objective function (sum of squared deviations from an arbitrary candidate value $\mu$):

$$S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squared terms using the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i^2 - 2\mu x_i + \mu^2)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the linearity of summation $\sum(a + b + c) = \sum a + \sum b + \sum c$:

$$S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 \;-\; 2\mu \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;+\; N\mu^2$$

**Step 4:** To minimize $S(\mu)$, take the first derivative with respect to $\mu$ and set it to zero (necessary condition for a stationary point):

$$\frac{dS}{d\mu} = 0 \;-\; 2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;+\; 2N\mu \;=\; 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve the resulting linear equation for $\mu$:

$$2N\mu = 2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

$$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \;=\; \bar{x}$$

**Step 6:** Verify it is a *minimum* using the second derivative test. The second derivative is:

$$\frac{d^2 S}{d\mu^2} = 2N \;>\; 0$$

Since $2N > 0$ for any valid sample size, $S(\mu)$ is strictly convex and the stationary point is a **global minimum**.

$$\therefore \; \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad \text{is the unique least-squares estimator}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of the Mean (Standard Error of the Mean)

We want to derive $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \sigma^2 / N$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of variance of a random variable:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = E\!\left[(\bar{x} - E[\bar{x}])^2\right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ into the expression:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = E\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - \mu\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 3:** Use the linearity of expectation to write $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$ (since each $E[x_i] = \mu$):

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = E\!\left[\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Pull the constant $\dfrac{1}{N^2}$ outside the square:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\;E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 5:** Expand the square of the summation $\left(\sum a_i\right)^2 = \sum a_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} a_i a_j$:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} E\!\left[(x_i - \mu)^2\right] + 2\sum_{i<j} E\!\left[(x_i - \mu)(x_j - \mu)\right]\right]$$

**Step 6:** Apply the **independence assumption**: if $x_i$ and $x_j$ are independent for $i \neq j$, then $E[(x_i-\mu)(x_j-\mu)] = 0$, eliminating all cross-product terms:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E\!\left[(x_i - \mu)^2\right]$$

**Step 7:** Recognize $E[(x_i-\mu)^2] = \sigma^2$ for every $i$ (all measurements share the same population variance). The summation becomes $N\sigma^2$:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

**Step 8:** Take the square root to obtain the **Standard Error of the Mean**:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{x})} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad \sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** This formula tells us that to **reduce the uncertainty of the mean by a factor of 2**, we must take **four times as many measurements** ($N$ quadruples). Uncertainty reduction is expensive — a fundamental trade-off in laboratory metrology.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A digital pressure indicator is used to measure a steady pressure of **10.000 bar** (reference standard). Ten repeated readings (in bar) are: 9.98, 10.02, 9.99, 10.01, 9.97, 10.00, 10.03, 9.99, 10.01, 10.00. Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and standard error of the mean.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Sum the readings:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 9.98 + 10.02 + 9.99 + 10.01 + 9.97 + 10.00 + 10.03 + 9.99 + 10.01 + 10.00 = 100.00$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{100.00}{10} = 10.000 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each deviation $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 9.98 | $-0.020$ | $0.000400$ |
| 2 | 10.02 | $+0.020$ | $0.000400$ |
| 3 | 9.99 | $-0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 4 | 10.01 | $+0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 5 | 9.97 | $-0.030$ | $0.000900$ |
| 6 | 10.00 | $0.000$ | $0.000000$ |
| 7 | 10.03 | $+0.030$ | $0.000900$ |
| 8 | 9.99 | $-0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 9 | 10.01 | $+0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 10 | 10.00 | $0.000$ | $0.000000$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0.000** | **$\sum d_i^2 = 0.003000$** |

**Step 4:** Compute the standard deviation using Bessel's correction:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum d_i^2} = \sqrt{\frac{0.003000}{9}} = \sqrt{0.000333} \approx 0.01826 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the standard error of the mean:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{0.01826}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{0.01826}{3.1623} \approx 0.005774 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 6:** Final reported result with expanded uncertainty ($k = 2$ for 95% confidence):

$$\bar{x} \pm U = 10.000 \pm (2 \times 0.005774) = 10.000 \pm 0.012 \text{ bar}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mean Formulas at a Glance

| Scenario | Best Formula | When to Use | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equal-trust measurements | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ | All instruments have identical precision | Don't drop small readings |
| Unequal-trust measurements | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Measurements come from sensors with different accuracies | Weights must be $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$ |
| Want uncertainty of the mean | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sigma / \sqrt{N}$ | For uncertainty reporting in calibration certificates | Sample variance uses $N-1$, not $N$ |
| Detect outlier before averaging | Use Grubbs' or Dixon's Q test | One reading seems "weird" | Don't remove without statistical justification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — The Mean in the Statistical Treatment of Measurements

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture flow** showing how the Mean serves as the gateway to all subsequent statistical descriptors in measurement data analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Measurement Readings x1 to xN"] --> B["Arithmetic Mean Calculation x-bar"]
    B --> C["Sample Variance sigma-squared"]
    B --> D["Standard Deviation sigma"]
    B --> E["Standard Error of Mean sigma-x-bar"]
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F["Expanded Uncertainty U equals k times sigma"]
    E --> F
    B --> G["Bias Error Estimation x-bar minus True Value"]
    D --> H["Repeatability Assessment"]
    F --> I["Calibration Certificate Reporting"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["ISO IEC 17025 Compliant Output"]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing the Right Mean

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Have a Set of N Repeated Readings"] --> B{"Are All Measurements Equally Reliable?"}
    B -->|"Yes"| C["Use Arithmetic Mean x-bar equals sum xi divided by N"]
    B -->|"No, instruments differ in precision"| D["Use Weighted Mean x-bar-w equals sum w-i xi divided by sum w-i"]
    C --> E{"Are There Outliers in the Dataset?"}
    D --> E
    E -->|"Yes"| F["Apply Grubbs Test or Dixon Q Test First"]
    E -->|"No"| G["Compute Standard Deviation sigma"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Compute Standard Error sigma-x-bar equals sigma divided by root N"]
    H --> I["Report Result with Expanded Uncertainty U"]
    I --> J["End: ISO 17025 Compliant Measurement Report"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Statistical Estimators

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Population True Mean mu"] --> S1["Sample Mean x-bar"]
    P2["Population Variance sigma-squared"] --> S2["Sample Variance s-squared"]
    S1 --> O1["Reported Measurement Value"]
    S2 --> S3["Standard Deviation sigma"]
    S3 --> O2["Uncertainty Value"]
    S3 --> SE1["Standard Error sigma-x-bar"]
    SE1 --> O2
    O1 --> OUT["Final Measurement Statement x-bar plus or minus U"]
    O2 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Weighted Mean Coefficient Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Reading x1 from Sensor A precision sigma-1"] --> W1["Weight w1 equals 1 divided by sigma-1 squared"]
    A2["Reading x2 from Sensor B precision sigma-2"] --> W2["Weight w2 equals 1 divided by sigma-2 squared"]
    A3["Reading x3 from Sensor C precision sigma-3"] --> W3["Weight w3 equals 1 divided by sigma-3 squared"]
    W1 --> SUM["Weighted Sum: sum w-i times x-i"]
    W2 --> SUM
    W3 --> SUM
    W1 --> WSUM["Weight Total: sum w-i"]
    W2 --> WSUM
    W3 --> WSUM
    SUM --> DIV["Division Operation"]
    WSUM --> DIV
    DIV --> RESULT["Weighted Mean x-bar-w"]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define the *arithmetic mean* of a set of repeated measurements. State any **two** properties that make it the best estimator of the true value.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The arithmetic mean $\bar{x}$ of a set of $N$ repeated readings $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ of a physical quantity is defined as their sum divided by the number of readings:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Property 1 (1 Mark):** $\bar{x}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of the true value $\mu$, i.e., $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$, meaning that on average it gives the correct value.

**Property 2 (1 Mark):** $\bar{x}$ has **minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators (Gauss-Markov theorem). Its variance equals $\sigma^2/N$, which decreases as the sample size increases, making it the most statistically efficient estimator.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** What is the *standard error of the mean*? How does it differ from the *standard deviation*?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The standard error of the mean (SEM) quantifies how precisely the sample mean $\bar{x}$ estimates the true value $\mu$. It is given by:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Difference 1 (1 Mark):** The standard deviation $\sigma$ measures the **dispersion of individual readings** around the mean, while the SEM measures the **dispersion of the sample mean itself** as an estimator of the true value.

**Difference 2 (1 Mark):** The standard deviation is independent of sample size (it estimates a population property), whereas the SEM **decreases as $\sqrt{N}$ increases**, reflecting improved precision of the mean estimate with more measurements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Derive the expression for the arithmetic mean as the **least-squares estimator** of a set of $N$ measurements $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the sum of squared deviations from a candidate value $\mu$ as:

$$S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Expand and apply the linearity of summation:

$$S(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 - 2\mu\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + N\mu^2$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Differentiate $S(\mu)$ with respect to $\mu$:

$$\frac{dS}{d\mu} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + 2N\mu$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Set the derivative to zero (stationary point condition):

$$-2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + 2N\mu = 0 \implies \mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Verify minimum using second derivative test:

$$\frac{d^2 S}{d\mu^2} = 2N > 0 \implies \text{convex function, point is a minimum}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Conclude with the boxed result:

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** State the engineering significance: the mean minimizes total squared error, justifying its use as the best estimate.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A voltmeter is used to measure a standard voltage of **5.000 V**. Five independent readings are: 4.98, 5.01, 4.99, 5.02, and 5.00 V. Compute the mean, sample standard deviation, and standard error of the mean. Comment on the bias.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute the sum and the mean:

$$\sum x_i = 4.98 + 5.01 + 4.99 + 5.02 + 5.00 = 25.00 \text{ V}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{25.00}{5} = 5.000 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute deviations and their squares:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | $d_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.98 | $-0.020$ | $0.000400$ |
| 2 | 5.01 | $+0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 3 | 4.99 | $-0.010$ | $0.000100$ |
| 4 | 5.02 | $+0.020$ | $0.000400$ |
| 5 | 5.00 | $0.000$ | $0.000000$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$0.000$** | **$0.001000$** |

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply Bessel's corrected standard deviation formula with $N = 5$, so $N - 1 = 4$:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum d_i^2} = \sqrt{\frac{0.001000}{4}} = \sqrt{0.00025} = 0.01581 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute the standard error of the mean:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{0.01581}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.01581}{2.2361} = 0.00707 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the expanded uncertainty with coverage factor $k = 2$:

$$U = k \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 2 \times 0.00707 = 0.01414 \text{ V} \approx 0.014 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Compute the bias (systematic error):

$$\text{Bias} = \bar{x} - V_{\text{true}} = 5.000 - 5.000 = 0.000 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Final reported result and comment:

$$\boxed{\text{Voltage} = (5.000 \pm 0.014) \text{ V at 95\% confidence, Bias} \approx 0.000 \text{ V}}$$

**Comment:** The mean equals the true value, indicating negligible bias; only random uncertainty is present, characterized by $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = 0.00707$ V.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Alternative Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the concept of *weighted mean*. When is it preferred over the simple arithmetic mean? A temperature is measured by three sensors with the following data:

- Sensor 1: Reading $T_1 = 100.2$ °C, standard deviation $\sigma_1 = 0.5$ °C
- Sensor 2: Reading $T_2 = 100.5$ °C, standard deviation $\sigma_2 = 0.2$ °C
- Sensor 3: Reading $T_3 = 100.4$ °C, standard deviation $\sigma_3 = 0.1$ °C

Compute the weighted mean.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the weighted mean formula:

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}, \quad \text{where } w_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the weights for each sensor:

$$w_1 = \frac{1}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ (°C)}^{-2}$$

$$w_2 = \frac{1}{(0.2)^2} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25 \text{ (°C)}^{-2}$$

$$w_3 = \frac{1}{(0.1)^2} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100 \text{ (°C)}^{-2}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the weighted sum $\sum w_i x_i$:

$$\sum w_i x_i = (4)(100.2) + (25)(100.5) + (100)(100.4)$$

$$= 400.8 + 2512.5 + 10040.0 = 12953.3$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute the total weight $\sum w_i$:

$$\sum w_i = 4 + 25 + 100 = 129$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the weighted mean:

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{12953.3}{129} = 100.41 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Identify when weighted mean is preferred (answer to "when" question):

The weighted mean is preferred when:
- Measurements come from instruments with **different precisions**.
- Some observations are **known to be more reliable** than others based on prior calibration data.
- Combining measurements across different **environmental conditions or methods**.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Engineering commentary:

$$\boxed{\bar{T}_w \approx 100.41 \text{ °C}}$$

Note that the weighted mean (100.41 °C) lies closer to Sensor 3's reading (100.4 °C) because Sensor 3 has the smallest standard deviation ($\sigma_3 = 0.1$ °C), and thus receives the largest weight. The simple arithmetic mean would give $(100.2 + 100.5 + 100.4)/3 = 100.367$ °C, which is biased toward the noisier readings.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Discuss the **standard error of the mean** in detail. Show how it relates to the number of measurements and explain its significance in **industrial calibration procedures**.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the standard error of the mean:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

where $\sigma$ is the sample standard deviation and $N$ is the number of measurements.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Derive the relationship (or state the derivation logic): the variance of $\bar{x}$ equals $\sigma^2 / N$ because of the independence of measurements and the linearity of expectation, as shown in Derivation 2 of Section 3.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Discuss the $1/\sqrt{N}$ dependence: doubling $N$ reduces the SEM by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, not by 2.

| $N$ | $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$ relative to $\sigma$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $1.000 \, \sigma$ |
| 4 | $0.500 \, \sigma$ |
| 9 | $0.333 \, \sigma$ |
| 16 | $0.250 \, \sigma$ |
| 25 | $0.200 \, \sigma$ |
| 100 | $0.100 \, \sigma$ |

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Explain significance in industrial calibration:

In **ISO/IEC 17025** accredited calibration laboratories, the standard error of the mean directly determines the **minimum reported uncertainty**. A calibration certificate for a pressure gauge will state the measurement as $P = \bar{x} \pm U$, where $U = k \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Discuss practical implications:

- For **tight tolerance** measurements (e.g., aerospace component certification), $N$ must be large (often $N = 10$ to $30$) to drive SEM below tolerance limits.
- For **rough industrial** measurements, $N = 5$ is often deemed sufficient.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Discuss the **diminishing returns**: increasing $N$ from 1 to 10 reduces SEM by ~68%, but from 100 to 109 (only 9 more readings) reduces SEM by only ~4%. Beyond $N = 25$, additional measurements are often economically unjustifiable.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclude with the engineering recommendation:

$$\boxed{\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Balancing cost and uncertainty yields optimal } N \in [5, 30]}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insights — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction ($-1$ in denominator):** Many students compute $\sigma$ using $N$ instead of $N-1$ in the denominator. This gives a **biased (under-estimated) standard deviation**. KTU examiners specifically test this: a student using $N$ instead of $N-1$ will lose **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing standard deviation with standard error of the mean:** These are *two different quantities*. Standard deviation describes **individual readings**; SEM describes **the mean as an estimator**. Mixing them up in a calibration certificate is a serious metrological error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Every mean, standard deviation, and uncertainty must carry the **same unit as the original measurand**. A mean voltage reported as "5.000" without "V" loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 4. **Reporting without uncertainty:** A bare mean like $\bar{x} = 5.000$ V is **incomplete**. KTU valuation keys explicitly require $\bar{x} \pm U$ at a stated confidence level.
>
> 5. **Wrong application of weighted mean weights:** Students often use $w_i = \sigma_i$ instead of $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$. Remember: **higher precision (smaller $\sigma$) → larger weight → weight is inverse-variance.**
>
> 6. **Skipping the derivation steps in 14-mark questions:** KTU 14-mark Part (a) sub-questions on derivations require **every intermediate step** shown. Skipping the expansion or differentiation will cost 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mean (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ — the arithmetic mean of $N$ readings.
- **Best-Estimate Property:** Unbiased ($E[\bar{x}] = \mu$) and minimum-variance (Gauss-Markov).
- **Least-Squares Foundation:** Minimizes $\sum(x_i - \mu)^2$; proven by first derivative test ($dS/d\mu = 0$ gives $\mu = \bar{x}$).
- **Bessel's Correction:** Always use $N-1$ in denominator when computing sample standard deviation $\sigma$.
- **Standard Error of the Mean:** $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sigma / \sqrt{N}$ — quantifies the precision of the mean itself.
- **Expanded Uncertainty:** $U = k \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$ — with $k = 2$ for ~95% confidence.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** $\nu = N - 1$ — used in t-distribution for small samples ($N < 30$).
- **Weighted Mean:** $\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i / \sum w_i$ with $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$ — used when measurements have unequal precision.
- **Bias Calculation:** $\text{Bias} = \bar{x} - x_{\text{true}}$ — quantifies systematic error of the instrument.
- **Practical Range of $N$:** In industrial metrology, $N$ typically lies between **5 and 30**, balancing precision and cost.
- **Reporting Standard:** Always report measurement as $\bar{x} \pm U$ with units, confidence level, and coverage factor $k$.
- **Central Limit Theorem:** As $N \to \infty$, $\bar{x} \to \mu$, and the distribution of $\bar{x}$ approaches Normal distribution regardless of the parent distribution of $x_i$.
- **KTU Boards Love:** Derivations, "calculate mean & standard deviation" numerical problems, and ISO 17025 reporting format questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measure of Dispersion from the Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measure of Dispersion from the Mean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Dispersion" in the Context of Measurements?

In engineering metrology, industrial instrumentation, and quality control, a single number — the **arithmetic mean ($\bar{x}$)** — is **never enough** to fully describe a set of repeated measurements. Two batches of resistors may have the *same* average resistance of **100 Ω**, yet one batch may be tightly clustered around 100 Ω (highly reliable) while the other may swing wildly between 80 Ω and 120 Ω (unreliable). The numerical property that quantifies this *spread*, *scatter*, or *variability* of data around the central value is called the **Measure of Dispersion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Dispersion is the extent to which the values of a statistical distribution (or a set of measurements) are stretched or squeezed around a central tendency measure (typically the mean). It is a quantitative expression of the "scattering" of data points relative to a reference value.*

### 1.2 Why Is Dispersion Critical in Instrumentation?

In industrial instrumentation, dispersion is **not** merely a statistical curiosity — it is the **physical fingerprint of measurement uncertainty**.

| Engineering Scenario | Mean (Alone) | Dispersion Tells Us |
|---|---|---|
| Calibration of a pressure transducer | "Average error = 0.1%" | Are errors random (low σ) or systematic (high σ)? |
| Batch quality of manufactured bearings | "Mean diameter = 25.00 mm" | How many bearings fall outside the tolerance band? |
| Repeatability test of a digital multimeter | "Mean reading = 10.02 V" | Does the instrument drift, or is it repeatable? |
| Six-Sigma process control | "Mean cycle time = 45 s" | What is the defect rate (DPMO) under this spread? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Two instruments with identical mean readings can have **vastly different** reliability profiles. Dispersion is what distinguishes a *precise* instrument from an *imprecise* one.

## 1.3 The Four Classical Measures of Dispersion (from the Mean)

KTU 2024 Scheme focuses on **dispersion measured from the mean** as the reference point. The four principal measures are:

1. **Range ($R$)** — the crudest measure; the difference between the largest and smallest observation.
2. **Mean Deviation / Mean Absolute Deviation (M.A.D.)** — the average of absolute deviations from the mean.
3. **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$ or $s$)** — the *root mean square* deviation from the mean; the **most important** measure in engineering.
4. **Variance ($\sigma^2$ or $s^2$)** — the square of the standard deviation; equals the mean of squared deviations.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Archery Target" Intuition 🎯

Imagine **three archers** each shoot 6 arrows at a bullseye. The bullseye represents the **mean (true value)**.

- **Archer A (low dispersion, high precision):** Arrows are tightly clustered *near* the center → low spread → **reliable instrument**.
- **Archer B (high dispersion, low precision):** Arrows are scattered all over the target → high spread → **noisy instrument**.
- **Archer C (biased, moderate dispersion):** Arrows form a tight cluster *off-center* → low spread but **systematic error (accuracy ≠ precision)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision = Inverse of Dispersion.** Low dispersion ⇒ high precision. The standard deviation $\sigma$ is the numerical ruler we use to express precision in engineering units (e.g., $\pm 0.02$ mA).

## 1.5 Population vs. Sample — A Crucial Distinction

| Symbol | Refers To | Division Factor | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma^2$ | **Population** variance | $N$ (full data size) | Entire process is measured (rare in practice) |
| $s^2$ | **Sample** variance | $n - 1$ (Bessel's correction) | We have a *finite sample* from a larger process |
| $\sigma$ | Population standard deviation | — | — |
| $s$ | Sample standard deviation | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** In engineering laboratory data, the divisor is almost always **$n-1$** because we deal with *samples*, not entire populations. The $\pm 1$ degree of freedom accounts for the fact that the sample mean $\bar{x}$ is itself estimated from the same data.

## 1.6 Physical Units of Each Measure

| Measure | Symbol | SI Unit Example (length) |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $R$ | metres (m) |
| Mean Deviation | M.D. | metres (m) |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ or $s$ | metres (m) |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ or $s^2$ | metres² (m²) — *always squared units* |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Variance has **squared** units (m², V², °C²), which is *not* physically interpretable. Hence **standard deviation** (with the same units as the original data) is overwhelmingly preferred in instrumentation work.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Philosophy Behind "Measuring from the Mean"

Given a dataset $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with arithmetic mean $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, every individual deviation from the mean is:

$$
d_i = x_i - \bar{x}
$$

A naïve first attempt at "average deviation" would be $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum d_i$. But by a fundamental property of the arithmetic mean, **the algebraic sum of deviations from the mean is always zero**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x}) = 0
$$

This is because the mean is the *balancing point* of the data. Therefore, to get a *meaningful* measure of dispersion, we must perform one of three operations to eliminate the sign cancellations:

1. Take the **absolute value** of each deviation (→ Mean Deviation).
2. **Square** each deviation (→ Variance / Standard Deviation).
3. Use **rank-based** differences (→ Quartile Deviation, treated separately).

## 2.2 The Four Measures — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Range ($R$)

The simplest and crudest. It uses **only two** data points — the maximum and minimum — and discards all intermediate information.

- **Use case:** Quick sanity check, sample-size-1 inspection, control chart quick limits.
- **Drawback:** Ignores the distribution *between* extremes. Two datasets with identical $R$ can have radically different spreads.

### 2.2.2 Mean Deviation (M.D.) — Average Absolute Deviation

Take the **absolute value** of each deviation, then average.

- **Why absolute value?** Because squaring gives more weight to outliers; absolute values treat all deviations equally.
- **Use case:** When robustness against extreme outliers is desired (e.g., environmental noise data with occasional spikes).

### 2.2.3 Variance and Standard Deviation

Square each deviation, average them, then (for standard deviation) take the square root.

- **Why square?** Because squaring (a) eliminates sign cancellation, (b) penalizes large deviations more heavily (good for detecting rare but catastrophic measurement errors), and (c) yields an analytically tractable function that connects to the **Normal (Gaussian) distribution**.
- **Use case:** Universal — error analysis, propagation of uncertainty, process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$), Six Sigma, statistical process control (SPC).

### 2.2.4 Quartile Deviation / Semi-Interquartile Range (Q.D. = Q)

Based on the **interquartile range** $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$:

$$
Q.D. = \frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}
$$

- **Use case:** When data has extreme outliers or is heavily skewed (e.g., measurement of natural phenomena, particle sizes).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the formulas KTU examiners test in ESE and Module Tests.**

| # | Measure | Population Formula | Sample Formula | Units | Robust to Outliers? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Range** | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Same as data | ❌ No |
| 2 | **Mean Deviation** | $M.D. = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$ | $M.D. = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$ | Same as data | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \mu)^2$ | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Squared units | ❌ No |
| 4 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Same as data | ❌ No |
| 5 | **Quartile Deviation** | $Q.D. = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$ | $Q.D. = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$ | Same as data | ✅ Yes |

### 2.4 The Two Forms of the Variance Formula (KTU Favourite!)

For computational efficiency (avoiding round-off errors when $\bar{x}$ is not a "nice" number), use the **alternative form**:

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum x_i^2 - \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When the data values are large (e.g., 487, 492, 488…), the direct formula $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ leads to catastrophic cancellation. The *shortcut* form using $\sum x_i^2$ is numerically far more stable. KTU problems **frequently** ask students to use this form.

### 2.5 Coefficient of Variation (C.V.) — A Dimensionless Dispersion Index

Since standard deviation has the same units as the mean, we can form a **dimensionless ratio** that allows comparison of variability across different processes or units:

$$
C.V. = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use:** A C.V. of 2% is excellent for a precision resistor; a C.V. of 15% may be acceptable for a low-cost thermistor. C.V. is the *standardized yardstick* used in **Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA)** per AIAG/ISO standards.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calibration Certificates:** ISO/IEC 17025 reports expanded uncertainty as $U = k \cdot s$ where $k$ is the coverage factor (typically $k = 2$ for 95% confidence).
- **Process Capability:** $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$.
- **Measurement Repeatability:** Standard deviation of repeated measurements under identical conditions.
- **Sensor Noise Characterization:** Allan deviation of oscillator data; RMS noise of ADC readings.
- **Tolerance Stack-Up:** RSS (Root-Sum-Squares) method: $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\sum d_i = 0$ (The Foundation of All "Mean" Dispersion Measures)

We start with the definition of the arithmetic mean:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Multiplying both sides by $n$:

$$
n\bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Now compute the sum of deviations from the mean:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bar{x}
$$

Since $\bar{x}$ is a *constant*, $\sum \bar{x} = n\bar{x}$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\bar{x}
$$

Substituting $n\bar{x} = \sum x_i$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therefore, the sum of deviations from the arithmetic mean is identically zero.** This is why we *must* apply absolute value, square, or another transformation to obtain a non-zero measure of dispersion. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: From the Direct Variance Formula to the Shortcut Form

**Start** with the direct formula for population variance:

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Expand the squared term:

$$
(x_i - \mu)^2 = x_i^2 - 2\mu x_i + \mu^2
$$

**Step 2:** Sum over all $i$:

$$
\sum(x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - 2\mu\sum x_i + N\mu^2
$$

**Step 3:** Note that $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i$, hence $\sum x_i = N\mu$:

$$
\sum(x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - 2\mu(N\mu) + N\mu^2
$$

$$
= \sum x_i^2 - 2N\mu^2 + N\mu^2 = \sum x_i^2 - N\mu^2
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $N$:

$$
= \sum x_i^2 - N\left(\frac{\sum x_i}{N}\right)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{N}
$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $N$ to recover the variance:

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\left[\sum x_i^2 - \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{N}\right]
$$

Replacing $N$ with $n-1$ and $\mu$ with $\bar{x}$ for the *sample* variance:

$$
\boxed{\, s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}{n}\right] \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This algebraic identity is **exact** (not an approximation). It is computationally superior and is a KTU-favourite for "calculate variance from raw data" problems.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — Standard KTU 14-Mark Style

**Problem:** The following data represents the output voltage (in volts) of a digital voltmeter across 8 repeated measurements of a 5.000 V reference standard:

$$
5.01,\ 5.03,\ 4.98,\ 5.00,\ 5.02,\ 4.99,\ 5.01,\ 5.04
$$

**Required:**
(a) Compute the arithmetic mean and standard deviation (sample).
(b) Compute the mean deviation from the mean.
(c) Compute the range and coefficient of variation. Comment on the precision of the instrument.

---

### Solution — Part (a): Mean and Standard Deviation

**Step 1:** Compute $\sum x_i$:

$$
\sum x_i = 5.01 + 5.03 + 4.98 + 5.00 + 5.02 + 4.99 + 5.01 + 5.04
$$

$$
= 5.01 + 5.03 = 10.04;\ 10.04 + 4.98 = 15.02;\ 15.02 + 5.00 = 20.02
$$

$$
20.02 + 5.02 = 25.04;\ 25.04 + 4.99 = 30.03;\ 30.03 + 5.01 = 35.04;\ 35.04 + 5.04 = 40.08
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{40.08}{8} = 5.010 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\sum x_i^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i^2 &= (5.01)^2 + (5.03)^2 + (4.98)^2 + (5.00)^2 + (5.02)^2 + (4.99)^2 + (5.01)^2 + (5.04)^2 \\
&= 25.1001 + 25.3009 + 24.8004 + 25.0000 + 25.2004 + 24.9001 + 25.1001 + 25.4016 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding term-by-term:

$$
25.1001 + 25.3009 = 50.4010;\ 50.4010 + 24.8004 = 75.2014
$$

$$
75.2014 + 25.0000 = 100.2014;\ 100.2014 + 25.2004 = 125.4018
$$

$$
125.4018 + 24.9001 = 150.3019;\ 150.3019 + 25.1001 = 175.4020;\ 175.4020 + 25.4016 = 200.8036
$$

So $\sum x_i^2 = 200.8036$.

**Step 4:** Compute the correction term $\dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$:

$$
\frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = \frac{(40.08)^2}{8} = \frac{1606.4064}{8} = 200.8008
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum x_i^2 - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = 200.8036 - 200.8008 = 0.0028
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the sample variance:

$$
s^2 = \frac{0.0028}{n-1} = \frac{0.0028}{7} = 0.000400 \text{ V}^2
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the sample standard deviation:

$$
s = \sqrt{0.000400} = 0.0200 \text{ V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\bar{x} = 5.010$ V, $s = 0.020$ V. The instrument reads *correctly* on average (only 0.01 V off true value) and is highly precise (1 part in 250).

---

### Solution — Part (b): Mean Deviation

**Step 1:** Compute absolute deviations $\vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$ (recall $\bar{x} = 5.010$):

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$ |
|---|---|
| 5.01 | $\vert 5.01 - 5.010\vert = 0.000$ |
| 5.03 | $\vert 5.03 - 5.010\vert = 0.020$ |
| 4.98 | $\vert 4.98 - 5.010\vert = 0.030$ |
| 5.00 | $\vert 5.00 - 5.010\vert = 0.010$ |
| 5.02 | $\vert 5.02 - 5.010\vert = 0.010$ |
| 4.99 | $\vert 4.99 - 5.010\vert = 0.020$ |
| 5.01 | $\vert 5.01 - 5.010\vert = 0.000$ |
| 5.04 | $\vert 5.04 - 5.010\vert = 0.030$ |

**Step 2:** Sum the absolute deviations:

$$
\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert = 0.000 + 0.020 + 0.030 + 0.010 + 0.010 + 0.020 + 0.000 + 0.030 = 0.120
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mean deviation:

$$
M.D. = \frac{0.120}{8} = 0.015 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** M.D. (0.015 V) < $s$ (0.020 V). This is mathematically *expected* for symmetric data because squaring penalizes the larger deviations (0.030) more heavily.

---

### Solution — Part (c): Range and Coefficient of Variation

**Range:**

$$
R = x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 5.04 - 4.98 = 0.06 \text{ V}
$$

**Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
C.V. = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.020}{5.010} \times 100\% \approx 0.3992\% \approx 0.40\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Comment:** A C.V. of 0.40% is excellent for a digital voltmeter at the 5 V level. The instrument is precise (low dispersion) and the mean is within 0.2% of the reference value (good accuracy as well). For laboratory-grade instrumentation, the standard is typically $\pm 0.1\%$ — this instrument is on the borderline of "industrial grade" specification.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Measure

| Scenario | Best Measure | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetric, no outliers | Standard Deviation | Mathematically tractable, ties to Normal distribution |
| Skewed data, outliers present | Quartile Deviation / M.D. | Robust, not affected by tails |
| Quick check, $n \le 5$ | Range | Simplest, only two points needed |
| Comparing variability of *different* processes | C.V. (relative) | Dimensionless, fair comparison |
| Combining independent uncertainties | Variance (RSS) | Variances add for independent sources |
| ISO 17025 uncertainty report | $s$ (with coverage factor) | International metrology standard |
