# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** Define *dispersion*. Why is the arithmetic mean alone insufficient to characterize a set of industrial measurements?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dispersion is the measure of the extent to which the values in a dataset are spread or scattered around a central value (typically the mean). The arithmetic mean alone is insufficient because two completely different datasets can have the same mean yet drastically different variabilities. For example, two pressure transducers may both read an average of 100 kPa, but one may fluctuate within ±0.1 kPa (precise) while the other swings by ±5 kPa (noisy). Dispersion quantifies this scatter, which is essential for assessing the **precision, repeatability, and uncertainty** of any measurement system as required by ISO/IEC 17025 and modern quality standards. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between *variance* and *standard deviation*. Why is standard deviation preferred in engineering reports?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Variance ($\sigma^2$ or $s^2$) is the mean of the squared deviations from the mean, and its unit is the **square** of the original data unit (e.g., V², m², °C²). Standard deviation ($\sigma$ or $s$) is the positive square root of the variance, and it carries the **same unit** as the original measurement. Standard deviation is preferred in engineering reports because (i) it is dimensionally consistent with the measured quantity, making physical interpretation direct, (ii) it can be directly compared against tolerance bands and specification limits, and (iii) it is the natural scale parameter of the Normal (Gaussian) distribution, which underlies most measurement uncertainty models. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module Test, 2024 Scheme]**

### ❓ Question A — *(14 Marks)*

**Q(A). (a)** The following data set represents the temperature (in °C) recorded by a calibrated RTD sensor at 10 successive time instants during a process-control experiment:

$$
\{72.4,\ 71.9,\ 72.6,\ 72.1,\ 72.3,\ 71.8,\ 72.5,\ 72.0,\ 72.2,\ 72.4\}
$$

Calculate:
- (i) The arithmetic mean of the readings.
- (ii) The mean deviation from the mean.
- (iii) The sample standard deviation using the shortcut formula.

**(b)** A manufacturer claims that the coefficient of variation of a batch of pressure gauges is **0.85%**. If the sample mean is **250 kPa** and the sample standard deviation is **2.20 kPa**, verify the claim. Also compute the **range** of the data if the maximum and minimum readings are **254.7 kPa** and **245.3 kPa** respectively, and comment on the relative merits of C.V. and range as dispersion measures.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Arithmetic Mean (2 marks):**

Sum of the readings:

$$
\sum x_i = 72.4 + 71.9 + 72.6 + 72.1 + 72.3 + 71.8 + 72.5 + 72.0 + 72.2 + 72.4
$$

Pair-wise addition:

$$
72.4 + 71.9 = 144.3;\ 72.6 + 72.1 = 144.7;\ 72.3 + 71.8 = 144.1
$$

$$
72.5 + 72.0 = 144.5;\ 72.2 + 72.4 = 144.6
$$

$$
\sum x_i = 144.3 + 144.7 + 144.1 + 144.5 + 144.6 = 722.2
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{722.2}{10} = 72.22 \text{ °C}
$$

**(ii) Mean Deviation (2 marks):**

Compute $\vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$ for each reading (note $\bar{x} = 72.22$):

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 72.22\vert$ |
|---|---|
| 72.4 | 0.18 |
| 71.9 | 0.32 |
| 72.6 | 0.38 |
| 72.1 | 0.12 |
| 72.3 | 0.08 |
| 71.8 | 0.42 |
| 72.5 | 0.28 |
| 72.0 | 0.22 |
| 72.2 | 0.02 |
| 72.4 | 0.18 |

Sum:

$$
0.18 + 0.32 + 0.38 + 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.42 + 0.28 + 0.22 + 0.02 + 0.18 = 2.20
$$

$$
M.D. = \frac{2.20}{10} = 0.22 \text{ °C}
$$

**(iii) Sample Standard Deviation via Shortcut Formula (3 marks):**

Compute $\sum x_i^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i^2 &= (72.4)^2 + (71.9)^2 + (72.6)^2 + (72.1)^2 + (72.3)^2 \\
&\quad + (71.8)^2 + (72.5)^2 + (72.0)^2 + (72.2)^2 + (72.4)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each square:

- $(72.4)^2 = 5241.76$
- $(71.9)^2 = 5169.61$
- $(72.6)^2 = 5270.76$
- $(72.1)^2 = 5198.41$
- $(72.3)^2 = 5227.29$
- $(71.8)^2 = 5155.24$
- $(72.5)^2 = 5256.25$
- $(72.0)^2 = 5184.00$
- $(72.2)^2 = 5212.84$
- $(72.4)^2 = 5241.76$

Summing:

$$
5241.76 + 5169.61 = 10411.37
$$

$$
10411.37 + 5270.76 = 15682.13
$$

$$
15682.13 + 5198.41 = 20880.54
$$

$$
20880.54 + 5227.29 = 26107.83
$$

$$
26107.83 + 5155.24 = 31263.07
$$

$$
31263.07 + 5256.25 = 36519.32
$$

$$
36519.32 + 5184.00 = 41703.32
$$

$$
41703.32 + 5212.84 = 46916.16
$$

$$
46916.16 + 5241.76 = 52157.92
$$

So $\sum x_i^2 = 52157.92$.

Correction term:

$$
\frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = \frac{(722.2)^2}{10} = \frac{521572.84}{10} = 52157.284
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum x_i^2 - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = 52157.92 - 52157.284 = 0.636
$$

Sample variance:

$$
s^2 = \frac{0.636}{n-1} = \frac{0.636}{9} = 0.07067 \text{ °C}^2
$$

Sample standard deviation:

$$
s = \sqrt{0.07067} \approx 0.2658 \text{ °C} \approx 0.266 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (a):** $\bar{x} = 72.22$ °C, $M.D. = 0.22$ °C, $s \approx 0.266$ °C.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute the coefficient of variation:

$$
C.V._{\text{calculated}} = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.20}{250} \times 100\% = 0.88\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the manufacturer's claim:

$$
C.V._{\text{calculated}} = 0.88\% \approx C.V._{\text{claimed}} = 0.85\%
$$

The calculated value of 0.88% is within an acceptable tolerance (typically ±0.1%) of the claimed 0.85%. Therefore, the manufacturer's claim is **verified to a reasonable engineering approximation**. *(3 marks)*

**Step 3:** Compute the range:

$$
R = x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 254.7 - 245.3 = 9.4 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Comparative comment on C.V. vs. Range *(4 marks)*:

The **coefficient of variation (0.88%)** is a *relative* measure — it is dimensionless and expressed as a percentage. It is universally comparable across instruments of different ranges, types, and physical units. Hence, the manufacturer's claim of "0.85% C.V." is a *standardised specification* that buyers can compare against competitor products globally.

In contrast, the **range (9.4 kPa)** is an *absolute* measure. It conveys a single number that depends entirely on the unit (kPa) and on the specific measurement scale. Two different pressure gauges with very different ranges (e.g., 0–100 kPa vs. 0–1000 kPa) cannot be fairly compared using the absolute range. Furthermore, the range uses only **two** data points (the maximum and minimum) and discards all information about the *intermediate* distribution, making it extremely sensitive to a single outlier.

**Conclusion:** The C.V. is the **superior** measure for cross-instrument comparison and for process-capability reporting, while the range remains useful for *quick, in-line* checks during manufacturing where a single out-of-spec reading must be flagged instantly.

---

### ❓ Question B — *(14 Marks)* *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q(B). (a)** Define *coefficient of variation* and *range*. The resistances (in ohms) of 6 nominally 100 Ω resistors measured in a quality-control lab are:

$$
\{98.5,\ 101.2,\ 99.8,\ 100.5,\ 97.9,\ 102.1\}
$$

Calculate the range, mean deviation, and standard deviation. Which of these three measures is most informative for a quality-control engineer? Justify.

**(b)** Two production lines, A and B, manufacture the same component. Line A has $\bar{x}_A = 50.0$ mm, $s_A = 0.20$ mm. Line B has $\bar{x}_B = 50.0$ mm, $s_B = 0.45$ mm. The design specification is $50.00 \pm 0.60$ mm. Determine the **process capability index** $C_p$ for both lines. Comment on which line should be selected for the order.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Range (1 mark):**

$$
R = x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 102.1 - 97.9 = 4.2 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 2: Mean (1 mark):**

$$
\sum x_i = 98.5 + 101.2 + 99.8 + 100.5 + 97.9 + 102.1 = 600.0
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{600.0}{6} = 100.0 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Mean Deviation (2 marks):**

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 100.0\vert$ |
|---|---|
| 98.5 | 1.5 |
| 101.2 | 1.2 |
| 99.8 | 0.2 |
| 100.5 | 0.5 |
| 97.9 | 2.1 |
| 102.1 | 2.1 |

$$
\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert = 1.5 + 1.2 + 0.2 + 0.5 + 2.1 + 2.1 = 7.6
$$

$$
M.D. = \frac{7.6}{6} = 1.2667 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.27 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation via shortcut (3 marks):**

Compute $\sum x_i^2$:

$$
(98.5)^2 + (101.2)^2 + (99.8)^2 + (100.5)^2 + (97.9)^2 + (102.1)^2
$$

- $(98.5)^2 = 9702.25$
- $(101.2)^2 = 10241.44$
- $(99.8)^2 = 9960.04$
- $(100.5)^2 = 10100.25$
- $(97.9)^2 = 9584.41$
- $(102.1)^2 = 10424.41$

Sum:

$$
9702.25 + 10241.44 = 19943.69;\ 19943.69 + 9960.04 = 29903.73
$$

$$
29903.73 + 10100.25 = 40003.98;\ 40003.98 + 9584.41 = 49588.39;\ 49588.39 + 10424.41 = 60012.80
$$

So $\sum x_i^2 = 60012.80$.

Correction term:

$$
\frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n} = \frac{(600.0)^2}{6} = \frac{360000}{6} = 60000.00
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
60012.80 - 60000.00 = 12.80
$$

Sample variance:

$$
s^2 = \frac{12.80}{n-1} = \frac{12.80}{5} = 2.56 \text{ Ω}^2
$$

Sample standard deviation:

$$
s = \sqrt{2.56} = 1.6 \text{ Ω}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (a):** $R = 4.2$ Ω, $M.D. \approx 1.27$ Ω, $s = 1.6$ Ω.

**Justification (3 marks — included in total):** The **standard deviation** is the most informative for a quality-control engineer because (i) it uses *all* data points (unlike range, which uses only two), (ii) it penalizes large deviations quadratically, making it sensitive to the rare but critical outliers that exceed tolerance, and (iii) it ties directly to the Normal distribution and to process capability indices like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, which are the universal language of Six-Sigma quality engineering.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Process Capability Index Formula:**

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

where USL = Upper Specification Limit, LSL = Lower Specification Limit, and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the process.

For both lines: USL = 50.60 mm, LSL = 49.40 mm.

**For Line A:** $s_A = 0.20$ mm

$$
C_{p,A} = \frac{50.60 - 49.40}{6 \times 0.20} = \frac{1.20}{1.20} = 1.00
$$

**For Line B:** $s_B = 0.45$ mm

$$
C_{p,B} = \frac{50.60 - 49.40}{6 \times 0.45} = \frac{1.20}{2.70} \approx 0.444
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - $C_p = 1.00$ (Line A) means the process *just barely* meets the specification — the natural 6σ spread exactly equals the tolerance band. Defect rate ≈ 0.27% (2700 ppm).
> - $C_p \approx 0.444$ (Line B) means the process variability is *greater* than the tolerance band — the process is **not capable**. Defect rate is very high (likely > 10%).

**Recommendation:** **Line A must be selected.** Only Line A meets the minimum industry threshold of $C_p \ge 1.00$ for a capable process. Line B should be sent back for process improvement (machine calibration, tooling change, operator retraining) before it can be used. For world-class quality (Six Sigma), a $C_p \ge 2.00$ is targeted.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read carefully before writing the exam):**
> 1. **Divisor Mistake:** Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the *sample* variance formula costs **2 full marks** in KTU evaluations. Always check whether the question states "sample" or "population."
> 2. **Mean Deviation Sign Error:** Forgetting to take the *absolute value* of deviations makes $\sum(x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$, and the resulting M.D. = 0, which is mathematically wrong. Always write $\vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$.
> 3. **Shortcut Formula Sign:** The formula is $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum x_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}\right]$. The *minus* sign is mandatory. Writing a *plus* sign will yield a wrong (and obviously nonsensical) result. Verifying $\sum x_i^2 \ge \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$ by Cauchy-Schwarz is a good self-check.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** Reporting variance in V² when the question asks for "standard deviation" is a **1-mark** deduction. Always match the unit to the measure.
> 5. **Coefficient of Variation in %:** C.V. *must* be expressed as a percentage (multiply by 100) — not as a decimal — to align with industry convention.
> 6. **Range Inclusivity:** $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$, *not* the count of intervals. Many students write "$R = n - 1$", which is the number of *class intervals* in a frequency distribution — a different concept entirely.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Dispersion ≠ Mean.** Mean gives the "center"; dispersion gives the "spread." Both are needed.
- [x] **Sum of deviations from the mean is always zero** — a foundational identity proven by the definition of $\bar{x}$.
- [x] **Range $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$** is the simplest but the weakest measure; uses only 2 data points.
- [x] **Mean Deviation $M.D. = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$** uses absolute values; robust to outliers.
- [x] **Variance $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$** uses squaring; sensitive to outliers; squared units.
- [x] **Standard Deviation $s = \sqrt{s^2}$** is the *gold standard* in engineering; same units as the data.
- [x] **Shortcut Formula:** $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum x_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}\right]$ — preferred for hand calculations.
- [x] **Use $n$ for population, $n-1$ for sample.** KTU problems are almost always sample-based.
- [x] **Coefficient of Variation $C.V. = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$** allows fair comparison across instruments and units.
- [x] **Quartile Deviation** is the measure of choice for skewed data with extreme outliers.
- [x] **Process Capability $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$** is the industrial standard for accepting/rejecting a production line.
- [x] **Numerical trick:** Always cross-check $\sum x_i^2 \ge \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}$ — if violated, you have made an arithmetic error.
- [x] **Reporting style:** Standard deviation is written as $\bar{x} \pm s$ (e.g., $72.22 \pm 0.27$ °C) in instrumentation reports.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range (R):** In the context of a measuring instrument, the **range** is the region between the *Lower Range Limit (LRL)* and the *Upper Range Limit (URL)* within which the instrument is designed to operate and produce reliable measurements. Mathematically, it is expressed as the **difference between the maximum and minimum values** of the measurand that the instrument can accept as input.

In other words, the **range** of an instrument is the **total extent of input values** over which the instrument is expected to function correctly. It is often expressed as a *span of values* — for example, a pressure gauge may have a range of **0 to 10 bar**, or a thermocouple-based thermometer may have a range of **−200 °C to +1372 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Measuring Range vs. Indication Range:**
> - The **measuring range** refers to the input signal values the instrument can measure.
> - The **indication range** refers to the corresponding output (display) values the instrument can produce.
> In a *linear* instrument, these two are identical, but in *non-linear* instruments (like square-root extractors for flow), they may differ.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **standard bathroom weighing scale** in your home. It typically has markings from **0 kg to 150 kg**.

- The **0 kg** mark is the **Lower Range Limit (LRL)**.
- The **150 kg** mark is the **Upper Range Limit (URL)**.
- The complete scale from 0 to 150 kg is the **Range** of the weighing scale.
- The *difference* between these two extremes — i.e., **150 kg − 0 kg = 150 kg** — is called the **Span**.

If you try to weigh a baby elephant (say 500 kg) on this scale, the scale will either break or give an erroneous reading — because you are operating **outside its range**.

Similarly, in industrial instrumentation, every transducer, sensor, and display device has a defined range. Operating outside this range leads to:
1. **Saturation** (output no longer changes with input),
2. **Damage to the sensing element**, and
3. **Loss of accuracy and linearity**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **Range** as the *highway* on which your instrument drives. The **Span** is the *length* of the highway. The **LRL** is the *entry point*, and the **URL** is the *exit point*. Going beyond the exit is risky!

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | The region between LRL and URL over which the instrument is designed to measure. |
| **Span** | The algebraic difference between URL and LRL (Span = URL − LRL). |
| **Zero (Z)** | The lower limit of the range, expressed as a *value of the measured variable*. |
| **Upper Range Limit (URL)** | The maximum value of the measured variable the instrument can measure. |
| **Lower Range Limit (LRL)** | The minimum value of the measured variable the instrument can measure. |
| **Range of a Calibrated Instrument** | A specific subrange within the overall instrument range, bounded by calibrated lower and upper values. |
| **Suppressed-Zero Range** | A range where the LRL is *greater than zero* (e.g., 20 °C to 100 °C). |
| **Elevated-Zero Range** | A range where LRL is *above absolute zero* of the measurand (e.g., 50 bar to 200 bar). |
| **Vacuum-Zero Range** | A range starting from a *negative* value (e.g., −1 bar to +10 bar gauge pressure). |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **SI Unit of Range:** Same as the unit of the measurand (e.g., **°C** for temperature, **bar** for pressure, **m/s** for velocity).
> - **Dimension:** Same as the measurand.
> - **Standard Reference Conditions:** As per **ISA-S51.1 (Process Instrumentation Terminology)**, range is defined under *Standard Conditions* of temperature (25 °C), humidity (60% RH), and atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — How Range Works in an Instrument

A measuring instrument does not function usefully across *all possible values* of a physical quantity. Every sensor is designed for a specific **range**, beyond which its behaviour is undefined, non-linear, or destructive. The following structured logic explains the *why* and *how* of instrument range:

- **Step 1 — Selection of Sensing Element:** The manufacturer chooses a sensing element (e.g., a strain gauge, RTD, bourdon tube) whose physical response curve is monotonic and reasonably linear within a *chosen physical input band*.
- **Step 2 — Calibration Span:** During factory calibration, the instrument is exposed to two reference standards — one at the **LRL** and one at the **URL** — to set the *zero* and *span* adjustment points.
- **Step 3 — Linearisation:** Internal firmware (or external signal conditioning) maps the sensor's raw output to a *linear scale* spanning from the LRL to the URL of the output.
- **Step 4 — Indication/Transmission:** The processed signal is either displayed locally (indication) or transmitted as a standardised signal (e.g., 4–20 mA current loop, 0–10 V, 3–15 psi pneumatic).
- **Step 5 — Range Verification:** In field calibration, the instrument is verified at **0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%** of its range (a **5-point calibration** as per KTU/ISA standards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Range important?**
> The range directly influences:
> - **Accuracy:** Instruments are most accurate in the *middle* of their range.
> - **Resolution:** A small range over a fixed output span gives *better resolution*.
> - **Cost:** Wider range instruments are typically more expensive.
> - **Safety:** Operating outside the range can lead to instrument failure and plant accidents.

### 2.2 Span, Zero, and Range — The Golden Triangle

These three parameters *completely define* the operating envelope of a static measuring instrument:

- **Span (S):** S = URL − LRL
- **Zero (Z):** Z = LRL (when the output is 0% of the output range)
- **Range:** [LRL, URL] = [Z, Z + S]

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> In process industries (oil refineries, power plants, chemical plants), range selection is critical. For example, a *differential pressure transmitter* for a boiler drum level measurement may have a calibrated range of **0 to 500 mm H₂O**, even though the *static head pressure* may be **10 bar** — the range is set to the *change* in level, not the absolute pressure. This is a classic example of **suppressed-zero range**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme University Examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Span (S)** | $S = \text{URL} - \text{LRL}$ | Same as measurand | Always positive |
| 2 | **Range (R)** | $R = \text{LRL} \text{ to } \text{URL}$ | Same as measurand | Expressed as an interval |
| 3 | **Zero (Z)** | $Z = \text{LRL}$ | Same as measurand | Value of measurand at 0% output |
| 4 | **% of Range (x%)** | $x\% = \dfrac{M - \text{LRL}}{S} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | $M$ = measured value |
| 5 | **Turndown Ratio (TDR)** | $\text{TDR} = \dfrac{\text{URL}}{M_{\min, \text{useful}}}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of max to min useful input |
| 6 | **Dynamic Range (DR)** | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | Used in electronic/sensor specs |
| 7 | **Resolution (Res)** | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{S}{2^{n}}$ | Same as measurand | $n$ = number of ADC bits |
| 8 | **Output Signal Range** | $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + \left(\dfrac{M - \text{LRL}}{S}\right) \times 16$ | mA | Standard 4–20 mA loop |
| 9 | **Suppressed Zero Output** | $I_{0} = 4 + \left(\dfrac{\text{LRL}}{S}\right) \times 16$ | mA | At suppressed zero point |
| 10 | **Rangeability** | $R_b = \dfrac{\text{URL}}{M_{\text{min, accurate}}}$ | Dimensionless | Useful for flowmeters |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Oil & Gas Refineries:** Pressure transmitters on distillation columns have ranges like **0 to 50 bar** with suppressed zero, while differential pressure transmitters for flow use **0 to 2000 mmWC**.
2. **Power Plants:** Thermocouples in superheaters have ranges of **0 to 1600 °C**, while feedwater temperature RTDs are calibrated to **0 to 300 °C**.
3. **HVAC Systems:** Differential pressure sensors across filters have small ranges of **0 to 500 Pa** to detect clogging.
4. **Medical Electronics:** A pulse oximeter has an SpO₂ range of **0 to 100%** and a heart rate range of **30 to 250 bpm**.
5. **Automotive Sensors:** MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensors in cars have a range of **10 kPa to 300 kPa**.
6. **Aerospace:** Pitot-static systems use differential pressure ranges of **0 to 100 kPa** for airspeed measurement.

> [!TIP]
> **Field Tip:** When selecting an instrument, always choose a range such that the **normal operating point lies between 50% and 75% of the range** for optimal accuracy and safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion of Measured Value to % of Range

**Given:** An instrument with LRL = 20 °C and URL = 120 °C. A measured value of $M$ = 80 °C.

**Step 1 — Write down the given parameters.**

$$\text{LRL} = 20\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad \text{URL} = 120\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad M = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Span using the fundamental range equation.**

The span is the algebraic difference between the upper and lower range limits. This represents the *total length* of the measurement band.

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{URL} - \text{LRL} \\
  &= 120\,^\circ\text{C} - 20\,^\circ\text{C} \\
  &= 100\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Express the measured value as a percentage of the range.**

The percentage of range is the normalised distance from the LRL to the measured value, expressed as a fraction of the span. This conversion is the foundation of *4–20 mA current loop calculations*.

$$\begin{aligned}
x\% &= \frac{M - \text{LRL}}{S} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{80\,^\circ\text{C} - 20\,^\circ\text{C}}{100\,^\circ\text{C}} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{60\,^\circ\text{C}}{100\,^\circ\text{C}} \times 100 \\
    &= 0.6 \times 100 \\
    &= 60\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary case.**

If $M$ = LRL = 20 °C, then $x\% = 0\%$ ✓
If $M$ = URL = 120 °C, then $x\% = 100\%$ ✓
Therefore, the formula is dimensionally and logically consistent.

**Step 5 — Compute the corresponding 4–20 mA output.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{out}} &= 4 + (x\%) \times \frac{16}{100} \\
               &= 4 + 0.6 \times 16 \\
               &= 4 + 9.6 \\
               &= 13.6\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

This means that at 80 °C, the transmitter outputs **13.6 mA** in a standard 4–20 mA current loop — a typical KTU exam problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Range Determination from Span and Zero

**Given:** A pressure gauge has a Span $S$ = 50 bar and a Zero $Z$ = 10 bar. Find the Range and the URL.

**Step 1 — State the definitions.**

$$\text{URL} = Z + S, \quad \text{Range} = [Z, \, Z+S]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

The URL is computed by adding the span to the zero point. This is a *suppressed-zero* instrument because the LRL is non-zero.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{URL} &= Z + S \\
           &= 10\,\text{bar} + 50\,\text{bar} \\
           &= 60\,\text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — State the range as an interval.**

$$\text{Range} = [10\,\text{bar}, \, 60\,\text{bar}] \quad \text{or} \quad 10 \text{ to } 60\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret physically.**

This is a **suppressed-zero pressure gauge** — it cannot measure vacuum or pressures below 10 bar. It is ideal for *boost pressure* measurements in hydraulic systems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Turndown Ratio and Dynamic Range

**Given:** A vortex flowmeter has a URL = 1000 m³/h and a minimum useful flow of 25 m³/h.

**Step 1 — Define Turndown Ratio.**

The turndown ratio is the ratio of the maximum measurable value to the minimum useful measurable value. It indicates the *flexibility* of the instrument.

$$\text{TDR} = \frac{\text{URL}}{M_{\min,\text{useful}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDR} &= \frac{1000\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}}{25\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}} \\
           &= 40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dynamic range (in dB) if required.**

The dynamic range in decibels expresses the ratio in logarithmic scale, often used in *electronic instrumentation* and *signal processing*.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DR} &= 20 \log_{10}(\text{TDR}) \\
          &= 20 \log_{10}(40) \\
          &= 20 \times 1.6021 \\
          &= 32.04\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

| Example | LRL | URL | Span | Zero | Type of Range | 4–20 mA at midpoint |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | 0 °C | 100 °C | 100 °C | 0 °C | Normal zero | 12.0 mA |
| 2 | 20 °C | 120 °C | 100 °C | 20 °C | Suppressed zero | 12.0 mA |
| 3 | −30 °C | 70 °C | 100 °C | −30 °C | Vacuum zero | 12.0 mA |
| 4 | 10 bar | 60 bar | 50 bar | 10 bar | Suppressed zero | 12.0 mA |
| 5 | 4 mA | 20 mA | 16 mA | 4 mA | Current loop | 12.0 mA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the matrix:** Although the **midpoint current is always 12 mA** in a 4–20 mA loop (since $(4+20)/2 = 12$), the *physical measured value* at this midpoint changes depending on the range. This is why **range identification** is critical in the field.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Resolution from Range and ADC Bits

**Given:** A digital voltmeter with Range = 0 to 10 V uses a 12-bit ADC.

**Step 1 — Compute the total number of discrete steps.**

A 12-bit ADC produces $2^{12}$ discrete output levels, which is the resolution of the digital encoding.

$$N = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096\,\text{steps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the resolution in volts per step.**

The resolution is the smallest change in input that can be detected, equal to the span divided by the number of discrete steps.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Res} &= \frac{S}{2^{n}} \\
           &= \frac{10\,\text{V} - 0\,\text{V}}{4096} \\
           &= \frac{10}{4096}\,\text{V} \\
           &= 2.44 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \\
           &= 2.44\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

The smallest voltage change this instrument can resolve is **2.44 mV**. If the range were *narrower* (say 0 to 1 V), the resolution would improve to **0.244 mV** — a key reason why range selection affects precision.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Range, Span, and Zero Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instrument Range Block] --> B[Lower Range Limit LRL]
    A --> C[Upper Range Limit URL]
    A --> D[Span S = URL minus LRL]
    A --> E[Zero Z = LRL]
    A --> F[Range = Z to Z plus S]

    B --> G[0 percent of output]
    C --> H[100 percent of output]
    D --> I[Total length of measurement band]
    E --> J[Value of measurand at 0 percent output]

    G --> K[4 mA in 4 to 20 mA loop]
    H --> L[20 mA in 4 to 20 mA loop]

    K --> M[Live Zero Concept]
    L --> M

    F --> N{Normal Zero}
    F --> O{Suppressed Zero}
    F --> P{Vacuum Zero}
    F --> Q{Elevated Zero}
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Range Types in Industrial Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Range Classification] --> A1[Normal Zero Range]
    Start --> A2[Suppressed Zero Range]
    Start --> A3[Vacuum Zero Range]
    Start --> A4[Elevated Zero Range]

    A1 --> B1[LRL is zero and Measurand is positive]
    A1 --> B1a[Example: 0 to 100 degree Celsius]
    A1 --> B1b[Example: 0 to 10 bar gauge pressure]

    A2 --> C1[LRL is greater than zero]
    A2 --> C1a[Example: 20 to 200 degree Celsius]
    A2 --> C1b[Example: 50 to 100 bar absolute pressure]

    A3 --> D1[LRL is negative relative to atmospheric reference]
    A3 --> D1a[Example: minus 1 to plus 9 bar gauge]
    A3 --> D1b[Example: minus 30 to plus 70 degree Celsius]

    A4 --> E1[LRL is above zero measurand value]
    A4 --> E1a[Example: 10 to 60 bar in hydraulic systems]
    A4 --> E1b[Example: 100 to 500 mmWC for level measurement]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Range-Based Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Measurand Input Physical Quantity] --> SENSOR[Sensing Element]
    SENSOR --> COND[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Lineariser]
    COND --> ADC[ADC with N bit Resolution]
    ADC --> MICRO[Microcontroller and Range Calibration Logic]
    MICRO --> DISPLAY[Local Digital Display]
    MICRO --> OUTPUT[4 to 20 mA or 0 to 10 V Output]
    MICRO --> COMMS[HART or Fieldbus Communication]

    RANGECONF[Range Configuration Block] --> MICRO
    RANGECONF --> RC1[Set LRL]
    RANGECONF --> RC2[Set URL]
    RANGECONF --> RC3[Compute Span]
    RANGECONF --> RC4[Set Zero and Live Zero]
    RANGECONF --> RC5[Apply Engineering Units]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Interpretation

The **block-level diagram** above demonstrates how a modern smart transmitter uses its **range configuration** to:

1. Accept the **LRL** and **URL** entered by the engineer (either via local keys or HART communicator).
2. Compute the **Span** internally.
3. Apply the **Live Zero** concept (output = 4 mA at LRL, not 0 mA — to distinguish a true zero from a wire break).
4. Map all subsequent measured values to the **0–100%** of output range using the formula:

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + \left(\frac{M - \text{LRL}}{S}\right) \times 16 \,\,\text{mA}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Live Zero (4 mA)?** If the output started at 0 mA, it would be impossible to distinguish between a *true zero reading* and a *broken wire* (open loop). The 4 mA "live zero" provides a *diagnostic baseline* — anything below 4 mA indicates a fault.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define the term "Range" of a measuring instrument. Differentiate between Range and Span with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **range** of a measuring instrument is the *region* between the lowest and highest values of the measurand for which the instrument is designed to operate. It is expressed as the interval **[LRL, URL]**.
>
> The **span** is the *algebraic difference* between the upper and lower range limits: $S = \text{URL} - \text{LRL}$.
>
> **Example:** A thermometer with a range of **0 °C to 100 °C** has a span of **100 °C**. Thus, the *range* is the *interval*, while the *span* is its *width*.
>
> *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for span formula, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is meant by "suppressed-zero range"? Give one industrial example.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **suppressed-zero range** is a range in which the *lower range limit (LRL)* is **greater than zero**. In other words, the instrument does not start measuring from zero; the zero of the *output scale* corresponds to a *non-zero* value of the measurand.
>
> **Example:** A pressure transmitter used for boiler drum level measurement has a suppressed-zero range of **0 to 500 mm H₂O** above the static head. Here, the LRL (0 mm H₂O differential) corresponds to the *minimum* level, not to *true zero* absolute pressure.
>
> **Advantage:** Suppression of zero *expands the scale* over the region of interest, thereby improving *resolution* and *accuracy*.
>
> *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for example and advantage.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q. (a)** Define the following terms with reference to a measuring instrument: **Range, Span, Zero, Lower Range Limit, and Upper Range Limit**. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A temperature transmitter has a range of **−20 °C to +180 °C**. The output is a standard **4–20 mA** current signal.
   - (i) Calculate the **span** of the instrument. *(2 marks)*
   - (ii) Find the current output when the measured temperature is **75 °C**. *(3 marks)*
   - (iii) Find the temperature corresponding to a current output of **12.8 mA**. *(2 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks)**

1. **Range (R):** The range of a measuring instrument is the *interval* between the lowest and highest values of the measurand that the instrument is designed to measure with specified accuracy. It is expressed as **[LRL, URL]**.
   *(1 mark)*

2. **Span (S):** The span is the *algebraic difference* between the upper range limit and the lower range limit. $S = \text{URL} - \text{LRL}$. The span is always expressed in the same unit as the measurand and is always a *positive* quantity.
   *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Zero (Z):** The zero of an instrument is the *value of the measurand* at which the output is set to its *minimum designed value* (e.g., 4 mA in a 4–20 mA loop or 0% of the output range). The zero is numerically equal to the LRL.
   *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Lower Range Limit (LRL):** The lowest value of the measurand for which the instrument is rated to give reliable measurements. It defines the *start* of the operating range.
   *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Upper Range Limit (URL):** The highest value of the measurand for which the instrument is rated to give reliable measurements. It defines the *end* of the operating range.
   *(1.5 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$\text{LRL} = -20\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad \text{URL} = +180\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**(i) Span Calculation (2 marks):**

The span is the algebraic difference between the URL and LRL. The negative sign of LRL is treated algebraically.

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{URL} - \text{LRL} \\
  &= 180\,^\circ\text{C} - (-20\,^\circ\text{C}) \\
  &= 180 + 20 \\
  &= 200\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Span = 200 °C** ✓ *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Current Output at 75 °C (3 marks):**

First, express the measured value as a percentage of the range.

$$\begin{aligned}
x\% &= \frac{M - \text{LRL}}{S} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{75 - (-20)}{200} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{95}{200} \times 100 \\
    &= 47.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

Now, convert this percentage to a 4–20 mA current output.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{out}} &= 4 + (x\%) \times \frac{16}{100} \\
               &= 4 + 0.475 \times 16 \\
               &= 4 + 7.6 \\
               &= 11.6\,\text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Current output = 11.6 mA** ✓ *(3 marks — 1.5 for %, 1.5 for mA)*

**(iii) Temperature at 12.8 mA (2 marks):**

First, find the percentage of range corresponding to 12.8 mA.

$$\begin{aligned}
x\% &= \frac{I_{\text{out}} - 4}{16} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{12.8 - 4}{16} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{8.8}{16} \times 100 \\
    &= 55\%
\end{aligned}$$

Now, find the corresponding temperature.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \text{LRL} + (x\%) \times \frac{S}{100} \\
  &= -20 + 0.55 \times 200 \\
  &= -20 + 110 \\
  &= 90\,^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Temperature = 90 °C** ✓ *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of **range, span, and zero** in a measuring instrument. Discuss the **types of ranges** (normal, suppressed, vacuum, and elevated zero) with one example each. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A level transmitter measures water level in a tank from **0.5 m to 8.5 m**. The output is **3–15 psi** pneumatic signal.
   - (i) Find the span and zero of the transmitter. *(2 marks)*
   - (ii) Determine the pneumatic output pressure when the level is **5.0 m**. *(3 marks)*
   - (iii) What is the level when the output pressure is **9 psi**? *(2 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)**

*(4 marks for conceptual explanation, 3 marks for range types with examples)*

**Conceptual Explanation:**

A measuring instrument operates over a well-defined region of the measurand called its **range**. The range is bounded by the **lower range limit (LRL)** and the **upper range limit (URL)**. The *span* is the *width* of this region, computed as $S = \text{URL} - \text{LRL}$. The *zero* of the instrument is the *value of the measurand* that produces the *minimum output* of the instrument (e.g., 4 mA in a current loop, 3 psi in a pneumatic loop, or 0% of the display).

These three parameters — *range, span, and zero* — together define the **complete operating envelope** of a static measuring instrument.

*(3 marks)*

**Block Diagram (text representation for exam):**

```
[Measurand Input] → [Sensor] → [Signal Conditioner] → [Range-Specific Lineariser] → [Output: 4-20 mA / 3-15 psi / 0-10 V]
                                  ↑                         ↑
                              [LRL Setting]            [URL Setting]
                                  ↑                         ↑
                              [Zero Adjustment]      [Span Adjustment]
```

*(1 mark for block diagram)*

**Types of Ranges (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Normal-Zero Range:** LRL = 0. Example: A pressure gauge with range **0 to 10 bar**.
2. **Suppressed-Zero Range:** LRL > 0. Example: A temperature transmitter with range **100 °C to 300 °C**.
3. **Vacuum-Zero Range:** LRL < 0 (relative to atmospheric). Example: A compound pressure gauge with range **−1 bar to +10 bar**.
4. **Elevated-Zero Range:** LRL is above the zero of the measurand but the range is "elevated" above atmospheric. Example: A gauge pressure transmitter with range **5 bar to 50 bar**.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$\text{LRL} = 0.5\,\text{m}, \quad \text{URL} = 8.5\,\text{m}, \quad \text{Output: 3 to 15 psi}$$

**(i) Span and Zero (2 marks):**

The span is the algebraic difference between URL and LRL. The zero is numerically equal to the LRL in a *suppressed-zero* configuration.

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{URL} - \text{LRL} = 8.5 - 0.5 = 8.0\,\text{m} \\
Z &= \text{LRL} = 0.5\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Span = 8.0 m, Zero = 0.5 m** ✓ *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Pneumatic Output at 5.0 m (3 marks):**

First, find the percentage of range corresponding to a level of 5.0 m.

$$\begin{aligned}
x\% &= \frac{M - \text{LRL}}{S} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{5.0 - 0.5}{8.0} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{4.5}{8.0} \times 100 \\
    &= 56.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

Now, convert this percentage to the 3–15 psi pneumatic output.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{out}} &= 3 + (x\%) \times \frac{12}{100} \\
               &= 3 + 0.5625 \times 12 \\
               &= 3 + 6.75 \\
               &= 9.75\,\text{psi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Pneumatic output = 9.75 psi** ✓ *(3 marks — 1.5 for %, 1.5 for psi)*

**(iii) Level at 9 psi (2 marks):**

First, find the percentage of range for 9 psi output.

$$\begin{aligned}
x\% &= \frac{P_{\text{out}} - 3}{12} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{9 - 3}{12} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{6}{12} \times 100 \\
    &= 50\%
\end{aligned}$$

Now, find the corresponding level.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \text{LRL} + (x\%) \times \frac{S}{100} \\
  &= 0.5 + 0.50 \times 8.0 \\
  &= 0.5 + 4.0 \\
  &= 4.5\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Level = 4.5 m** ✓ *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Range with Span:** Students often write "Range = 100 °C" when they mean *Span*. The *range* is the *interval* (0–100 °C); the *span* is the *number* (100 °C). Marks are lost on the very first line.
> 2. **Sign Error in Span Calculation:** For ranges like *−20 °C to 180 °C*, students write $S = 180 - 20 = 160$ instead of $S = 180 - (-20) = 200$. The minus sign *must* be treated algebraically.
> 3. **Mixing up Current Loop Formulas:** For a 4–20 mA loop, the formula is $I = 4 + (x\%) \times 0.16$. For a 3–15 psi loop, the formula is $P = 3 + (x\%) \times 0.12$. Using the wrong one is a *3-mark* error.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Live Zero":** Some students compute output as if it starts at 0 mA. Always remember: *4 mA* (or *3 psi*) is the *zero output*, not *0 mA*.
> 5. **Not Stating Units:** KTU examiners *strictly deduct* 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers. Always write "°C", "bar", "mA", "psi" explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Range" in KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Range** = Interval [LRL, URL] — *where the instrument works*.
- **Span** = URL − LRL — *the width* of the range (always positive).
- **Zero** = LRL — *the measurand value at minimum output*.
- **Four Types of Ranges:** Normal zero, Suppressed zero, Vacuum zero, Elevated zero.
- **Live Zero Concept:** 4 mA (or 3 psi) is the *true zero output*, not 0 mA — enables fault detection.
- **% of Range Formula:** $x\% = \dfrac{M - \text{LRL}}{S} \times 100$ — *the most important formula* for KTU numericals.
- **4–20 mA Conversion:** $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + (x\%) \times 0.16\,\text{mA}$.
- **3–15 psi Conversion:** $P_{\text{out}} = 3 + (x\%) \times 0.12\,\text{psi}$.
- **Turndown Ratio (TDR):** $\text{TDR} = \dfrac{\text{URL}}{M_{\min,\text{useful}}}$ — indicates measurement flexibility.
- **Dynamic Range:** $\text{DR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}\right)$ dB — used in electronic sensors.
- **Resolution:** $\text{Res} = \dfrac{S}{2^n}$ — improves with narrower range or higher ADC bits.
- **Selection Rule:** Normal operating point should be in the **50–75%** of the range for best accuracy.
- **Standard Reference:** ISA-S51.1 and IEC 61298 define these terms globally.
- **Typical Exam Weightage:** 3 to 4 marks in Part A; 7 to 14 marks in Part B — *high-yield topic*!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deviation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deviation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the discipline of **Measurements and Industrial Instrumentation**, **Deviation** is formally defined as the **algebraic difference between the measured value (indicated value) of a quantity and its true (or reference) value**. Mathematically, it is the most fundamental representation of **measurement error** at any given instant or operating point.

$$\text{Deviation } (D) = \text{Measured Value } (M) - \text{True Value } (T)$$

It is a signed scalar quantity — a **positive deviation** indicates the instrument is *over-reading* (reading higher than reality), while a **negative deviation** indicates *under-reading*. Deviation is the building block for almost every other static characteristic of an instrument, including **accuracy, precision, repeatability, and error analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Deviation is the numerical departure of an instrument's output from the accepted true value of the measurand under specified reference conditions. It must not be confused with *error band* or *tolerance*, which describe ranges rather than point departures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you step onto a **digital bathroom weighing scale** and it reads **70.4 kg**, but you happen to know from a calibrated laboratory balance that your actual mass is **70.0 kg**. The deviation is simply:

$$D = 70.4 - 70.0 = +0.4 \text{ kg}$$

That **+0.4 kg** is the deviation. The scale consistently shows slightly *more* than reality. In a factory, if a **pressure transmitter** is supposed to read **5.000 bar** at a known reference and instead reads **5.025 bar**, the deviation is **+0.025 bar** — engineers call this the **static deviation** or **zero/span offset** depending on the operating point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Deviation = "How wrong is my instrument reading from what it *should* show?" — nothing more, nothing less.

### 1.3 Classification of Deviation (Hierarchical View)

Deviation in measurement science is broadly classified into:

1. **Absolute Deviation** — Expressed in the same units as the measurand.
2. **Relative (Fractional) Deviation** — Ratio of absolute deviation to the true value (dimensionless).
3. **Percentage Deviation** — Relative deviation expressed as a percentage.
4. **Static Deviation** — Deviation under steady-state (DC) conditions.
5. **Dynamic Deviation** — Deviation arising from transient/dynamic response limitations.
6. **Systematic Deviation (Bias Error)** — Predictable, repeatable, deterministic.
7. **Random Deviation (Random Error)** — Unpredictable, statistical in nature.

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> - **Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)** of an instrument is the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum indicated values — used to normalize deviation.
> - **Standard Reference Temperature** for industrial instrumentation: **23 °C** (per ISO/IEC 17025 conventions).
> - **Standard Gravity** for pressure-related deviations: **9.80665 m/s²** (when converting kgf/cm² to bar).
> - All deviation quantities in KTU problems are typically expressed in **SI base units** unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The 'Why' and 'How'

Every measuring instrument is a **physical transducer** that converts a measurand into a human-readable or machine-readable signal. Because **no physical system is perfectly ideal**, the output almost never matches the input exactly. The difference is the deviation, and it arises from:

- **Calibration imperfections** of the sensing element
- **Friction, hysteresis, and dead-zone** in mechanical linkages
- **Thermal drift** of electronic components
- **Loading effect** when the instrument is connected to the process
- **Environmental interference** (vibration, EMI, humidity)
- **Quantization noise** in digital instruments (LSB size)
- **Random noise** from Brownian motion of charge carriers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A measurement system is judged *not* by the absence of deviation (which is impossible) but by **how small, predictable, and stable** the deviation is across its operating range, time, and environmental envelope.

### 2.2 Types of Deviation — Detailed Logical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Absolute Deviation
- The raw algebraic difference between measured and true value.
- Carries the **same units** as the measurand.
- **Use case:** When a single, dimensionally consistent number is needed for further engineering calculations (e.g., error budget, calibration certificate).

#### 2.2.2 Relative Deviation (Fractional Deviation)
- The **ratio** of absolute deviation to the true value.
- **Dimensionless** — useful for comparing instruments that measure *different* physical quantities.
- **Use case:** Sensor selection, comparator datasheets.

#### 2.2.3 Percentage Deviation
- Relative deviation multiplied by 100.
- **Most commonly used** in industry datasheets and KTU numerical problems.
- **Use case:** Accuracy class specification (e.g., Class 0.5 instrument → ±0.5% of FSD).

#### 2.2.4 Static Deviation
- Deviation present when the input is **constant or very slowly varying**, so dynamic lags have died out.
- Governed purely by the **static calibration curve** of the instrument.
- Comprises **zero error** (intercept) + **span error** (slope error) + **linearity error** (curvature).
- **Use case:** Acceptance testing, periodic calibration.

#### 2.2.5 Dynamic Deviation
- Deviation that appears when the input changes with time, due to the **inability of the instrument to respond instantaneously** (finite bandwidth, time constant, damping).
- For a first-order instrument: $D_{dyn}(t) = x(t) - x_{out}(t)$, where $x_{out}$ lags behind $x$.
- **Use case:** Process control loop tuning, transient response analysis.

#### 2.2.6 Systematic Deviation (Bias)
- **Deterministic** and reproducible. Same direction and magnitude under identical conditions.
- Can be **eliminated by recalibration**.
- Examples: Zero offset, scale-factor error, linear drift with temperature.

#### 2.2.7 Random Deviation (Random Error)
- **Stochastic**, varying unpredictably in sign and magnitude.
- Treated by **statistical methods** (mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals).
- **Cannot be eliminated** by calibration — only minimized by repeated measurements and averaging.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Absolute Deviation | $D = M - T$ | Same as measurand | Signed scalar |
| 2 | Relative Deviation | $D_r = \dfrac{M - T}{T}$ | Dimensionless (or $\vert\cdot\vert$) | Often uses absolute value |
| 3 | Percentage Deviation | $D_\% = \dfrac{M - T}{T} \times 100$ | % | Most-used in KTU problems |
| 4 | % Full-Scale Deviation | $D_{FS\%} = \dfrac{M - T}{\text{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Used for instrument class rating |
| 5 | Zero Error (Static) | $D_0 = M \big\vert_{x=0}$ | Measurand units | Intercept of cal. curve |
| 6 | Span Error | $D_{span} = M_{FS} - T_{FS}$ | Measurand units | Slope (gain) error |
| 7 | Linearity Error | $D_{lin} = M_{act}(x) - M_{ideal}(x)$ | Measurand units | Best-straight-line reference |
| 8 | Mean Deviation (Statistical) | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i - T_i)$ | Measurand units | Sample bias |
| 9 | Standard Deviation (Sample) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | Measurand units | Random spread |
| 10 | Standard Deviation (Population) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | Measurand units | When $n$ is large |
| 11 | Variance | $\sigma^2$ | (units)² | Square of std. deviation |
| 12 | Probable Error | $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | Measurand units | 50% confidence band |
| 13 | Average Deviation | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert D_i \vert$ | Measurand units | Mean of absolute deviations |
| 14 | Dynamic Deviation (1st-order) | $D_{dyn}(t) = x(t) - x_{out}(t)$ | Measurand units | Time-dependent residual |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** When a question gives a range of measured values without explicit true value, treat the **arithmetic mean** of the readings as the best estimate of the true value, then compute deviations from that mean.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Deviation Analysis |
|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Process Industries (Oil & Gas, Petrochem)** | Calibration certificates, ISO 9001 audits, safety instrumented systems (SIS) proof testing |
| **Power Plants** | Turbine governor performance, boiler drum level transmitter drift monitoring |
| **Automotive** | Sensor calibration for ECU, OBD-II diagnostic thresholds |
| **Aerospace** | Flight-instrument accuracy budgets (pitot-static, INS/GPS deviation budgets) |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | cGMP-compliant measurement uncertainty for batch release |
| **Metrology Labs** | NABL/ISO 17025 accreditation requires formal deviation quantification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Standard Deviation from First Principles

The **standard deviation** is the most fundamental statistical measure of random deviation. It is derived by minimizing the squared-error between data points and the mean.

**Step 1 — Define the squared-deviation objective function**

Let $J(\mu)$ be the sum of squared deviations from an arbitrary central value $\mu$:

$$J(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 — Minimize $J(\mu)$ with respect to $\mu$ by setting derivative to zero**

$$\dfrac{dJ}{d\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(D_i - \mu)(-1) = 0$$

$$-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \mu) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i - n\mu = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the optimal $\mu$**

$$\mu_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i = \bar{D}$$

> **Logic:** The point that minimizes the sum of squared deviations is the *arithmetic mean* of the deviations. Hence the **mean is the most probable value of the true reading** when only random deviations are present.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\bar{D}$ back to define the variance**

The minimum value of $J$ is:

$$J_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \bar{D})^2$$

By definition, the **variance** is the average of this minimum squared-deviation sum. To obtain an *unbiased* estimator, we divide by $n - 1$ (Bessel's correction):

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \bar{D})^2$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain the standard deviation**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - \bar{D})^2}$$

> **Logic:** Standard deviation has the same units as the deviation itself, making it physically interpretable as the "typical" magnitude of a random deviation from the mean.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A voltmeter is used to measure a reference voltage of **50.00 V** (traceable to NABL standards). The following **ten** readings are taken under repeatability conditions: 50.4, 50.2, 49.9, 50.1, 50.3, 49.8, 50.0, 50.2, 50.1, 50.5 V.

Compute:
(a) The **mean reading** and the **absolute deviation** of the mean.
(b) The **percentage deviation** of the mean.
(c) The **standard deviation** of the readings.
(d) The **probable error** of a single reading.

---

**Step (a) — Mean Reading and Absolute Deviation of the Mean**

Sum of all ten readings:

$$S = 50.4 + 50.2 + 49.9 + 50.1 + 50.3 + 49.8 + 50.0 + 50.2 + 50.1 + 50.5$$

Adding term by term:
- $50.4 + 50.2 = 100.6$
- $100.6 + 49.9 = 150.5$
- $150.5 + 50.1 = 200.6$
- $200.6 + 50.3 = 250.9$
- $250.9 + 49.8 = 300.7$
- $300.7 + 50.0 = 350.7$
- $350.7 + 50.2 = 400.9$
- $400.9 + 50.1 = 451.0$
- $451.0 + 50.5 = 501.5$

So $S = 501.5$ V. Mean:

$$\bar{M} = \dfrac{S}{n} = \dfrac{501.5}{10} = 50.15 \text{ V}$$

Absolute deviation of the mean from the true value:

$$D = \bar{M} - T = 50.15 - 50.00 = +0.15 \text{ V}$$

---

**Step (b) — Percentage Deviation of the Mean**

$$D_\% = \dfrac{D}{T} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.15}{50.00} \times 100 = 0.30\,\%$$

---

**Step (c) — Standard Deviation**

Compute each deviation from the mean: $d_i = M_i - \bar{M}$

| $i$ | $M_i$ (V) | $d_i = M_i - 50.15$ | $d_i^2$ |
|-----|-----------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | 50.4 | +0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 2 | 50.2 | +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 3 | 49.9 | -0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 4 | 50.1 | -0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 5 | 50.3 | +0.15 | 0.0225 |
| 6 | 49.8 | -0.35 | 0.1225 |
| 7 | 50.0 | -0.15 | 0.0225 |
| 8 | 50.2 | +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 9 | 50.1 | -0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 10 | 50.5 | +0.35 | 0.1225 |

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum d_i^2 = 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.0225 + 0.1225 + 0.0225 + 0.0025 + 0.0025 + 0.1225$$

Adding sequentially:
- $0.0625 + 0.0025 = 0.0650$
- $0.0650 + 0.0625 = 0.1275$
- $0.1275 + 0.0025 = 0.1300$
- $0.1300 + 0.0225 = 0.1525$
- $0.1525 + 0.1225 = 0.2750$
- $0.2750 + 0.0225 = 0.2975$
- $0.2975 + 0.0025 = 0.3000$
- $0.3000 + 0.0025 = 0.3025$
- $0.3025 + 0.1225 = 0.4250$

So $\sum d_i^2 = 0.4250 \text{ V}^2$

Sample standard deviation (using Bessel's correction, $n-1 = 9$):

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.4250}{9}} = \sqrt{0.047222} = 0.2173 \text{ V}$$

---

**Step (d) — Probable Error**

$$PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma = 0.6745 \times 0.2173 = 0.1466 \text{ V}$$

**Interpretation:** 50% of the readings lie within ±0.1466 V of the mean value of 50.15 V.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Deviation Quantities Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| True Voltage | $T$ | 50.00 V | NABL-traceable reference |
| Mean Reading | $\bar{M}$ | 50.15 V | Best estimate from $n=10$ |
| Absolute Deviation | $D$ | +0.15 V | Voltmeter over-reads |
| Percentage Deviation | $D_\%$ | 0.30% | Within typical ±0.5% class |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | 0.2173 V | Random spread measure |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | 0.0472 V² | Squared spread |
| Probable Error | $PE$ | 0.1466 V | 50%-confidence radius |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Deviation (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["DEVIATION IN MEASUREMENT"]
    nodeB["ABSOLUTE DEVIATION"]
    nodeC["RELATIVE DEVIATION"]
    nodeD["PERCENTAGE DEVIATION"]
    nodeE["STATIC DEVIATION"]
    nodeF["DYNAMIC DEVIATION"]
    nodeG["SYSTEMATIC BIAS"]
    nodeH["RANDOM ERROR"]
    nodeI["ZERO ERROR"]
    nodeJ["SPAN ERROR"]
    nodeK["LINEARITY ERROR"]
    nodeL["TIME RESPONSE LAG"]
    nodeM["BANDWIDTH LIMIT"]
    nodeN["THERMAL DRIFT"]
    nodeO["HYSTERESIS"]
    nodeP["NOISE FLUCTUATIONS"]
    nodeQ["QUANTIZATION ERROR"]
    nodeR["REPEATABILITY ERROR"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeP
    nodeH --> nodeQ
    nodeH --> nodeR
    nodeG --> nodeN
    nodeG --> nodeO
```

### 4.2 Measurement System Functional Block Diagram with Deviation Injection Points

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT"]
    nodeB["VARIABLE CONVERSION ELEMENT"]
    nodeC["VARIABLE MANIPULATION ELEMENT"]
    nodeD["DATA TRANSMISSION ELEMENT"]
    nodeE["DATA PRESENTATION ELEMENT"]
    nodeF["TRUE VALUE T"]
    nodeG["MEASURED VALUE M"]
    nodeH["DEVIATION D = M MINUS T"]

    nodeA -->|"Transduces measurand"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"Amplifies or conditions"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"Sends signal"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"Displays or records"| nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram — Sources of Deviation

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["INPUT MEASURAND X"]
    nodeB["SENSOR STAGE"]
    nodeC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    nodeD["ADC OR INDICATOR"]
    nodeE["OUTPUT READING Y"]
    nodeF["ZERO ERROR INJECTION"]
    nodeG["SPAN ERROR INJECTION"]
    nodeH["LINEARITY ERROR INJECTION"]
    nodeI["RANDOM NOISE INJECTION"]
    nodeJ["FINAL DEVIATION D = Y MINUS X"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeA --> nodeJ
```

### 4.4 Calibration Curve Diagram (Static Deviation Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["IDEAL CALIBRATION LINE Y = MX PLUS C"]
    nodeB["ACTUAL CALIBRATION CURVE"]
    nodeC["ZERO DEVIATION AT ORIGIN"]
    nodeD["SPAN DEVIATION AT FULL SCALE"]
    nodeE["LINEARITY DEVIATION AT MIDPOINT"]
    nodeF["INPUT X AXIS"]
    nodeG["OUTPUT Y AXIS"]

    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the term **deviation** as applied to a measurement system. Distinguish clearly between **absolute deviation** and **percentage deviation**.

**Model Answer:**
Deviation in a measurement system is the algebraic difference between the value indicated by the instrument (Measured Value, $M$) and the accepted true value of the measurand (True Value, $T$):

$$D = M - T$$

- **Absolute Deviation** is expressed in the **same physical units** as the measurand. It directly indicates by how much (in magnitude) the instrument reading is off from the true value.
- **Percentage Deviation** is the absolute deviation expressed as a **percentage of the true value** (or of the full scale, depending on the convention):

$$D_\% = \dfrac{M - T}{T} \times 100\,\%$$

Percentage deviation is dimensionless and is the preferred metric for specifying instrument **accuracy class** (e.g., a Class 0.5 instrument has a maximum percentage deviation of ±0.5% of full scale).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** What is **systematic deviation**? Give two examples. How can it be minimized?

**Model Answer:**
Systematic deviation (also called **bias error** or **deterministic error**) is a **reproducible and predictable** departure of the measured value from the true value. It occurs consistently in the same direction and magnitude whenever the measurement is performed under identical conditions.

**Examples:**
1. **Zero error** in a Bourdon pressure gauge — the pointer does not return to zero when the applied pressure is removed.
2. **Thermal drift** in a strain-gauge bridge — the output shifts systematically with ambient temperature.

**Minimization methods:**
- Periodic **recalibration** against traceable standards.
- Application of **correction factors** in software (look-up tables).
- Use of **better compensation techniques** (e.g., dummy gauges, four-wire RTD connections).
- Maintaining the instrument under **controlled reference conditions** (temperature, humidity).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Past Pattern]**

#### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(a). [7 Marks]** A resistance temperature detector (RTD) is used to measure the temperature of a stirred oil bath. The true temperature, measured by a platinum resistance standard, is **82.4 °C**. The RTD indicates the following six readings when the bath is held at this temperature: **83.1, 82.9, 82.7, 83.0, 82.8, 83.2 °C**.

Compute:
1. The **mean indicated value**.
2. The **absolute deviation** of the mean.
3. The **percentage deviation** of the mean (with respect to the true value).

**Q(b). [7 Marks]** For the same data, compute:
1. The **standard deviation** of the readings.
2. The **probable error** of a single observation.
3. Comment on whether the RTD can be considered acceptable for a **Class 1.0** application.

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Step 1 — Sum of all six readings:**

$$S = 83.1 + 82.9 + 82.7 + 83.0 + 82.8 + 83.2$$

Adding sequentially:
- $83.1 + 82.9 = 166.0$
- $166.0 + 82.7 = 248.7$
- $248.7 + 83.0 = 331.7$
- $331.7 + 82.8 = 414.5$
- $414.5 + 83.2 = 497.7$

So $S = 497.7$ °C.

**Step 2 — Mean indicated value:**

$$\bar{M} = \dfrac{S}{n} = \dfrac{497.7}{6} = 82.95 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute deviation of the mean:**

$$D = \bar{M} - T = 82.95 - 82.4 = +0.55 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage deviation:**

$$D_\% = \dfrac{D}{T} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.55}{82.4} \times 100 = 0.6675\,\%$$

The RTD over-reads the true temperature by 0.55 °C, which is 0.6675% high.

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute individual deviations $d_i = M_i - \bar{M}$:**

| $i$ | $M_i$ (°C) | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ |
|-----|-----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | 83.1 | +0.15 | 0.0225 |
| 2 | 82.9 | -0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 3 | 82.7 | -0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 4 | 83.0 | +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| 5 | 82.8 | -0.15 | 0.0225 |
| 6 | 83.2 | +0.25 | 0.0625 |

**Step 2 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum d_i^2 = 0.0225 + 0.0025 + 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.0225 + 0.0625 = 0.1750 \text{ °C}^2$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviation (using $n-1 = 5$):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.1750}{5}} = \sqrt{0.0350} = 0.1871 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Probable error:**

$$PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma = 0.6745 \times 0.1871 = 0.1262 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Class 1.0 Acceptance Check:**

For a Class 1.0 instrument, the maximum allowable percentage deviation is ±1.0% of the true value (or full scale, depending on convention). The percentage deviation computed is 0.6675%, which is **less than 1.0%**, so the RTD **is acceptable** for a Class 1.0 application, although a calibration correction of −0.55 °C should be applied to remove the systematic bias.

---

#### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q(a). [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the difference between:
1. **Static deviation** and **Dynamic deviation**.
2. **Systematic deviation** and **Random deviation**.

**Q(b). [7 Marks]** The following ten readings of a reference **10.000 V** standard are taken: **10.12, 9.98, 10.05, 10.01, 9.95, 10.08, 10.03, 9.97, 10.00, 10.06 V**. Determine the **average deviation**, **standard deviation**, and the **variance** of the data set.

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

| Aspect | Static Deviation | Dynamic Deviation |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Deviation observed when the input is constant or changes very slowly, so all transients have died out. | Deviation observed when the input varies with time, due to the finite speed of response of the instrument. |
| **Cause** | Calibration imperfections, zero/span errors, non-linearity. | Time constant, damping, dead time, bandwidth limits. |
| **Time Domain** | Steady-state / DC. | Transient / AC. |
| **Magnitude** | Fixed for a given input level. | Varies with $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and frequency content of input. |
| **Elimination** | Recalibration, correction factor. | Cannot be eliminated — only mitigated by faster sensors. |

| Aspect | Systematic Deviation | Random Deviation |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Deterministic, repeatable departure in a fixed direction. | Stochastic, unpredictable variation around the mean. |
| **Reproducibility** | Same magnitude and sign on every trial. | Sign and magnitude change randomly. |
| **Treatment** | Calibration correction, look-up tables. | Statistical methods (mean, std. dev., confidence intervals). |
| **Examples** | Zero offset, scale-factor error, thermal drift. | Johnson–Nyquist noise, quantization noise, vibration. |
| **Effect of Averaging** | Unaffected by repeated measurements. | Reduced by $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ factor. |

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Step 1 — Mean of the readings:**

$$S = 10.12 + 9.98 + 10.05 + 10.01 + 9.95 + 10.08 + 10.03 + 9.97 + 10.00 + 10.06$$

Adding sequentially:
- $10.12 + 9.98 = 20.10$
- $20.10 + 10.05 = 30.15$
- $30.15 + 10.01 = 40.16$
- $40.16 + 9.95 = 50.11$
- $50.11 + 10.08 = 60.19$
- $60.19 + 10.03 = 70.22$
- $70.22 + 9.97 = 80.19$
- $80.19 + 10.00 = 90.19$
- $90.19 + 10.06 = 100.25$

So $S = 100.25$ V, and:

$$\bar{M} = \dfrac{100.25}{10} = 10.025 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations from the mean $d_i = M_i - 10.025$:**

| $i$ | $M_i$ (V) | $d_i$ | $\vert d_i \vert$ | $d_i^2$ |
|-----|-----------|-------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | 10.12 | +0.095 | 0.095 | 0.009025 |
| 2 | 9.98 | -0.045 | 0.045 | 0.002025 |
| 3 | 10.05 | +0.025 | 0.025 | 0.000625 |
| 4 | 10.01 | -0.015 | 0.015 | 0.000225 |
| 5 | 9.95 | -0.075 | 0.075 | 0.005625 |
| 6 | 10.08 | +0.055 | 0.055 | 0.003025 |
| 7 | 10.03 | +0.005 | 0.005 | 0.000025 |
| 8 | 9.97 | -0.055 | 0.055 | 0.003025 |
| 9 | 10.00 | -0.025 | 0.025 | 0.000625 |
| 10 | 10.06 | +0.035 | 0.035 | 0.001225 |

**Step 3 — Average Deviation:**

$$\sum \vert d_i \vert = 0.095 + 0.045 + 0.025 + 0.015 + 0.075 + 0.055 + 0.005 + 0.055 + 0.025 + 0.035 = 0.430 \text{ V}$$

$$D_{avg} = \dfrac{0.430}{10} = 0.0430 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum d_i^2 = 0.009025 + 0.002025 + 0.000625 + 0.000225 + 0.005625 + 0.003025 + 0.000025 + 0.003025 + 0.000625 + 0.001225 = 0.02545 \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 5 — Standard Deviation (sample, $n - 1 = 9$):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.02545}{9}} = \sqrt{0.002828} = 0.05318 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = 0.002828 \text{ V}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong denominator in standard deviation:** KTU students frequently divide by $n$ instead of $n - 1$. For small samples ($n < 30$), Bessel's correction ($n - 1$) is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing true value with mean value:** When the true value is unknown, the **arithmetic mean** of the readings must be used as the reference. The first deviation computed is then the *systematic bias*, and the residual spread gives the *random* component.
> 3. **Sign convention error:** Forgetting that deviation is **signed**. Writing $|D|$ everywhere loses the over-reading/under-reading information.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Standard deviation carries the same units as the measurand; **variance** carries the **square** of the units. Students often mark both with the same unit and lose marks.
> 5. **Incomplete problem statement handling:** When full-scale value (FS) is given instead of true value, percentage deviation is computed with respect to FS, not the true value — this distinction is **frequently tested**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Deviation = Measured Value − True Value** (signed scalar).
- **Absolute Deviation** is in measurand units; **Percentage Deviation** is dimensionless × 100.
- **Static Deviation** appears under steady input (zero, span, linearity errors).
- **Dynamic Deviation** appears under time-varying input (lag, bandwidth).
- **Systematic Deviation** = predictable, eliminable by recalibration; **Random Deviation** = stochastic, reducible by averaging.
- **Mean** of readings = best estimate of true value when only random deviation exists.
- **Variance** uses $n$ in denominator for population, $n-1$ for sample (Bessel's correction).
- **Probable Error** = $0.6745\,\sigma$ → 50% confidence band; **Standard Error** = $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ → uncertainty of the mean.
- **Class rating** of an instrument is specified as a percentage of **Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)**, not of the true value.
- **KTU exam focus areas:** formula recall, manual computation of $\sigma$ and $D_\%$, and clear distinction between systematic vs random components.
- **Quick reference identities:** $\bar{D} = 0$ (when deviations are measured from their own mean); $\sum D_i = 0$ identically.
- **Industrial implication:** Smaller, stable, and predictable deviations = better instrument; total absence of deviation is physically impossible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Deviation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Average Deviation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Measurements and Industrial Instrumentation**, **Average Deviation (A.D.)** is a fundamental statistical measure of dispersion (scatter) that quantifies the average absolute distance of a set of measured observations from a chosen central tendency — typically the **arithmetic mean (A.M.)** or the **median** of the dataset. Unlike standard deviation, it ignores the algebraic sign of each deviation and treats every departure from the central value as equally important, giving a robust, intuitive picture of measurement scatter.

Mathematically, the average deviation is defined as the arithmetic mean of the absolute deviations of each observation from the central value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Board Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *"Average Deviation is the mean of the absolute values of the deviations of the individual measured values from a measure of central tendency (mean or median). It is a first-order measure of dispersion extensively used in precision analysis of industrial measuring instruments, calibration data reduction, and quality-control laboratories."*

For an ungrouped (raw) dataset of size $n$ with central value $A$, the average deviation is formally given by:

$$
A.D. = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - A \vert
$$

where $x_i$ represents the $i^{\text{th}}$ measured value, and $A$ is either the **mean ($\bar{x}$)** or the **median ($\tilde{x}$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a quality-control engineer at a bearing manufacturing plant. You pick **5 finished bearings** from a batch and weigh them. The ideal (target) weight is **100 g**, but the actual readings are: **98, 102, 99, 101, 100 g**.

If you simply ask, *"By how much, on average, do the bearings deviate from 100 g?"* — you are asking for the **Average Deviation**.

- $|98-100| = 2$
- $|102-100| = 2$
- $|99-100| = 1$
- $|101-100| = 1$
- $|100-100| = 0$

Sum of absolute deviations = $2+2+1+1+0 = 6$
Average Deviation = $6/5 = \mathbf{1.2\ g}$

So, on average, each bearing is **1.2 g away from the target** — a direct, easy-to-grasp statement about precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "absolute" values?** Because in measurements, a deviation of $-2$ (under-weight) and $+2$ (over-weight) are equally undesirable. Taking the magnitude (modulus) captures this symmetry.

### 1.3 Industrial Significance

In instrumentation, the Average Deviation is preferred over the **mean** as a central value for the following practical reasons:

1. It is **less sensitive to extreme outliers** (rogue readings, electrical transients) than standard deviation.
2. It directly expresses the **typical measurement error** in the same physical units as the measurement.
3. It is extensively used in **calibration certificates, ISO 17025 reports, and Six-Sigma process capability studies** for first-order precision assessment.
4. Combined with the **Coefficient of Average Deviation**, it allows **dimensionless comparison** between instruments measuring different physical quantities.

### 1.4 Two Variants Recognized by KTU Syllabus

| Variant | Symbol | Central Value Used | Industrial Use |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Average Deviation from Mean | $A.D._{\bar{x}}$ | Arithmetic Mean $\bar{x}$ | Process monitoring, calibration drift |
| Average Deviation from Median | $A.D._{\tilde{x}}$ | Median $\tilde{x}$ | Skewed data (e.g., particle size, lifetime) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Coefficient of Average Deviation** is a normalized, unitless quantity:
> **C.A.D. (from mean)** = $\dfrac{A.D.}{\bar{x}}$
> **C.A.D. (from median)** = $\dfrac{A.D.}{\tilde{x}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**For Ungrouped (Raw) Data — Average Deviation from Mean:**

1. Collect $n$ measured values: $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
2. Compute the arithmetic mean:
   $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
3. Compute the absolute deviation of every reading from the mean: $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$.
4. Sum all absolute deviations: $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$.
5. Divide the sum by $n$ to obtain the **Average Deviation**.

**For Grouped (Frequency) Data — Average Deviation from Mean:**

1. Tabulate class intervals and corresponding frequencies $f_i$.
2. Identify the class mark (mid-value) $x_i$ for each class.
3. Compute the mean using:
   $$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{N}$$
4. Compute $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ for each class and multiply by its frequency: $f_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$.
5. Sum the products and divide by $N$:
   $$A.D. = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$$

**Average Deviation from Median** is computed analogously, except the central value is the median $\tilde{x}$ (the middle value for ungrouped data, or obtained via cumulative frequency interpolation for grouped data).

### 2.2 Why Average Deviation Exists Alongside Standard Deviation

- **Standard Deviation** squares each deviation, which:
  - Amplifies large errors disproportionately.
  - Requires more computation (no closed-form for grouped data using absolute values).
- **Average Deviation** uses **first-power** absolute deviations:
  - Linear, intuitive, and directly in measurement units.
  - Algebraically simpler and well-suited for **first-order precision analysis** in industrial metrology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A low Average Deviation indicates high **precision** (repeatability), while a low value relative to the true value indicates high **accuracy**. They are *not* the same — Average Deviation speaks to *precision*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean (ungrouped) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = number of observations | Same as $x$ |
| 2 | Arithmetic Mean (grouped) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $f_i$ = class frequency, $x_i$ = class mark | Same as $x$ |
| 3 | Average Deviation (ungrouped, from mean) | $A.D. = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ | Sum of absolute deviations divided by $n$ | Same as $x$ |
| 4 | Average Deviation (grouped, from mean) | $A.D. = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum f_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ | $N = \sum f_i$ | Same as $x$ |
| 5 | Average Deviation from Median | $A.D. = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \vert x_i - \tilde{x} \vert$ | $\tilde{x}$ = median | Same as $x$ |
| 6 | Coefficient of A.D. (mean) | $C.A.D. = \dfrac{A.D.}{\bar{x}}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 7 | Coefficient of A.D. (median) | $C.A.D. = \dfrac{A.D.}{\tilde{x}}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 8 | Median (ungrouped, odd $n$) | Middle value when sorted | — | Same as $x$ |
| 9 | Median (ungrouped, even $n$) | Mean of two middle values | — | Same as $x$ |
