Substituting $y = z + x$:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{x} = -m \, a(t)$$

where $a(t) = \ddot{x}$ is the input acceleration of the housing. This is the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\,m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \, a(t)\,}$$

Dividing by $m$ and defining the **natural angular frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and the **damping ratio** $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$:

$$\ddot{z} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = -a(t)$$

The **steady-state amplitude ratio** of the relative displacement $z$ to the input acceleration $a$ for sinusoidal input $a = a_0 \sin\omega t$ is:

$$\frac{\vert Z \vert}{\vert a_0 / \omega_n^2 \vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1 - r^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta r\right)^2}}, \quad r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Flat Region" Rule:** For an accelerometer to faithfully reproduce the input acceleration, the operating frequency $\omega$ must be **well below the natural frequency** ($\omega \ll \omega_n$). In the limit $r \to 0$, the magnitude ratio approaches 1, and $z \approx -a/\omega_n^2$, meaning the relative displacement is directly proportional to the input acceleration — which is exactly what we want to measure.

## 2.2 Frequency Response & Bandwidth Considerations

| Frequency Region | Behavior of Accelerometer | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \le \omega \le 0.4\,\omega_n$ | Output faithfully tracks input acceleration | **Useful measurement range** |
| $0.4\,\omega_n \le \omega \le 0.7\,\omega_n$ | Slight gain increase due to resonance onset | Marginal |
| $\omega \approx \omega_n$ | Large resonant peak (especially at low $\zeta$) | Avoid |
| $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | Output attenuates, $z$ approaches a constant (mass becomes stationary in space) | Invalid |

Hence the rule of thumb: **upper useful frequency $\approx 0.3\,f_n$ to $0.5\,f_n$** for $\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ (which yields maximally flat response).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the boxed formulas. The 14-mark questions in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations (ESE) for this module typically ask you to **derive the sensitivity** of one of the three accelerometers from first principles.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m a$ | Inertial force on proof mass | N |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural angular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Z}{a/\omega_n^2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Frequency response (displacement) | ratio |
| 6 | $z = \dfrac{m\,a}{k}$ | Static relative displacement | m |
| 7 | Cantilever strain: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 m L}{E b t^2}\,a$ | Strain in cantilever-beam strain gauge accelerometer | dimensionless |
| 8 | Cantilever deflection: $y = \dfrac{4 m L^3}{E b t^3}\,a$ | Tip deflection of cantilever (force at tip) | m |
| 9 | Gauge factor: $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Piezoresistive sensitivity of strain gauge | dimensionless |
| 10 | Bridge output: $V_o = V_{ex}\,G_F\,\varepsilon$ | Wheatstone bridge output voltage | V |
| 11 | Piezoelectric charge: $Q = d_{ij}\,F = d_{ij}\,m\,a$ | Charge generated by quartz | C |
| 12 | Piezoelectric sensitivity: $S_q = Q/a = d_{ij}\,m$ | Charge sensitivity | C·s²/m |
| 13 | Piezoelectric voltage: $V = Q/C_p$ | Output voltage across capacitance $C_p$ | V |
| 14 | MEMS differential capacitance: $\Delta C = \dfrac{2 C_0 \Delta z}{d_0}$ | Capacitance change due to proof-mass displacement | F |
| 15 | MEMS sensitivity: $S_C = \dfrac{\Delta C}{a} = \dfrac{2 C_0}{d_0\,\omega_n^2}$ | Capacitive sensitivity | F·s²/m |
| 16 | $1g = 9.80665$ m/s² | Standard gravity (use $9.81$ unless specified) | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard notation** used in the table: $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m), $m$ = proof mass (kg), $c$ = damping coefficient (N·s/m), $L$ = cantilever length, $b$ = cantilever width, $t$ = cantilever thickness, $E$ = Young's modulus, $d_{ij}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N), $C_0$ = nominal capacitance, $d_0$ = nominal gap, $\Delta z$ = displacement.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Typical Accelerometer Family Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Aircraft flight testing & turbomachinery vibration | **Piezoelectric (Quartz)** | Wide bandwidth (1 Hz – 10 kHz), high sensitivity, rugged |
| Civil structural monitoring of bridges/buildings | **MEMS** | Low cost, DC response, integrated electronics |
| Automotive airbag crash sensing | **MEMS** | Fast response, small, low power, digital output |
| Industrial machinery condition monitoring | **Piezoelectric** | Long-term stability, high-frequency content |
| Low-frequency seismology & tilt sensing | **Strain Gauge / Force-Balance** | DC response (can measure static $g$) |
| Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) in drones & robotics | **MEMS** | Miniature, low power, triaxial integration |

## 2.5 Why Three Different Families? – A Trade-off Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge Accelerometers** are essentially DC-responsive but have limited high-frequency bandwidth and lower sensitivity. **Quartz (Piezoelectric) Accelerometers** offer excellent high-frequency response but cannot measure true DC (static) acceleration because the generated charge leaks away. **MEMS Accelerometers** combine the strengths of both, but with very small proof masses, so the per-axis signal is tiny and requires sophisticated on-chip signal conditioning.

The remainder of this module expands each of these three families in detail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Strain-Gauge-Based Accelerometer — Derivation of Sensitivity

### 3.1.1 Construction

A typical strain-gauge accelerometer consists of:
- A **proof mass** $m$ bonded to the free end of a **cantilever beam** (or to a flexure/spring element).
- **Strain gauges** bonded onto the top and bottom surfaces of the beam (two active, two dummy in a full Wheatstone bridge).
- A rigid housing that is bolted or glued to the vibrating body whose acceleration is to be measured.

When the housing accelerates with $a$, the inertial force $F = ma$ acts on the proof mass, bending the beam. The bending moment produces **strain** $\varepsilon$ on the gauge surface, changing its resistance, which the bridge converts to a voltage.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Cantilever Tip Deflection (Force at Free End)

Consider a uniform rectangular cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $t$, and Young's modulus $E$. A concentrated force $F$ is applied at the free end, perpendicular to the beam. The classical Euler-Bernoulli tip deflection is given by the standard formula:

$$y_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

The second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section is:

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$y_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E \left(\dfrac{b t^3}{12}\right)} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b t^3}$$

For an accelerometer, the force is inertial: $F = m a$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,y_{tip} = \frac{4 m L^3}{E b t^3}\,a\,}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Surface Strain on the Cantilever

The bending moment at a distance $s$ from the fixed end is:

$$M(s) = F (L - s)$$

The bending stress at the outer fibre (distance $t/2$ from the neutral axis) is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (t/2)}{I}$$

The strain at that fibre is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{M (t/2)}{E I} = \frac{6 M}{E b t^2}$$

The maximum strain (at the fixed end, $s = 0$) is:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^2} = \frac{6 m L}{E b t^2}\,a$$

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon = \frac{6 m L}{E b t^2}\,a\,}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Bridge Output Voltage

Each strain gauge has gauge factor $G_F$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \, \varepsilon$$

For a **full Wheatstone bridge** with two active gauges (one in tension, one in compression — top and bottom of the beam) and two dummy (or two more active) gauges, the differential output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_{ex}\, G_F\, \varepsilon$$

Substituting the strain expression:

$$\boxed{\,V_o = V_{ex}\, G_F\, \left(\frac{6 m L}{E b t^2}\right) a\,}$$

The **voltage sensitivity** is:

$$S_V = \frac{V_o}{a} = \frac{6 m L\, V_{ex}\, G_F}{E b t^2} \quad \left[\frac{\text{V}}{\text{m/s}^2}\right]$$

Or, in $g$-units:

$$S_V = \frac{V_o}{a/g} = \frac{6 m L\, V_{ex}\, G_F\, g}{E b t^2} \quad \left[\frac{\text{V}}{g}\right]$$

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Natural Frequency

The cantilever stiffness (force per unit tip deflection) is:

$$k = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{E b t^3}{4 L^3}$$

The natural angular frequency is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{E b t^3}{4 m L^3}}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E b t^3}{4 m L^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A stiffer, shorter, thicker beam (large $E$, $b$, $t$; small $L$) gives high $f_n$ and thus wide measurement bandwidth, but at the cost of lower sensitivity (small $\varepsilon$). This is the classic **sensitivity–bandwidth trade-off** in accelerometer design.

---

## 3.2 Quartz (Piezoelectric) Accelerometer — Derivation of Charge Sensitivity

### 3.2.1 Construction

A piezoelectric (quartz) accelerometer consists of:
- A **piezoelectric crystal** (typically quartz, tourmaline, or PZT ceramic) sandwiched between the base and the proof mass.
- The mass is preloaded against the crystal by a central stud/washer/spring to ensure firm contact.
- The whole assembly is housed in a rugged metal case with an electrical connector.

When the housing accelerates, the inertial force of the mass compresses (or shears) the crystal, generating a charge proportional to the applied force.

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Generated Charge

The fundamental direct piezoelectric constitutive relation (in the 31-mode) is:

$$Q = d_{ij}\, F$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N) and $F$ is the force applied along the appropriate axis.

For an accelerometer, the force is inertial: $F = m a$. Therefore:

$$Q = d_{ij}\, m\, a$$

The **charge sensitivity** is:

$$\boxed{\,S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d_{ij}\, m \quad \left[\frac{\text{C}\cdot\text{s}^2}{\text{m}}\right]\,}$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Output Voltage

The piezoelectric element is electrically modeled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with the element's own capacitance $C_p$ (plus cable capacitance $C_c$ and amplifier input capacitance $C_a$ in parallel: $C_{total} = C_p + C_c + C_a$).

The open-circuit voltage is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_{total}} = \frac{d_{ij}\, m\, a}{C_{total}}$$

The **voltage sensitivity** is:

$$S_V = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{d_{ij}\, m}{C_{total}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Implication for KTU Problems:** Reducing the cable length and using a high-input-impedance charge amplifier **reduces** $C_{total}$, which **increases** the voltage sensitivity. This is why piezoelectric accelerometers are sold with **integral cable / IEPE (Integrated Electronic Piezo-Electric)** configurations using built-in JFET amplifiers.

### 3.2.4 Frequency Response

Because the generated charge is proportional to the *instantaneous* force (and thus to instantaneous acceleration), the piezoelectric accelerometer is inherently a **high-pass device** — it cannot respond to truly static (DC) acceleration. The low-frequency cutoff $f_L$ is governed by the charge amplifier's time constant $\tau = R_f C_f$:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$

The high-frequency cutoff is set by the **mechanical resonance** of the seismic mass and crystal stack:

$$f_H \approx 0.3\, f_n$$

where $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ with $k$ being the stack stiffness.

### 3.2.5 Natural Frequency of Quartz Accelerometer

For a quartz stack of thickness $t$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E_q$:

$$k = \frac{E_q A}{t}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E_q A}{m t}}$$

A typical industrial quartz accelerometer has $f_n$ in the range 20 – 100 kHz.

---

## 3.3 MEMS Accelerometer — Derivation of Capacitive Sensitivity

### 3.3.1 Construction

A **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** accelerometer is fabricated on a silicon chip using batch microfabrication techniques. The typical structure (comb-finger or gap-closing) consists of:
- A silicon **proof mass** suspended by four thin flexure beams.
- A series of **interdigitated comb fingers** (fixed + movable) forming a **differential capacitor pair**.
- The structure is overlaid by ASIC circuitry for capacitance-to-voltage conversion, signal conditioning, and temperature compensation.

When the chip accelerates, the proof mass moves relative to the substrate, changing the gap $d_0$ between the fingers, which changes the capacitance. The differential change is sensed and converted to a voltage or digital output.

### 3.3.2 Derivation of Differential Capacitance Change

The nominal capacitance between one pair of comb fingers is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

where $A$ is the overlap area, $d_0$ is the gap, and $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ is the permittivity of the gap medium (typically air: $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$).

When the proof mass displaces by $\Delta z$ (small compared to $d_0$), the gap on one side becomes $d_0 - \Delta z$ and on the opposite side becomes $d_0 + \Delta z$. The two capacitances are:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta z}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + \Delta z}$$

The **differential capacitance** is:

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left(\frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta z} - \frac{1}{d_0 + \Delta z}\right) = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \,\Delta z}{d_0^2 - (\Delta z)^2}$$

For small displacements ($\Delta z \ll d_0$):

$$\boxed{\,\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \,\Delta z}{d_0^2} = \frac{2 C_0 \,\Delta z}{d_0}\,}$$

### 3.3.3 Derivation of Capacitive Sensitivity to Acceleration

The static relative displacement is:

$$\Delta z = \frac{m a}{k} = \frac{a}{\omega_n^2}$$

Substituting into the differential capacitance:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 C_0}{d_0} \cdot \frac{a}{\omega_n^2}$$

The **capacitive sensitivity** is:

$$\boxed{\,S_C = \frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2 C_0}{d_0\, \omega_n^2} = \frac{2 C_0 m}{d_0 k}\,}$$

### 3.3.4 Derivation of Output Voltage Using a Differential Capacitance-to-Voltage Converter

A typical switched-capacitor ASIC applies a carrier voltage $V_{ref}$ to the two capacitors alternately and reads the difference. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\, \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{2 V_{ref}\, \Delta z}{d_0} = \frac{2 V_{ref}\, a}{d_0 \,\omega_n^2}$$

The MEMS accelerometer is therefore **DC-responsive** (the output exists even at $\omega = 0$ as long as there is a sustained acceleration such as gravity), which makes it ideal for tilt sensing and static inertial measurements.

### 3.3.5 Why MEMS Technology Is Revolutionary

| Property | Benefit |
|---|---|
| Batch silicon fabrication | Very low cost per unit (cents to a few dollars) |
| Tiny size | A few mm² on a chip |
| Low power | Few mA; suitable for battery-powered IoT |
| On-chip ASIC | Digital (I²C/SPI) output, no external amplifier needed |
| High reliability | No moving wear surfaces, monolithic structure |
| Triaxial integration | Three orthogonal sensors on one die |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Strain-Gauge | Quartz (Piezoelectric) | MEMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensing principle | Resistance change due to strain | Charge generation due to force | Capacitance change due to displacement |
| Transduction equation | $\Delta R / R = G_F \varepsilon$ | $Q = d_{ij} F$ | $\Delta C = (2 C_0 / d_0)\,\Delta z$ |
| Sensitivity expression | $S_V = 6 m L V_{ex} G_F / (E b t^2)$ | $S_q = d_{ij} m$ | $S_C = 2 C_0 / (d_0\, \omega_n^2)$ |
| DC response | **Yes** | **No** (high-pass only) | **Yes** |
| Typical bandwidth | 0 – 500 Hz | 1 Hz – 10 kHz | 0 – 1 kHz (typ.) |
| Typical sensitivity | 1 – 100 mV/g | 10 – 1000 pC/g | 0.1 – 10 pF/g or LV/g |
| Operating temperature | $-20^\circ$C to $80^\circ$C | $-50^\circ$C to $250^\circ$C | $-40^\circ$C to $125^\circ$C |
| Power supply needed | Yes (bridge excitation) | No (self-generating) | Yes (ASIC supply) |
| Ruggedness | Moderate | High (no electronics in head) | Moderate (delicate fingers) |
| Cost | Moderate | High | Low |
| Best suited for | Static / low-frequency vibration | High-frequency dynamic vibration | Consumer / industrial / automotive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Block-Level Architecture of an Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input\nAcceleration a on housing] --> B[Seismic Mass m\nsuspended on spring k\nand damper c]
    B --> C[Transduction\nElement\nStrain / Charge / Capacitance]
    C --> D[Signal\nConditioning\nAmplifier / Filter]
    D --> E[Output\nVoltage / Charge /\nDigital Code]
    B -.feedback.-> F[Relative\nDisplacement z\nstrain or charge]
    F --> C
```

> **Interpretation.** The seismic mass converts acceleration into relative displacement, strain, or force. The transduction element converts that mechanical quantity into an electrical signal. The signal conditioning block amplifies, filters, and linearizes the output.

## 4.2 Strain-Gauge Cantilever Accelerometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Housing[Rigid Housing attached to vibrating body]
        direction TB
        F1[Inertial Force\nF = m a] -->|acts on| M1[Proof Mass m]
        M1 -->|bends| B1[Cantilever Beam\nlength L, width b, thickness t]
        B1 -->|develops strain| SG1[Strain Gauge 1\ntop surface in tension]
        B1 -->|develops strain| SG2[Strain Gauge 2\nbottom surface in compression]
        SG1 --> WB1[Wheatstone Bridge\nfull bridge excitation Vex]
        SG2 --> WB1
        WB1 -->|output| OP1[Output Voltage Vo\nproportional to a]
    end
```

> **Reading guide.** The cantilever bends under the inertial load; the two active gauges (top + bottom) double the bridge sensitivity. Dummy gauges in the other two arms compensate for temperature.

## 4.3 Quartz (Piezoelectric) Accelerometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Body[Accelerometer Housing]
        direction TB
        F2[Inertial Force\nF = m a] -->|compresses| M2[Proof Mass m]
        M2 --> PZ[Preloaded Stack of\nPiezoelectric Discs\nquartz or PZT]
        PZ -->|generates charge Q\nper Q = d33 F| CHG[Charge Source Q\nin parallel with Cp]
        CHG -->|output| CB[Coaxial Cable\nCc]
        CB --> AMP[Charge / Voltage\nAmplifier\nhigh input impedance]
        AMP -->|output| OP2[Output Voltage\nV = Q / Ctotal]
    end
```

> **Reading guide.** Mass preloading ensures the crystal is always in compression (positive force generates positive charge). The charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$ converts $Q$ to a usable voltage and sets the low-frequency cutoff.

## 4.4 MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Chip[Silicon Chip]
        direction TB
        F3[Acceleration a] -->|displaces| M3[Proof Mass m\nsuspended by flexures]
        M3 -->|changes gap| CF1[Finger Set 1\nC1 increases]
        M3 -->|changes gap| CF2[Finger Set 2\nC2 decreases]
        CF1 --> SC[Switched-Capacitor\nASIC Front End]
        CF2 --> SC
        SC -->|delta C| ADC[Sigma-Delta ADC\nor analog front end]
        ADC -->|digital| DSP[Temperature\nCompensation DSP]
        DSP --> I2C[I2C or SPI\nDigital Output]
    end
```

> **Reading guide.** The differential comb structure converts displacement into differential capacitance. The on-chip ASIC performs C-to-V conversion, demodulation, A/D conversion, and temperature compensation in a single mixed-signal IC.

## 4.5 Comparative Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Selection of Accelerometer] --> Q1{Is DC or static\nacceleration\nto be measured?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Frequency\nabove 500 Hz?}
    Q1 -->|No| PZT[Use Piezoelectric\nQuartz Accelerometer]
    Q2 -->|No| SG[Use Strain-Gauge\nAccelerometer]
    Q2 -->|Yes, but DC also needed| MEMS[Use MEMS\nCapacitive Accelerometer]
    SG --> COST{Cost target?}
    COST -->|Low cost, high volume| MEMS2[Use MEMS]
    COST -->|Premium, lab grade| SG2[Use Bonded\nStrain Gauge]
    PZT --> PZT2[Use Industrial\nPiezoelectric Sensor]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three main types of accelerometers covered in your syllabus and state one distinguishing feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Strain-Gauge Accelerometer** — uses bonded or piezoresistive strain gauges on a flexure element; gives **DC response** and is suitable for low-frequency vibration and static measurements. *(1 mark)*
2. **Quartz (Piezoelectric) Accelerometer** — uses a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz) that generates a charge proportional to the applied inertial force; has **wide bandwidth and self-generating** nature (no excitation needed). *(1 mark)*
3. **MEMS Accelerometer** — uses a microfabricated silicon proof mass with comb-finger capacitors; offers **miniature size, low cost, and on-chip signal conditioning**. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why cannot a quartz (piezoelectric) accelerometer measure a truly static (DC) acceleration of, say, $1g$ continuously?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A piezoelectric accelerometer generates an electric charge $Q$ only when the applied force is *changing*. Under truly static acceleration, the force is constant, so a constant charge is generated, but this charge immediately begins to **leak away** through the finite insulation resistance of the crystal, the cable, and the amplifier input. The high-pass cutoff frequency $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_f C_f)$ of the charge amplifier determines how slowly the input can change for the output to remain valid. Therefore, a quartz accelerometer is **fundamentally a high-pass device** and cannot measure steady-state (DC) accelerations such as gravity sustained over long periods. *(3 marks: 1 for static charge leakage, 1 for high-pass nature, 1 for the cutoff relation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When asked to *derive* the sensitivity of any accelerometer, students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** for:
> (1) Skipping the standard equation of motion $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -ma$ and going straight to the final result.
> (2) Forgetting to substitute $F = ma$ when transferring from force to acceleration.
> (3) For strain-gauge problems, omitting the gauge factor $G_F$ in the final bridge-output equation.
> (4) For piezoelectric problems, ignoring cable capacitance $C_c$ in $C_{total}$ — examiners specifically look for $C_{total} = C_p + C_c + C_a$.
> Always define every symbol; always box the final answer; always state units.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Strain-Gauge Accelerometer**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **strain-gauge-based accelerometer**. Derive an expression for its output voltage in terms of the input acceleration, beam dimensions, and material properties. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A strain-gauge accelerometer uses a steel cantilever ($E = 200 \text{ GPa}$) of length $L = 25 \text{ mm}$, width $b = 5 \text{ mm}$, and thickness $t = 1 \text{ mm}$. The proof mass is $m = 0.05 \text{ kg}$ and the bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$. The gauge factor is $G_F = 2.0$. Determine: (i) the static strain at the fixed end, (ii) the natural frequency, and (iii) the voltage sensitivity in mV/g. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:** A proof mass $m$ is rigidly fixed at the free end of a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$. Two active strain gauges are bonded to the top and bottom surfaces near the fixed end of the beam, and two dummy (temperature-compensating) gauges are placed on a non-strained section. The four gauges are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by $V_{ex}$. The entire assembly is enclosed in a rigid housing which is bolted to the vibrating body. *(2 marks)*

**Working:** When the housing is accelerated with $a$, an inertial force $F = ma$ acts on the proof mass. This force bends the cantilever, producing tensile strain on one surface and compressive strain on the other. The two active gauges experience equal and opposite strains, doubling the bridge output and automatically rejecting common-mode (temperature) effects. The output voltage is therefore proportional to the input acceleration. *(2 marks)*

**Derivation:** From the Euler-Bernoulli tip-load formula with $F = ma$:

$$y_{tip} = \frac{4 m L^3}{E b t^3} \, a$$

Maximum bending strain at the fixed end:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 m L}{E b t^2} \, a$$

Each active gauge changes resistance by $\Delta R / R = G_F \varepsilon$. For a full bridge with two active gauges, the differential output voltage is:

$$\boxed{\,V_o = V_{ex}\, G_F\, \varepsilon = \frac{6 m L\, V_{ex}\, G_F}{E b t^2} \, a\,}$$ *(3 marks for the derivation, including $F = ma$ substitution and final boxed result)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $E = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $L = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $b = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $t = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $m = 0.05 \text{ kg}$, $V_{ex} = 10 \text{ V}$, $G_F = 2.0$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Static strain at the fixed end** for an input acceleration $a = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (10^{-3})^2} \times 9.81$$

Calculate the denominator: $200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6} = 200 \times 5 \times 10^{9-3-6} = 1000 \times 10^0 = 1000 \text{ N}$.

Calculate the numerator: $6 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1000} \times 9.81 = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 73.575 \times 10^{-6} = 73.6 \,\mu\varepsilon$$

**Strain per unit g = 7.5 μe / g** (multiplying by $g$ cancels $g$ in numerator and denominator — so the strain per $g$ is $7.5 \times 10^{-6}$). *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Natural frequency:**

Cantilever stiffness:

$$k = \frac{E b t^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (10^{-3})^3}{4 \times (25 \times 10^{-3})^3}$$

$$= \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-3}}{6.25 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.0}{6.25 \times 10^{-5}} = 16000 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{16000}{0.05}} = \sqrt{320000} \approx 565.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{565.7}{2\pi} \approx 90 \text{ Hz}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Voltage sensitivity:**

$$S_V = \frac{V_o}{a} = \frac{6 m L\, V_{ex}\, G_F}{E b t^2} = \frac{6 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 2.0}{1000} = \frac{0.15}{1000} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V·s}^2/\text{m}$$

In mV/g: $S_V \times g = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.81 = 1.47 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/g} = 1.47 \text{ mV/g}$ *(2 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Quartz / MEMS Accelerometer**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **quartz (piezoelectric) accelerometer**. Derive expressions for (i) the generated charge, (ii) the charge sensitivity, and (iii) the output voltage across the load capacitance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a quartz crystal with $d_{33} = 2.3 \text{ pC/N}$ and a proof mass $m = 50 \text{ g}$. The crystal and cable present a total capacitance of $C_{total} = 1.2 \text{ nF}$. Calculate: (i) the charge generated for an input acceleration of $5g$, (ii) the charge sensitivity in pC/g, and (iii) the peak output voltage for $a = 5g$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:** A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a stack of piezoelectric discs (usually quartz or PZT) sandwiched between a base plate and a proof mass. A central preload stud/spring presses the mass against the crystal stack to ensure firm mechanical coupling. The whole assembly is enclosed in a sealed metal housing. The output is taken from electrodes plated on the two faces of the crystal stack. *(2 marks)*

**Working:** When the housing is accelerated, the inertial force $F = ma$ of the proof mass compresses the crystal. By the direct piezoelectric effect, this force generates an electric charge $Q$ on the electrodes proportional to the force. The charge is converted to a voltage by a charge amplifier. *(2 marks)*

**Derivation:**

(i) Direct piezoelectric equation in the 33-mode: $Q = d_{33}\,F = d_{33}\,m\,a$ *(1 mark)*

(ii) Charge sensitivity: $S_q = Q/a = d_{33}\,m$ *(1 mark)*

(iii) Output voltage: The crystal is modeled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with capacitance $C_{total} = C_p + C_c + C_a$. Therefore $V = Q/C_{total} = d_{33}\,m\,a/C_{total}$. *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\,V = \frac{d_{33}\,m\,a}{C_{total}}\,}$$ *(boxed final result, full marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $d_{33} = 2.3 \text{ pC/N} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N}$, $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05 \text{ kg}$, $C_{total} = 1.2 \text{ nF} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$, $a = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(i) Generated charge:**

Inertial force: $F = ma = 0.05 \times 49.05 = 2.4525 \text{ N}$

$$Q = d_{33} \times F = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.4525 = 5.641 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C} \approx 5.64 \text{ pC}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Charge sensitivity:**

$$S_q = d_{33} \times m = 2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.05 = 1.15 \times 10^{-13} \text{ C}\cdot\text{s}^2/\text{m}$$

Convert to pC/g: $S_q \times g = 1.15 \times 10^{-13} \times 9.81 = 1.128 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/g} \approx 1.13 \text{ pC/g}$ *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Peak output voltage:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_{total}} = \frac{5.641 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.70 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.70 \text{ mV}$$ *(2 marks)*

**Cross-check (using the formula):** $V = d_{33}\,m\,a/C_{total} = (2.3 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.05 \times 49.05)/(1.2 \times 10^{-9}) = (5.641 \times 10^{-12})/(1.2 \times 10^{-9}) = 4.70 \text{ mV}$ ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as the last-minute revision sheet before the KTU 2024 ESE exam.** Each bullet is a high-yield fact that examiners love to test.

- ✅ An accelerometer is a **transducer that converts acceleration into an electrical signal** using a seismic mass, spring, and damper.
- ✅ The governing equation of all accelerometers is the **mass-spring-damper equation**: $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\,a(t)$.
- ✅ The **natural frequency** is $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$. Useful measurement bandwidth is $\approx 0.3\,f_n$.
- ✅ The **damping ratio** is $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$; an optimal value of $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ gives maximally flat response.
- ✅ **Strain-gauge accelerometers** use bonded or piezoresistive gauges on a flexure; output voltage is $V_o = V_{ex} G_F \varepsilon = (6 m L V_{ex} G_F / E b t^2)\,a$; **DC responsive**, narrow bandwidth.
- ✅ **Quartz (piezoelectric) accelerometers** generate charge $Q = d_{ij} m a$; **self-generating**, **wide bandwidth (1 Hz – 10 kHz)**, but **cannot measure DC** (high-pass device).
- ✅ **MEMS accelerometers** use a microfabricated comb-finger capacitor; differential capacitance $\Delta C = 2 C_0 \Delta z / d_0$; **DC responsive**, low cost, tiny, with on-chip ASIC; commonly used in automotive, consumer electronics, IoT.
- ✅ Cable and amplifier capacitances ($C_c$, $C_a$) directly **affect** the voltage sensitivity of piezoelectric accelerometers.
- ✅ The unit $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ must be used consistently in numerical problems.
- ✅ Gauge factor $G_F$ relates relative resistance change to strain: $\Delta R / R = G_F \varepsilon$.
- ✅ Always **derive sensitivity from $F = ma$** — this is the most common step where students lose marks.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions**: small displacement ($\Delta z \ll d_0$), linear elastic beam, ideal Wheatstone bridge, negligible cable effects (where applicable).
- ✅ Three classes of frequency response: **DC measurement** (strain-gauge, MEMS), **dynamic measurement** (quartz), and **inertial reference** (high $\omega \gg \omega_n$, mass nearly stationary).
- ✅ The relationship $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ implies a **trade-off**: large $m$ → high sensitivity but low $f_n$; small $m$ → low sensitivity but high $f_n$.
- ✅ In KTU ESE 14-mark questions, **always include a functional diagram** (3–4 marks) before the derivation (8 marks) and the numerical (3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thickness Measurement - Micrometers, Laser Scanners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thickness Measurement - Micrometers, Laser Scanners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thickness Measurement?

**Thickness Measurement** is the metrological process of quantifying the linear dimension between two opposing surfaces of a solid body, typically expressed in micrometers ($\mu m$), millimeters ($mm$), or mils ($1\text{ mil} = 0.0254\text{ mm}$). In modern industrial practice, thickness is considered one of the most critical **dimensional parameters** because it directly governs the structural integrity, mass, thermal conductance, and tolerance stack-up of manufactured components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Tone):**
> *Thickness measurement is the comparative displacement determination between two reference points of a body using a calibrated sensing mechanism — mechanical, optical, or electromagnetic — to yield a numeric value within an acceptable uncertainty bound.*

## 1.2 Classification of Thickness Measurement Instruments

Instruments used for thickness gauging are broadly classified into two families based on the underlying physical principle:

| Family | Sub-Types | Contact Type | Typical Range |
|--------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Mechanical (Contact)** | Micrometer, Vernier Caliper (depth arm), Dial Indicator | **Contact** | $0.01\text{ mm}$ to $300\text{ mm}$ |
| **Optical (Non-Contact)** | Laser Triangulation, Time-of-Flight, Laser Interferometer, Confocal Chromatic | **Non-Contact** | $0.001\text{ mm}$ to $1000\text{ mm}$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spindle and Light" Story 🍕

Imagine a freshly baked **pizza base**. You can measure its thickness in two distinct ways:

- **The Spindle Story (Micrometer):** You press the pizza base gently between your **thumb and index finger**. The amount your thumb moves inward gives you the thickness. A micrometer does exactly this — it converts the *linear displacement* of a precisely machined screw into a *rotational count* readable on a thimble. Each "click" of the screw represents a known distance (the pitch).

- **The Light Story (Laser Scanner):** Now imagine the pizza base rolling on a conveyor. You shoot a **torchlight** at it. The angle at which the reflected beam hits a sensor tells you how thick the dough is. A laser scanner uses the same principle — a collimated laser beam is projected onto the surface, and the reflected light's position on a Position Sensitive Detector (PSD) is mathematically converted into a thickness reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Different Methods?**
> - **Micrometers** offer **high precision (±0.001 mm)** for static, laboratory-grade measurements.
> - **Laser scanners** offer **high speed (kHz range)** for in-process, on-line thickness monitoring in rolling mills, paper manufacturing, and polymer extrusion lines where the material cannot be touched.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- **Standard reference temperature:** $20\text{ °C}$ (per ISO 1 & ASME B89.1.6M)
- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of light (air, approx.):** $c_{air} \approx 2.997 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$
- **Refractive index of standard air:** $n \approx 1.00027$
- **Standard micrometer pitch (metric):** $P = 0.5\text{ mm}$ (typical) or $1.0\text{ mm}$
- **Standard thimble divisions:** $N = 50$
- **Standard micrometer least count:** $LC = 0.01\text{ mm} = 10\text{ }\mu m$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Micrometer — Operational Principle Unpacked

The micrometer (also known as a **screw gauge** or **micrometer screw gauge**) operates on the **Principle of the Screw-and-Nut Mechanism**, first formalized by William Gascoigne in the 17th century.

### 2.1.1 Functional Components

1. **Frame** — A rigid U-shaped body (usually cast iron or steel) that houses the anvil and the sleeve.
2. **Anvil** — A fixed measuring face on one side of the frame.
3. **Spindle** — A precisely threaded moving rod that advances toward the anvil.
4. **Sleeve (Barrel / Stock)** — A stationary cylinder with a linear scale (main scale) engraved along its length.
5. **Thimble** — A rotating cylindrical sleeve with $N$ equal divisions on its beveled edge.
6. **Ratchet Stop (Friction Thimble)** — Ensures a constant measuring pressure (typically $5$ to $10\text{ N}$), preventing operator-induced errors.
7. **Lock Nut** — Freezes the spindle position for transfer measurements.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind the Screw Mechanism

The genius of the micrometer lies in **mechanical advantage via the inclined plane**. A screw with a small axial pitch $P$ rotates many times to move axially only a tiny distance. This converts a small, easily readable **angular displacement** into a precisely controlled **linear displacement**.

$$
\text{Linear displacement} = \text{Angular displacement (in revolutions)} \times P
$$

### 2.1.3 Reading a Micrometer (Three-Step Process)

1. **Read the Main Scale (Sleeve):** Count the number of fully exposed millimeter divisions ($1.0\text{ mm}$) and half-millimeter marks ($0.5\text{ mm}$) visible on the sleeve.
2. **Read the Thimble Scale:** Note which thimble division aligns with the datum line on the sleeve.
3. **Sum them up:**
$$
\text{Total Reading} = \text{Main Scale Reading} + (\text{Thimble Division} \times LC)
$$

## 2.2 Types of Micrometers — Engineering Catalogue

| Type | Application | Special Feature |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **External Micrometer** | Wires, sheets, ball bearings | Standard anvil-spindle configuration |
| **Internal Micrometer (Tubular)** | Bore diameters, slot widths | Extension rods for deep bores |
| **Depth Micrometer** | Depth of slots, holes, recesses | Flat base replaces the anvil; spindle acts as depth rod |
| **Bench Micrometer** | High-accuracy gauge block comparison | Long throat depth |
| **Digit Micrometer** | SPC integration in industry | Electronic encoder with $\pm 0.001\text{ mm}$ resolution |

## 2.3 Errors in Micrometer Measurement

- **Zero Error:** Deflection from the true zero when anvil and spindle just touch.
  - **Positive zero error:** Thimble reading is *below* the datum line — subtract the error from the observed reading.
  - **Negative zero error:** Thimble reading is *above* the datum line — add the absolute value to the observed reading.
- **Anvil-Frame Distortion:** Excessive measuring force causes elastic deformation. Eliminated by the **ratchet stop**.
- **Thermal Expansion Error:** Mitigated by operating at **$20\text{ °C}$** per ISO 1.
- **Backlash Error:** Caused by thread wear; mitigated by always approaching the workpiece in the same direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** *"Define zero error and zero correction. A micrometer has a zero error of $+0.03\text{ mm}$. The reading taken is $12.57\text{ mm}$. Find the corrected reading."*
> **Correction:** Apply the negative of the zero error → $12.57 - 0.03 = 12.54\text{ mm}$.

## 2.4 Laser Scanners — Theoretical Foundation

Laser-based thickness measurement represents the **non-contact, high-speed frontier** of modern metrology. The dominant techniques are:

### 2.4.1 Laser Triangulation Method

A semiconductor laser diode (typically **$635\text{ nm}$ to $905\text{ nm}$** wavelength) projects a beam onto the target surface. The reflected beam is captured by a lens and focused onto a **Position Sensitive Detector (PSD)** or a **CMOS/CCD linear array**.

When the target moves by a distance $\Delta d$, the spot on the PSD shifts laterally by a proportional amount $\Delta x$. The geometry of the triangle formed by the laser, the target, and the detector yields:

$$
\Delta d = \frac{\Delta x \cdot L}{f \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

where $L$ is the standoff distance, $f$ is the focal length of the receiver lens, and $\theta$ is the angle between incident and reflected beams.

### 2.4.2 Time-of-Flight (ToF) Method

A short laser pulse is emitted, hits the target, and returns to a photodetector. The round-trip time $t$ is measured with sub-nanosecond precision.

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

For **thickness** measurement, two synchronized ToF sensors measure the top and bottom surfaces of the sheet simultaneously, and the difference gives the thickness.

### 2.4.3 Confocal Chromatic Method

White light is dispersed through a hyperchromatic lens assembly. Only one specific wavelength is in focus at the target surface and returns through a pinhole to a spectrometer. The detected wavelength directly maps to a calibrated distance. **Best for glossy, transparent, or mirror-like surfaces** like glass sheets.

### 2.4.4 Laser Interferometry (Michelson Configuration)

A coherent laser beam is split into a reference arm and a measurement arm. The interference fringes shift as the target moves; counting fringes gives displacement to within **$\lambda/2$** resolution (typically $316\text{ nm}$ for a He-Ne laser).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Pitch of screw thread | $P$ | mm | Distance traveled per revolution |
| 2 | Least Count (LC) | $LC = \dfrac{P}{N}$ | mm | $N$ = thimble divisions |
| 3 | Total Reading | $R = MSR + (TSR \times LC)$ | mm | MSR + Thimble contribution |
| 4 | Zero Correction | $R_{true} = R_{obs} - e_{zero}$ | mm | Subtract zero error |
| 5 | Triangulation Distance | $d = \dfrac{x \cdot L}{f \cdot \sin\theta}$ | mm | $x$ = PSD displacement |
| 6 | Time-of-Flight Distance | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | m | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$ |
| 7 | Thickness (two ToF sensors) | $T = d_1 - d_2$ | mm | Difference of two measurements |
| 8 | Interferometric Resolution | $\delta = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | nm | He-Ne: $\delta \approx 316\text{ nm}$ |
| 9 | Interferometric with N fringes | $\Delta d = N \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | nm | Each fringe = half-wavelength |
| 10 | Measurement Force Effect | $F_{meas} \le 10\text{ N}$ | N | Ratchet stop limits force |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Least Count of a Standard Metric Micrometer

**Given:**
- Pitch of the screw thread: $P = 0.5\text{ mm/rev}$
- Number of divisions on the thimble: $N = 50$

**Step 1:** Express the pitch in linear distance per single revolution.

$$
\text{In one full revolution, the spindle advances} = 0.5\text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Since the thimble is divided into $N$ equal parts, the linear distance advanced per *one thimble division* is:

$$
\text{Distance per division} = \frac{0.5\text{ mm}}{50} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-1}}{5 \times 10^{1}}\text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$
\frac{5 \times 10^{-1}}{5 \times 10^{1}} = 10^{-1-1} = 10^{-2}\text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Express the final value.

$$
LC = 0.01\text{ mm} = 10\text{ }\mu m
$$

> **Conclusion:** The micrometer can resolve a thickness variation as small as $\mathbf{10\text{ }\mu m}$ — roughly **one-tenth the diameter of a human hair (≈ 70 µm)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrected Micrometer Reading (Zero Error Compensation)

**Given:**
- Micrometer least count: $LC = 0.01\text{ mm}$
- Observed main scale reading: $MSR = 12.50\text{ mm}$ (i.e., twelve $1\text{ mm}$ divisions plus one $0.5\text{ mm}$ mark visible)
- Observed thimble scale reading: $TSR = 7$ divisions coinciding with the datum line
- Zero error: $e_0 = +0.03\text{ mm}$

**Step 1:** Compute the uncorrected (observed) total reading.

$$
R_{obs} = MSR + (TSR \times LC) = 12.50 + (7 \times 0.01)
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply and add.

$$
R_{obs} = 12.50 + 0.07 = 12.57\text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply zero correction. Since $e_0$ is **positive** (thimble reading is below the datum when the anvils are in contact), the correction is **subtractive**.

$$
R_{true} = R_{obs} - e_0
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and compute.

$$
R_{true} = 12.57 - 0.03 = 12.54\text{ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The **true thickness** of the workpiece is $\mathbf{12.54\text{ mm}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students wrongly *add* positive zero error instead of subtracting. Remember the mnemonic: **"Positive zero error → Subtract; Negative zero error → Add the magnitude."**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laser Triangulation Distance Computation

**Given:**
- Laser wavelength: $\lambda = 655\text{ nm}$ (red)
- Receiver lens focal length: $f = 25\text{ mm}$
- Triangulation angle (between incident and reflected beam axes): $\theta = 30°$
- Standoff distance (lens to nominal target plane): $L = 100\text{ mm}$
- Spot displacement on PSD: $x = 0.5\text{ mm}$ (target moved closer)

**Step 1:** Recall the fundamental triangulation relation.

$$
\Delta d = \frac{x \cdot L}{f \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\Delta d = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m})}{(25 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}) \cdot \sin(30°)}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $\sin(30°) = 0.5$.

$$
\Delta d = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(100 \times 10^{-3})}{(25 \times 10^{-3})(0.5)}
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the factor of $0.5$ in numerator and denominator.

$$
\Delta d = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{100}{25}
$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$
\Delta d = 4.0\text{ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The target surface moved by **$4.0\text{ mm}$** from its nominal position, producing a PSD displacement of $0.5\text{ mm}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time-of-Flight Thickness in a Rolling Mill

**Given:**
- Laser pulse round-trip time from top sensor: $t_1 = 6.667\text{ ns}$
- Laser pulse round-trip time from bottom sensor: $t_2 = 6.733\text{ ns}$
- Speed of light in air: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$

**Step 1:** Compute distance from top sensor to top surface.

$$
d_1 = \frac{c \cdot t_1}{2} = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (6.667 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator.

$$
2.998 \times 10^{8} \times 6.667 \times 10^{-9} = 2.998 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
= 19.987 \times 10^{-1} \approx 1.9987\text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by 2.

$$
d_1 = \frac{1.9987}{2} = 0.99935\text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute distance from bottom sensor to bottom surface.

$$
d_2 = \frac{c \cdot t_2}{2} = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (6.733 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerator computation.

$$
2.998 \times 6.733 \times 10^{-1} = 20.1857 \times 10^{-1} = 2.01857\text{ m}
$$

**Step 6:** Divide by 2.

$$
d_2 = \frac{2.01857}{2} = 1.009285\text{ m}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute thickness (the difference between the two measured stand-off distances, assuming the sensors are a fixed distance apart and the sheet is between them).

If the sensors are mounted such that the **total stand-off** is constant $D$ and the sheet is positioned centrally:

$$
T = (D - d_1) - (D - d_2) = d_2 - d_1
$$

$$
T = 1.009285 - 0.99935 = 0.009935\text{ m}
$$

**Step 8:** Convert to micrometers.

$$
T = 9.935 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m} \times 10^{6}\text{ }\mu m / m = 9935\text{ }\mu m
$$

> **Conclusion:** The sheet thickness is approximately **$9.94\text{ mm}$**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Laser Interferometer Fringe Count

**Given:**
- He-Ne laser wavelength: $\lambda = 632.8\text{ nm}$
- Number of fringes counted: $N = 1280$

**Step 1:** Recall that one full fringe shift corresponds to a half-wavelength displacement.

$$
\delta = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{632.8}{2} = 316.4\text{ nm}
$$

**Step 2:** Total displacement for $N$ fringes.

$$
\Delta d = N \cdot \delta = 1280 \times 316.4 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
\Delta d = 404,992 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m} = 4.04992 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Express in micrometers.

$$
\Delta d = 404.992\text{ }\mu m \approx 405\text{ }\mu m
$$

> **Conclusion:** The measured displacement is **$≈ 405\text{ }\mu m$**, with sub-nanometer theoretical resolution available through phase-shift interpolation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Micrometer Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    C --> E[Thimble Circular Scale: 50 Divisions]
    D --> F[Operator Sums Both Readings]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Apply Zero Correction?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Subtract or Add Zero Error]
    G -- No --> I[Final Reading Reported]
    H --> I

```


## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Thickness Sensor

```mermaid

    Q1{Is the surface soft, hot, or moving?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need sub-micron accuracy?}

    Q2 -- No --> C{Is the surface transparent or glossy?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Confocal Chromatic Sensor]
    C -- No --> E{Need measurement range more than 100 mm?}

    E -- No --> G[Use Laser Triangulation Sensor]
```

## 4.4 Reading a Micrometer — Schematic Label Map

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define least count of a micrometer. A metric micrometer has a pitch of $0.5\text{ mm}$ and $50$ divisions on its thimble. Calculate its least count.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** *The least count (LC) of a micrometer is the smallest linear displacement that can be read directly on the instrument, given by the ratio of the screw pitch to the number of thimble divisions.*

**Computation:**

$$
LC = \frac{P}{N} = \frac{0.5\text{ mm}}{50} = 0.01\text{ mm} = 10\text{ }\mu m
$$

✅ **Marks Awarded:** Definition (1M) + Formula (1M) + Numerical substitution & answer (1M).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between contact and non-contact methods of thickness measurement. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Contact Method | Non-Contact Method |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Sensing Principle** | Mechanical probe touches surface | Light beam reflects from surface |
| **Examples** | Micrometer, Vernier depth gauge | Laser triangulation, Interferometer |
| **Risk of Surface Damage** | Possible (soft materials) | None |
| **Speed** | Slow, operator-dependent | Fast (kHz sampling rates) |
| **Typical Accuracy** | $\pm 1\text{ }\mu m$ | $\pm 0.1\text{ }\mu m$ (interferometer) |
| **Application** | Workshop, QC labs | Hot rolling mills, paper industry |

✅ **Marks Awarded:** Tabulated distinction (2M) + one valid example each (1M).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice Instruction:** *Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of an **external micrometer**. Explain the terms *pitch*, *thimble divisions*, and *least count*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A micrometer reads $25.75\text{ mm}$ when measuring a shaft. The instrument has a positive zero error of $+0.02\text{ mm}$ and a least count of $0.01\text{ mm}$. Determine:
  (i) The corrected diameter.
  (ii) The percentage error if the true diameter (from a gauge block) is $25.70\text{ mm}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📌 Model Solution for (a)

1. **Construction (3 Marks):** Frame, anvil, spindle, sleeve, thimble, ratchet, lock nut — each component explained with its function.
2. **Working Principle (2 Marks):** The screw-nut mechanism converts rotational motion into linear motion. One full rotation moves the spindle axially by the pitch $P = 0.5\text{ mm}$.
3. **Definitions (2 Marks):**
   - **Pitch:** Distance moved per revolution.
   - **Thimble division:** One of the 50 equal marks on the thimble bevel.
   - **Least count:** $LC = P/N = 0.01\text{ mm}$.

#### 📌 Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Corrected Diameter:**

$$
D_{corr} = D_{obs} - e_0 = 25.75 - 0.02 = 25.73\text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Error:**

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert D_{true} - D_{corr}\vert}{D_{true}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{\vert 25.70 - 25.73 \vert}{25.70} \times 100 = \frac{0.03}{25.70} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.1167\% \approx 0.117\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:**
> - Many students forget to **subtract** positive zero error (loses 1 mark).
> - Percentage error formula is often written without absolute value, leading to a negative percentage (loses 1 mark).
> - Always specify the unit (mm) and round to **3 decimal places** at most.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **laser triangulation method** of thickness measurement with a block diagram. Derive the relationship between target displacement and PSD spot shift. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A laser time-of-flight sensor measures the distance to a sheet of metal. The measured round-trip times to the top and bottom surfaces are $t_1 = 5.000\text{ ns}$ and $t_2 = 5.045\text{ ns}$ respectively. The speed of light in air is $3 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$. Calculate:
  (i) The distance from the sensor to the top surface.
  (ii) The thickness of the sheet. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📌 Model Solution for (a)

1. **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Laser source → projection lens → target → reflected beam → receiver lens → PSD → signal processor → display.
2. **Principle (2 Marks):** Triangulation uses geometric similarity. As the target moves, the reflected spot shifts on the PSD; this shift is proportional to displacement.
3. **Derivation (2 Marks):** From the similar triangles in the optical setup,

$$
\frac{\Delta d}{L} = \frac{x}{f \cdot \sin\theta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta d = \frac{x \cdot L}{f \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

#### 📌 Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Distance to top surface:**

$$
d_1 = \frac{c \cdot t_1}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (5.000 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

$$
d_1 = \frac{1.500}{2} = 0.750\text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Distance to bottom surface:**

$$
d_2 = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (5.045 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = \frac{1.5135}{2} = 0.75675\text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Thickness:**

$$
T = d_2 - d_1 = 0.75675 - 0.750 = 0.00675\text{ m} = 6.75\text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common mistake:** Dividing by $c$ instead of multiplying — students write $d = c/t$ (loses 1 mark).
> - **Unit conversion:** Students often leave the answer in $m$ when the question implicitly expects $mm$ (loses 0.5 marks).
> - **Diagram omission:** Without the labeled block diagram in part (a), expect a loss of 2 marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Micrometer LC** = $P/N$; standard metric value is **$0.01\text{ mm}$**.
- ✅ **Reading = MSR + (TSR × LC)** — always sum, never multiply incorrectly.
- ✅ **Zero error sign:** Positive → subtract; Negative → add magnitude.
- ✅ **Ratchet stop** is critical for eliminating operator-induced force variation.
- ✅ **Triangulation formula:** $\Delta d = \dfrac{x \cdot L}{f \cdot \sin\theta}$ — derived from similar triangles.
- ✅ **Time-of-flight:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ — remember the factor of 2 (round-trip).
- ✅ **Interferometric resolution:** $\delta = \lambda/2$ — each fringe = half-wavelength displacement.
- ✅ **Confocal chromatic sensors** are preferred for transparent / glossy surfaces (glass, polished metals).
- ✅ **Industry applications:** Rolling mills, paper production, polymer film extrusion, semiconductor wafer thickness control.
- ✅ **Reference temperature for metrology:** **$20\text{ °C}$** (per ISO 1).
- ✅ **Pitch direction always approaching** the workpiece to avoid **backlash error**.
- ✅ **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$ — memorize for ToF problems.
- ✅ **Digit micrometers** integrate with **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** systems for Industry 4.0 compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position, Location and Altitude Measurement - MEMS Devices, Global Positioning System (GPS), Range Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Position, Location and Altitude Measurement - MEMS Devices, Global Positioning System (GPS), Range Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Position, Location, and Altitude Measurement — The Big Picture

**Position** is the absolute spatial coordinates of an object in a defined reference frame (X, Y, Z). **Location** refers to the geographic identification on Earth's surface (latitude, longitude), while **Altitude** is the vertical elevation above a reference datum (typically Mean Sea Level, MSL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic integrates **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, **Satellite-based navigation (GPS)**, and **Range (distance) Measurement** — the three pillars of modern spatial awareness used in drones, autonomous vehicles, smartphones, and industrial automation.

---

## 1.2 MEMS Devices for Position & Altitude

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition
**Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** are miniaturized mechanical and electromechanical elements fabricated on silicon substrates using semiconductor batch-processing techniques (typically 1 µm – 100 µm feature sizes). For position measurement, MEMS devices include **accelerometers** (detect linear acceleration along an axis), **gyroscopes** (detect angular rate), and **pressure sensors** (used as barometric altimeters).

> [!NOTE]
> A **MEMS accelerometer** in your smartphone is essentially a tiny suspended proof mass (a few micrograms) held by compliant flexures. When the phone tilts or accelerates, the mass deflects, and this deflection is converted into an electrical signal by **capacitive**, **piezoresistive**, or **piezoelectric** transduction.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy
> Think of MEMS as a **"seesaw on a microchip."** Imagine a tiny diving board (the proof mass) anchored at one end, floating inside your phone. When the phone moves, the board bends. Sensors detect this bending — that's how your phone knows whether it's in portrait, landscape, or falling! Multiply that by three orthogonal axes (X, Y, Z) and you get **3D spatial awareness**.

### 1.2.3 Barometric Altitude Sensing
Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude according to the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) model**. A MEMS pressure sensor (e.g., Bosch BMP280) measures absolute pressure, which is then converted to altitude. Standard sea-level pressure = **1013.25 hPa**.

---

## 1.3 Global Positioning System (GPS)

### 1.3.1 Formal Definition
The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system owned by the **United States Space Force (USSF)**, consisting of a constellation of at least **24 satellites** in **6 orbital planes** at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**, providing 24/7 geospatial positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) information to any user with a GPS receiver on or near Earth.

### 1.3.2 Intuitive Analogy
> GPS works exactly like a **"cosmic triangulation game."** Imagine you're lost in a forest. If you know the exact distance to three lighthouses and where each lighthouse is, you can pinpoint your location by drawing three circles — they intersect at one point. Replace lighthouses with satellites and forest with Earth's surface — that's GPS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** A GPS receiver calculates its position by measuring the **time-of-flight (TOF)** of radio signals from at least **4 satellites**. Since radio waves travel at the speed of light (**c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**), even a 1 microsecond timing error leads to a 300 m position error. Hence, **atomic clocks** on satellites are critical.

### 1.3.3 GPS Segments
| Segment | Description |
|---|---|
| **Space Segment** | 24+ satellites broadcasting ranging codes & navigation data |
| **Control Segment** | Ground stations monitoring & correcting satellite orbits/clocks |
| **User Segment** | GPS receivers (handheld, vehicle-mounted, smartphone) |

---

## 1.4 Range Measurement

### 1.4.1 Formal Definition
**Range** is the **distance** between a sensor and a target object. Range measurement techniques include **Ultrasonic** (40–200 kHz acoustic pulses), **Laser/LIDAR** (optical pulses), and **Radar** (RF/microwave pulses) — collectively known as **RADAR/LIDAR/SONAR** family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Range measurement is the foundation of obstacle detection in **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)**, robotic vacuum cleaners (lidar), automotive parking sensors (ultrasonic), and military surveillance (radar).

### 1.4.2 Intuitive Analogy
> Range measurement is like a **bat's echolocation** — the sensor sends out a wave (sound, light, or radio), waits for it to bounce back, and uses the round-trip time to compute distance. Bats use sound; engineers use sound (ultrasonic), light (laser), or radio (radar).

### 1.4.3 Standard Metrics
- Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **343 m/s**
- Speed of light in vacuum: **299,792,458 m/s** (exact, by definition)
- Speed of radio waves (≈ speed of light): **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MEMS Accelerometer — Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Capacitive Sensing Mechanism
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** suspended by compliant springs. Under acceleration *a*, the mass experiences an inertial force *F = ma*, displacing it by distance *x*:

$$x = \frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

where *k* is the spring constant of the flexure.

This displacement *x* changes the gap of a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{d - x} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{d}\left(1 + \frac{x}{d}\right)$$

The differential capacitance change is:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \cdot A \cdot x}{d^2}$$

This ΔC is measured by a charge amplifier, yielding an output voltage proportional to acceleration.

### 2.1.2 Why MEMS for Position?
- **Miniaturization:** Sensor size typically 2 mm × 2 mm
- **Low power:** μW to mW range
- **Cost:** < $1 per axis in mass production
- **Integration:** Co-fabricated with CMOS signal conditioning

> [!NOTE]
> **Double Integration for Position:** A single accelerometer measures acceleration. To get velocity, integrate once; to get position, integrate twice. This is why **Inertial Navigation Systems (INS)** use accelerometers combined with gyroscopes (IMU — Inertial Measurement Unit).

---

## 2.2 Global Positioning System (GPS) — Mathematical Framework

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
Each GPS satellite continuously broadcasts a **pseudorandom noise (PRN) code** and its orbital position (ephemeris). The GPS receiver measures the **pseudorange** to each satellite:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i})$$

where *t_{rx}* is the receiver timestamp, *t_{tx}* is the satellite transmit timestamp, and *c* is the speed of light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The measured range is called a **"pseudorange"** because it contains a clock-bias error *b* (the receiver's clock is not synchronized with atomic satellite clocks):

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} + c \cdot b$$

This introduces **4 unknowns** *(x, y, z, b)* — hence the need for at least **4 satellites**.

### 2.2.2 Dilution of Precision (DOP)
**DOP** quantifies how satellite geometry affects positioning accuracy:

$$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$$

where *H* is the geometry matrix. Lower DOP = better accuracy.

---

## 2.3 Range Measurement Techniques

### 2.3.1 Time-of-Flight (TOF) Equation
All range measurement methods (ultrasonic, laser, radar) use TOF:

$$R = \frac{c_{medium} \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for round-trip travel (sensor → target → sensor).

### 2.3.2 Ultrasonic Range Sensor
- Frequency: **40 kHz** (most common, e.g., HC-SR04)
- Beam width: **~15°**
- Range: **2 cm – 400 cm**
- Speed of sound (temperature-corrected):

$$c_{air} = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \;\; (\text{m/s, with } T \text{ in } ^\circ C)$$

### 2.3.3 Laser/LIDAR Range Sensor
- Wavelength: **905 nm** or **1550 nm**
- Range: **up to 200 m**
- Beam divergence: **< 0.5°**
- Uses pulsed time-of-flight or phase-shift detection:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f}$$

where *φ* is phase shift, *f* is modulation frequency.

### 2.3.4 RADAR (Radio Detection and Ranging)
- Frequency bands: **X-band (8–12 GHz)**, **K-band (18–27 GHz)**
- Uses Doppler shift for velocity and TOF for range
- Range equation:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Topic** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MEMS proof mass displacement | $x = ma/k$ | *m*: kg, *a*: m/s², *k*: N/m | Capacitive readout |
| Differential capacitance | $\Delta C = 2\varepsilon_0 A x / d^2$ | *A*: m², *d*: m, *x*: m | Parallel-plate |
| Altitude from pressure (ISA) | $h = 44330 \cdot (1 - (P/P_0)^{1/5.255})$ | *P*, *P₀*: Pa | Standard atmosphere |
| GPS Pseudorange | $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + cb$ | Distances in m, *b*: s | Needs ≥ 4 satellites |
| Speed of sound in air | $c = 331.3 + 0.606 T$ | *T*: °C, *c*: m/s | Linearized at 0°C |
| TOF Range | $R = c \cdot t_{TOF} / 2$ | *c*: medium velocity, *t*: s | Round-trip |
| LIDAR Phase-shift | $R = c \phi / (4\pi f)$ | *φ*: rad, *f*: Hz | CW modulation |
| GPS Error budget (C/A code) | ~3–5 m (no SA) | Civilian accuracy | SA disabled since 2000 |
| DOP | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{tr}((H^TH)^{-1})}$ | Dimensionless | Geometry quality |
| Doppler velocity | $v = f_d \cdot c / (2 f_0)$ | *f_d*: Hz, *f_0*: Hz | Radar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations drive:
> - **Autonomous vehicles** (GPS + LIDAR + IMU fusion via Kalman filter)
> - **Surveying & geodetic mapping** (RTK-GPS, cm-level accuracy)
> - **Aerospace navigation** (INS/GPS coupled systems)
> - **Industrial robotics** (MEMS IMU for motion tracking)
> - **Smart agriculture** (GPS-guided tractors with cm accuracy)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Altitude from Barometric Pressure (Hypsometric Equation)

### 3.1.1 Start with Hydrostatic Equation
The pressure gradient in a static atmosphere is governed by:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho \cdot g$$

where *ρ* is air density and *g = 9.81 m/s²* is gravitational acceleration.

### 3.1.2 Apply Ideal Gas Law
$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$

where *M* = 0.02896 kg/mol (molar mass of air), *R* = 8.314 J/(mol·K) (universal gas constant), *T* = absolute temperature in Kelvin.

Substituting:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\frac{P \cdot M \cdot g}{R \cdot T}$$

### 3.1.3 Separate Variables and Integrate
$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{M g}{R T} \int_0^h dh$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{M g h}{R T}$$

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = e^{-Mgh/(RT)}$$

### 3.1.4 Solve for Altitude
$$\boxed{h = \frac{R T}{M g} \ln\left(\frac{P_0}{P}\right)}$$

This is the **Hypsometric equation**. With numerical values *R = 8.314*, *M = 0.02896*, *g = 9.81*, at *T = 288.15 K* (15°C):

$$h \approx 44330 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026}\right] \text{ meters}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: GPS Position from 4 Pseudoranges

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
We have **4 satellite position vectors** (known from ephemeris data):
- Satellite 1: $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$
- Satellite 2: $(x_2, y_2, z_2)$
- Satellite 3: $(x_3, y_3, z_3)$
- Satellite 4: $(x_4, y_4, z_4)$

And 4 measured pseudoranges: $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3, \rho_4$.

Unknowns: $(x, y, z, b)$ where *b* is the receiver clock bias.

### 3.2.2 Write 4 Nonlinear Equations
$$\rho_1 = \sqrt{(x-x_1)^2 + (y-y_1)^2 + (z-z_1)^2} + c b$$

$$\rho_2 = \sqrt{(x-x_2)^2 + (y-y_2)^2 + (z-z_2)^2} + c b$$

$$\rho_3 = \sqrt{(x-x_3)^2 + (y-y_3)^2 + (z-z_3)^2} + c b$$

$$\rho_4 = \sqrt{(x-x_4)^2 + (y-y_4)^2 + (z-z_4)^2} + c b$$

### 3.2.3 Linearize Using Taylor Expansion
Let $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$ be an initial guess. Expand each equation to first order:

$$\rho_i \approx R_{i,0} + c b_0 + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z}\Delta z + c \Delta b$$

where:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x} = \frac{-(x_i - x_0)}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(y_i - y_0)}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z} = \frac{-(z_i - z_0)}{R_{i,0}}$$

and $R_{i,0} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_0)^2 + (y_i - y_0)^2 + (z_i - z_0)^2}$.

### 3.2.4 Form the Linear System
Define the **geometry matrix** *H*:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_0 - x_1}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_1}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_1}{R_{1,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_2}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_2}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_2}{R_{2,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_3}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_3}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_3}{R_{3,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{x_0 - x_4}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{y_0 - y_4}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{z_0 - z_4}{R_{4,0}} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Define residual vector: $\Delta \rho_i = \rho_i - R_{i,0} - c b_0$

Then:

$$H \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c \Delta b \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2.5 Solve via Least Squares
$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c \Delta b \end{bmatrix} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update: $x = x_0 + \Delta x$, $y = y_0 + \Delta y$, $z = z_0 + \Delta z$, $b = b_0 + \Delta b$. **Iterate** until convergence (typically 5–10 iterations).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TOF Range Calculation — Worked Numerical Example

### 3.3.1 Problem
An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of **5.82 ms** at 25°C. Compute the range.

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Speed of Sound at 25°C
$$c = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 25 = 331.3 + 15.15 = 346.45 \text{ m/s}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Apply TOF Equation
$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_{TOF}}{2} = \frac{346.45 \times 5.82 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Compute
$$R = \frac{2.0163}{2} = 1.008 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: The target is approximately 1.0 m away.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Position from Double-Integration of Accelerometer

### 3.4.1 Setup
A MEMS accelerometer outputs acceleration $a(t)$ along one axis. Initial velocity = $v_0$, initial position = $x_0$.

### 3.4.2 Integrate to Get Velocity
$$v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t a(\tau) d\tau$$

### 3.4.3 Integrate Again to Get Position
$$\boxed{x(t) = x_0 + v_0 t + \int_0^t \int_0^\tau a(\sigma) d\sigma \, d\tau}$$

### 3.4.4 In Discrete Time (Digital Implementation)
For a sampling period $T_s$:

$$v_n = v_{n-1} + a_n T_s$$

$$x_n = x_{n-1} + v_n T_s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** This is an **open-loop** integration. Any small bias in the accelerometer (e.g., 0.01 m/s²) leads to position drift of $\frac{1}{2} a t^2$ — grows quadratically with time. Hence, MEMS-INS is **always fused with GPS** (Kalman filter) to bound drift.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **MEMS IMU** | **GPS** | **Ultrasonic** | **LIDAR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range / Coverage | Local (m) | Global (Earth) | 0.02 – 4 m | 0.1 – 200 m |
| Accuracy | Drift over time | 3–5 m (civilian) | ±1 cm | ±1 mm |
| Update Rate | 100–1000 Hz | 1–10 Hz | 10–40 Hz | 5–20 Hz |
| Cost (sensor) | $1 – $50 | $5 – $50 | $2 – $10 | $50 – $5000 |
| Power | mW | mW (rx) | 10s of mW | W |
| Affected by | Bias, noise | Atmosphere, jamming | Temp, soft surfaces | Rain, dust |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GPS System Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPACE["SPACE SEGMENT - 24+ SATELLITES AT 20200 km"]
        SAT1["SAT 1 L1 1575.42 MHz"]
        SAT2["SAT 2 L1 1575.42 MHz"]
        SAT3["SAT 3 L1 1575.42 MHz"]
        SAT4["SAT 4 L1 1575.42 MHz"]
    end
    
    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL SEGMENT - GROUND STATIONS"]
        MCS["MASTER CONTROL STATION"]
        MON["MONITOR STATIONS"]
        ANT["GROUND ANTENNAS"]
        MON --> MCS
        MCS --> ANT
    end
    
    subgraph USER["USER SEGMENT - GPS RECEIVER"]
        ANT2["GPS ANTENNA"]
        LNA["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER"]
        CORR["CORRELATOR - 4 CHANNELS"]
        SOLVER["POSITION SOLVER - LEAST SQUARES"]
        DISP["DISPLAY - LAT LON ALT"]
        ANT2 --> LNA
        LNA --> CORR
        CORR --> SOLVER
        SOLVER --> DISP
    end
    
    SAT1 -.Radio Signal.-> ANT2
    SAT2 -.Radio Signal.-> ANT2
    SAT3 -.Radio Signal.-> ANT2
    SAT4 -.Radio Signal.-> ANT2
    ANT -.Uplink Corrections.-> SAT1
    ANT -.Uplink Corrections.-> SAT2
```

---

## 4.2 MEMS Accelerometer — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APPLIED ACCELERATION a"] --> B["PROOF MASS m"]
    B --> C["SUSPENSION SPRINGS k"]
    B --> D["DISPLACEMENT x = ma/k"]
    D --> E["DIFFERENTIAL CAPACITOR C1 C2"]
    E --> F["CHARGE AMPLIFIER"]
    F --> G["DEMODULATOR AND FILTER"]
    G --> H["ADC"]
    H --> I["DIGITAL OUTPUT - m/s2"]
    J["TEMPERATURE SENSOR"] --> G
    K["VOLTAGE REFERENCE"] --> F
```

---

## 4.3 Range Measurement — TOF Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PULSE GENERATOR - TX"] --> A["EMITTER - ULTRASONIC OR LASER OR RADAR"]
    A -.Wave propagates.-> B["TARGET OBJECT"]
    B -.Wave reflects.-> C["RECEIVER - SAME OR SEPARATE APERTURE"]
    C --> D["TIMING CIRCUIT - DETECTS ROUND TRIP"]
    D --> E["COMPUTE tTOF"]
    E --> F["APPLY FORMULA R = c tTOF / 2"]
    F --> G["DISTANCE OUTPUT R meters"]
    H["TEMPERATURE SENSOR"] -.Compensate c.-> F
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion Architecture — GPS + IMU + LIDAR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["MEMS IMU - 100 Hz"] --> K1["KALMAN FILTER FUSION ENGINE"]
    G["GPS RECEIVER - 1 Hz"] --> K1
    L["LIDAR - 10 Hz"] --> K1
    U["ULTRASONIC - 20 Hz"] --> K1
    K1 --> O1["POSITION x y z"]
    K1 --> O2["VELOCITY vx vy vz"]
    K1 --> O3["ATTITUDE ROLL PITCH YAW"]
    K1 --> O4["CLOCK BIAS b"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern autonomous systems (self-driving cars, drones, robotics) **never rely on a single sensor**. Sensor fusion via **Kalman filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** combines the strengths of GPS (no drift, global) with IMU (high rate, no GPS dependency) and LIDAR/ultrasonic (local precision, obstacle detection).
