### 4.3 Operational Flow — Geiger Discharge Pulse Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Radiation Particle] --> B[Primary Ionization in Argon]
    B --> C[Electron Accelerated to Anode]
    C --> D[Secondary Ionization Townsend Avalanche]
    D --> E[UV Photon Emission]
    E --> F[Cathode Photoemission]
    F --> G[Townsend Avalanche Spreads Along Wire]
    G --> H[Positive Ion Space Charge Forms]
    H --> I[Electric Field Below Avalanche Threshold]
    I --> J[Geiger Pulse Generated at Anode]
    J --> K[Quenching Agent Absorbs UV]
    K --> L[Space Charge Dissipates]

```

flowchart TD
    A[Gamma Photon Enters Crystal] --> B{Interaction Type}

    C --> F[Electron Excites Activator Sites]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Visible Photon Emission]

    H --> I[Photoelectron Toward First Dynode]
    I --> J[Secondary Emission Cascade]
    J --> K[Anode Pulse Proportional to Energy]
    K --> L[MCA Energy Analysis]

```

flowchart LR

        GM4 --> GM5[Same Amplitude Every Time]
    end
    subgraph SC[Scintillation Counter Chain]
        SC1[Radiation] --> SC2[Crystal Fluorescence]
        SC2 --> SC3[Photocathode Electron]

        SC4 --> SC5[Amplitude Proportional to Energy]
    end
```

### 4.6 Plateau Characteristic Curve — GM Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Voltage Axis 0 to 1000 V] --> B[Region I Recombination]
    B --> C[Region II Ion Chamber Saturation]
    C --> D[Region III Proportional]
    D --> E[Threshold Voltage VG]
    E --> F[Region IV Geiger Plateau]
    F --> G[Continuous Discharge]
    F --> H[Recommended Operating Point Plateau Midpoint]
```

### 4.7 Coordinate Sketch — Pulse Amplitude vs. Applied Voltage (Text Schematic)

The characteristic curve of a gas-filled detector partitions into four operating zones, traversed by progressively increasing the applied voltage across the anode-cathode gap:

```
Pulse Amplitude
   ^                                      
   |                                  **** Region IV: Geiger (saturated)
   |                            ******
   |                       ****  <-- Plateau region
   |                  *****      Recommended operating voltage
   |              ****           (mid-plateau)
   |         *****               Region III: Proportional (slope ~V^n)
   |       ***                  
   |      **                    
   |    **                       Region II: Ion Chamber (saturated)
   |  **                         (constant pulse per ionization)
   | *                           
   |*  Region I: Recombination    
   |*   (ions recombine before collection)
   +------------------------------------------> Applied Voltage (V)
        V1    V2        V3     VG           Vbreakdown
```

**Reading the curve:**
- **V1:** Threshold where saturation begins (ion chamber region).
- **V2 to V3:** Proportional region — pulse amplitude rises with voltage and is proportional to radiation energy.
- **VG:** Geiger threshold — onset of self-propagating discharge.
- **Plateau:** From VG to Vbreakdown — all pulses have nearly equal amplitude (ideal for counting).
- **Vbreakdown:** Continuous discharge — tube damage likely.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

**Q1. Define the following terms with respect to radiation measurement:**
**(a) Activity, (b) Half-life, (c) Mean life.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Activity (1 mark):** Activity of a radioactive sample is defined as the number of nuclear disintegrations occurring per unit time. Mathematically, $A = -dN/dt = \lambda N$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant and $N$ is the number of undecayed nuclei. The SI unit is **Becquerel (Bq)** where $1 \text{ Bq} = 1 \text{ disintegration/second}$. The legacy unit is the **Curie (Ci)** where $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$.

**(b) Half-life (1 mark):** Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) is the time required for the activity (or number of undecayed nuclei) of a radioactive sample to reduce to exactly half its initial value. It is related to the decay constant by $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda = 0.693 / \lambda$.

**(c) Mean life (1 mark):** Mean life ($\tau_{\text{mean}}$) is the average lifetime of a radioactive atom before it decays. It equals the reciprocal of the decay constant: $\tau_{\text{mean}} = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2} / 0.693 \approx 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$.

---

**Q2. Explain the term "quenching" as applied to a Geiger-Muller counter. Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Quenching is the process of terminating the Geiger discharge (self-propagating Townsend avalanche) immediately after a pulse has been produced, so that the detector can become sensitive to the next incoming radiation event. It is achieved either by adding a **quenching gas** (halogen such as Br₂, Cl₂, or an organic vapor such as ethanol/isobutane) inside the tube, or by using an **external electronic quenching circuit** that momentarily reduces the anode voltage below the discharge threshold after each pulse.

**Why Essential (1.5 marks):** Without quenching, the UV photons emitted during the avalanche would continuously re-trigger secondary avalanches, and the discharge would persist as a continuous arc. This would: (i) prevent detection of subsequent radiation events (permanent paralysis), (ii) cause excessive power dissipation and overheating, and (iii) severely shorten tube life. Quenching restores the detector to its sensitive state within the **dead time** (typically 50–500 µs), allowing sequential event counting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern with Module-Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Full-spectrum question covering all three sub-topics*

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Geiger-Muller counter**. Discuss its characteristic plateau curve and explain how the operating voltage is selected. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Describe the construction and working of a **scintillation counter** with the help of a block diagram. Compare its performance with that of a GM counter. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (2 marks):** A GM counter consists of:

- A **cylindrical cathode** (stainless steel or copper tube), which also acts as the radiation entry window.
- A **central anode wire** (fine tungsten wire, ~0.1 mm diameter) stretched along the cylinder axis.
- A **gas filling** at low pressure (~0.1 atm): typically **Argon** as the ionizing medium, plus a small amount of **halogen** (Br₂ or Cl₂) as the quenching agent.
- **Insulating end seals** (glass/ceramic) with a thin **entrance window** (mica, ~1–2 mg/cm²) for entry of $\alpha$ and low-energy $\beta$ particles.
- A **load resistor** ($R_L \approx 1$–$10 \, \text{M}\Omega$) connected between the anode and the high-voltage supply ($V_0 \approx 300$–$1000 \, \text{V}$).

**Working (3 marks):**

1. **Primary Ionization:** The incoming radiation enters through the window and ionizes an Argon atom, producing one ion pair: $\text{Ar} \rightarrow \text{Ar}^+ + e^-$.
2. **Townsend Avalanche:** The free electron is accelerated toward the central anode. Near the thin anode wire, the radial electric field $E(r) = V_0 / [r \ln(b/a)]$ is very intense. The electron gains sufficient kinetic energy to cause secondary ionization by collision, producing an **electron avalanche**.
3. **UV Photon Emission and Cathode Photoemission:** Excited Ar atoms emit UV photons, which strike the cathode and release additional electrons via the photoelectric effect. This generates a **secondary avalanche** that propagates along the entire anode length, converting the localized primary event into a continuous **Geiger discharge**.
4. **Space Charge Formation:** Slow positive Ar⁺ ions accumulate around the anode, forming a positive space-charge sheath that reduces the local field below the avalanche threshold.
5. **Pulse Formation:** The sudden collection of electrons at the anode produces a large voltage drop across $R_L$, giving an output pulse of ~1–10 V.
6. **Quenching:** The halogen (or organic vapor) absorbs UV photons and neutralizes ions by charge exchange, terminating the discharge.

**Plateau Curve and Operating Point Selection (2 marks):** As the applied voltage is increased from zero, the detector passes through four regions (recombination, ion chamber, proportional, Geiger). In the **Geiger region**, the pulse count rate remains nearly constant over a range of applied voltage — this flat portion is called the **plateau**. The **operating voltage** is chosen at the **midpoint of the plateau** (typically 30–80 V above the Geiger threshold $V_G$) to ensure stable operation with minimal sensitivity to supply voltage fluctuations. The plateau should have a slope of $\leq 0.1\%$ per volt and a length of at least 100–200 V for a good quality tube.

---

### Model Answer to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Construction (2 marks):** A scintillation counter consists of three main sub-systems:

1. **Scintillator:** A solid crystal (most commonly **NaI doped with Thallium — NaI(Tl)**) that emits visible/near-UV photons when struck by ionizing radiation. NaI(Tl) is hygroscopic and must be hermetically sealed in an aluminum housing with a transparent window.
2. **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT):** A vacuum tube consisting of a **photocathode** (e.g., Sb-Cs bialkali), a series of **dynodes** (typically 10–14 stages), and an **anode collector**. Each dynode is held at progressively higher potential via a resistor divider chain.
3. **Signal Processing Electronics:** Preamplifier, linear amplifier, discriminator, and counter (or multichannel analyzer for spectroscopy).

**Working (3 marks):**

1. **Radiation Interaction:** A gamma photon interacts in the scintillator via photoelectric absorption, Compton scattering, or pair production, depositing its energy in the crystal lattice.
2. **Scintillation:** The energy excites electrons at Thallium activator sites. De-excitation produces isotropic visible photons (~3 eV each) with a characteristic decay time of ~230 ns.
3. **Photocathode Conversion:** Photons reaching the photocathode eject photoelectrons via the external photoelectric effect.
4. **Electron Multiplication:** Photoelectrons are accelerated to the first dynode (held at ~100–200 V), where each ejects ~4–10 secondary electrons (secondary emission ratio $\delta$). After $n$ dynode stages, the total gain is $G = \delta^n \approx 10^6$ to $10^9$.
5. **Pulse Output:** The multiplied electron cloud is collected at the anode, producing a measurable voltage pulse whose amplitude is **proportional to the original radiation energy** (key advantage over GM counter).

**Comparison with GM Counter (2 marks):**

| Feature | GM Counter | Scintillation Counter |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Resolution | None (all pulses equal size) | Good (~7–10% for NaI at 662 keV) |
| Dead Time | Long (50–500 µs) | Short (~ns to µs) |
| Gamma Efficiency | Low (~1%) | High (~10–100%) |
| Pulse Height Information | Not available | Available → enables spectroscopy |
| Ruggedness | Excellent | Fragile |

The scintillation counter is preferred when **gamma spectroscopy**, **high counting rates**, or **short time resolution** is required. The GM counter is preferred for **portable survey meters**, **personnel monitoring**, and applications where **simplicity and ruggedness** dominate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Alternative question (Internal Choice)*

**Q.B (a)** Explain the principle of **radiation detection** by ionization and excitation methods. Discuss the three modes of interaction of gamma radiation with matter. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** A radioactive source has half-life of 5.26 years. Calculate: (i) the decay constant, (ii) the activity after 10 years if the initial activity is $A_0 = 1 \, \mu\text{Ci}$, (iii) the number of radioactive atoms initially present. Given: $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle of Radiation Detection (3 marks):** Radiation detection is based on the transfer of energy from ionizing radiation to a detector medium, producing a measurable signal. Two principal energy-exchange mechanisms are exploited:

1. **Ionization Method:** Incident radiation removes orbital electrons from atoms of the detector medium, producing positive ions and free electrons. These charge carriers are collected by an applied electric field, generating a current pulse. This is the operating principle of **gas-filled detectors** (ion chambers, proportional counters, GM counters) and **semiconductor detectors**. The magnitude of the signal depends on the radiation energy deposited and the W-value (average energy required to create an ion pair; for air, $W \approx 33.97$ eV).

2. **Excitation Method:** Incident radiation raises electrons of the detector medium to higher energy states without ionizing the atoms. De-excitation produces **scintillation photons** (visible/near-UV light). These photons are detected by a photomultiplier tube and converted to a measurable electrical pulse. This is the operating principle of **scintillation counters**.

**Three Modes of Gamma Interaction with Matter (4 marks):**

Gamma photons are uncharged and interact primarily with orbital electrons or the nucleus of atoms in the absorbing medium. The three principal modes are:

1. **Photoelectric Absorption (dominant at low $\gamma$ energy, $E_\gamma < 100$ keV):** The gamma photon transfers ALL its energy to a bound orbital electron, which is ejected from the atom as a **photoelectron**. The photon is completely absorbed. The probability of this interaction varies approximately as $Z^4 / E_\gamma^3$ — strongly dependent on atomic number Z. Hence high-Z materials (Pb, NaI) are excellent gamma absorbers.

$$\gamma + \text{atom} \rightarrow e^- \text{(photoelectron)} + \text{ion}^{+} + E_{\text{kinetic}}$$

2. **Compton Scattering (dominant at intermediate $\gamma$ energy, $100 \, \text{keV} < E_\gamma < 1 \, \text{MeV}$):** The gamma photon interacts with a loosely bound (essentially free) outer-shell electron. The photon is scattered at an angle $\theta$ with reduced energy, and the electron recoils with the energy difference. Energy and momentum conservation yield:

$$E'_\gamma = \frac{E_\gamma}{1 + \frac{E_\gamma}{m_0 c^2}(1 - \cos\theta)}$$

where $m_0 c^2 = 511$ keV is the electron rest energy. Maximum energy transfer occurs at $\theta = 180°$ (back-scattering).

3. **Pair Production (dominant at $E_\gamma > 1.022$ MeV):** When the gamma energy exceeds **twice the electron rest mass energy** ($2 m_0 c^2 = 1.022$ MeV), the photon may convert into an electron–positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus. The excess energy ($E_\gamma - 1.022$ MeV) is shared as kinetic energy between the pair. The positron subsequently annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV annihilation photons.

$$\gamma \rightarrow e^- + e^+ \quad (\text{requires } E_\gamma \geq 1.022 \, \text{MeV})$$

---

### Model Answer to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Half-life $T_{1/2} = 5.26$ years
- Initial activity $A_0 = 1 \, \mu\text{Ci} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq} = 3.7 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Bq}$
- Time $t = 10$ years

**(i) Decay constant $\lambda$ (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.26 \, \text{years}} = 0.1317 \, \text{year}^{-1}$$

Convert to SI units: $\lambda = 0.1317 / (365.25 \times 24 \times 3600) = 4.18 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s}^{-1}$

**(ii) Activity after 10 years (2 marks):**

$$A = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} = 3.7 \times 10^4 \times e^{-0.1317 \times 10}$$

$$\lambda t = 1.317 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

$$A = 3.7 \times 10^4 \times e^{-1.317} = 3.7 \times 10^4 \times 0.2681$$

$$\boxed{A = 9.92 \times 10^3 \, \text{Bq} \approx 0.268 \, \mu\text{Ci}}$$

**(iii) Initial number of radioactive atoms $N_0$ (3 marks):**

Using $A_0 = \lambda N_0$:

$$N_0 = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{3.7 \times 10^4 \, \text{Bq}}{4.18 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s}^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{N_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{12} \, \text{atoms}}$$

**Verification using alternative approach:** $N_0 = A_0 \times \tau_{\text{mean}}$, where $\tau_{\text{mean}} = 1/\lambda = 1/(4.18 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.39 \times 10^8 \, \text{s} \approx 7.58$ years. Then $N_0 = 3.7 \times 10^4 \times 2.39 \times 10^8 = 8.85 \times 10^{12}$ atoms ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "ion chamber" with "GM counter":** Both use gas ionization, but operate in different voltage regions. Specifying the **operating voltage region** is mandatory. (–1 mark if omitted)
>
> 2. **Stating "GM counter measures radiation energy":** This is fundamentally wrong. The GM counter produces **equal-amplitude pulses regardless of energy** — it ONLY COUNTS, not measures energy. Scintillation counters can provide energy information. (–2 marks if wrongly stated)
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in the activity conversion:** Always show $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$ explicitly when converting between Curie and Becquerel. (–1 mark)
>
> 4. **Skipping the dead-time correction derivation steps:** Examiners require intermediate steps (equation of live time, expansion, isolation of $n$). Don't jump to $n = m/(1-m\tau)$. (–2 marks for unjustified final formula)
>
> 5. **Neglecting to identify which quenching agent is used:** A **halogen-quenched tube** has a much longer life than an **organic-quenched tube**. Examiners expect this distinction. (–1 mark)
>
> 6. **Confusing "characteristic plateau" with "operating point":** The plateau is the **flat region of the curve**; the operating voltage is a **single point chosen on the plateau** (mid-point). (–1 mark)
>
> 7. **Skipping the photoelectric, Compton, and pair-production energy ranges:** Each process has a specific energy regime of dominance — cite the energy boundaries. (–1 mark if missing)
>
> 8. **No block diagram or incorrect diagram labeling:** A question asking for a "neat block diagram" without a diagram scores maximum 60–70% of the marks allotted for that part.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Radiation Measurement**

**A. Foundational Concepts:**
- Three ionizing radiations: **Alpha** (charge +2e, paper-shielded), **Beta** (charge –1e, Al-shielded), **Gamma** (charge 0, Pb-shielded).
- Three interaction mechanisms of gamma: **Photoelectric** (low E, $Z^4$ dependence), **Compton** (mid E, $Z$ dependence), **Pair production** (E > 1.022 MeV, $Z^2$ dependence).
- Units: **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**; **1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg**; **1 Gy = 100 rad**; **1 Sv = 100 rem**.

**B. Decay Law Essentials:**
- $N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ ; $A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$
- $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$ ; $\tau_{\text{mean}} = 1/\lambda = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$

**C. GM Counter — 10 Must-Know Points:**
1. Gas-filled tube with central anode wire.
2. Argon + halogen (or organic vapor) filling.
3. Operating in Geiger region (saturation regime).
4. Pulse amplitude is **INDEPENDENT** of radiation energy.
5. Six-stage pulse formation: ionization → avalanche → UV photon → photoemission → space charge → pulse.
6. Dead time ($\tau_d$) due to positive ion space charge.
7. Quenching essential — halogens preferred for long life.
8. Operating point = midpoint of plateau.
9. Resolving time measured by **two-source method**: $\tau = (m_1 + m_2 - m_{12})/(2 m_1 m_2)$.
10. Count rate correction: $n = m/(1 - m\tau)$.

**D. Scintillation Counter — 10 Must-Know Points:**
1. Three sub-systems: **Scintillator + PMT + Electronics**.
2. NaI(Tl) is the most common inorganic scintillator — high Z, hygroscopic.
3. Organic scintillators (Anthracene, plastic, liquid) for fast timing.
4. PMT gain: $G = \delta^n \approx 10^6$ to $10^9$ for typical $n = 10$–$14$ dynodes.
5. Pulse amplitude is **PROPORTIONAL** to radiation energy → enables spectroscopy.
6. Photocathode (Sb-Cs or bialkali) converts photons to electrons.
7. Energy resolution ~7–10% for NaI at 662 keV (Cs-137).
8. Dead time ~ns to µs — much shorter than GM counter.
9. Requires high voltage (~1000 V) for dynode chain.
10. Fragile — NaI crystals crack on mechanical shock.

**E. Comparative Take-Away:**
- **Choose GM Counter** when: ruggedness, simplicity, low cost, alpha/beta detection, low count rates, no energy analysis needed.
- **Choose Scintillation Counter** when: gamma spectroscopy, high count rates, fast timing, energy discrimination, high gamma efficiency.

**F. Common Numerical Patterns to Practice:**
1. Decay constant from half-life (and vice versa).
2. Activity at time $t$ given initial activity.
3. Dead time from two-source data.
4. True count rate from observed count rate and resolving time.
5. PMT total gain from $\delta$ and $n$.
6. Decay chain problems (parent-daughter equilibrium).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Variables Measurement - Temperature Measurement, Thermistors, Thermopile, Infrared Sensors, Pyroelectric Sensors, Sensor ICs (LM34, LM35)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Variables Measurement - Temperature Measurement, Thermistors, Thermopile, Infrared Sensors, Pyroelectric Sensors, Sensor ICs (LM34, LM35)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature** is a scalar physical quantity that thermodynamically represents the *degree of hotness or coldness* of a body, defined as the *measure of the average kinetic energy of the atoms/molecules* of a system. It is one of the **seven SI base quantities**, measured in the SI unit **Kelvin (K)**.

Mathematically, for an ideal gas at thermodynamic equilibrium:
$$T = \lim_{m \to 0} \frac{pV}{mR}$$
where $p$ is pressure, $V$ is volume, $m$ is mass, and $R$ is the **specific gas constant = 287 J/(kg·K) for air**.

The four standard temperature scales used in industrial instrumentation are:

| Scale | Symbol | Lower Fixed Point | Upper Fixed Point | Unit Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Celsius | °C | 0 °C (273.15 K) | 100 °C (373.15 K) | 1 °C = 1 K |
| Fahrenheit | °F | 32 °F | 212 °F | 1.8 °F = 1 K |
| Kelvin | K | 0 K (Absolute Zero) | 273.15 K | 1 K |
| Rankine | °R | 0 °R | 491.67 °R | 1 °R = 1.8 K |

The conversions between these scales are:
$$T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15$$
$$T_{°F} = \frac{9}{5} T_{°C} + 32$$
$$T_{°R} = T_{°F} + 459.67$$

## 1.2 Classification of Thermal Sensors (KTU Syllabus Anchor)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Anchor:** Thermal sensors are broadly classified into two categories based on the *physical mode of heat transfer* used for measurement.

**1. Contact-Type Sensors** — The sensor is physically in contact with the measured body. Heat flows by *conduction*.

- Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers
- Bimetallic Strips
- Thermocouples
- Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)
- **Thermistors (NTC / PTC)**
- **Integrated Circuit (IC) Sensors — LM34, LM35**
- Thermopiles (when applied to surfaces)

**2. Non-Contact Type Sensors** — The sensor detects emitted *electromagnetic radiation* from the body. Heat flows by *radiation*.

- **Infrared (IR) Thermometers / Pyrometers**
- **Pyroelectric Sensors**
- Optical Pyrometers
- Thermal Imaging Cameras

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Thermometer 'Feels' Heat"

Imagine you place one hand in ice water and another in warm water. After a minute, both hands adapt to their respective temperatures. Your nerves are essentially *thermal sensors*. They give a voltage-like signal to your brain proportional to the thermal energy they receive.

This is exactly how every thermal sensor works:

> **Thermal Energy → Physical Property Change → Electrical Signal Output**

- In a **thermistor**, thermal energy changes the *electrical resistance* of a semiconductor.
- In a **thermocouple/thermopile**, thermal energy creates an *EMF* at the junction of two dissimilar metals.
- In an **infrared sensor**, thermal energy is emitted as *photons*, which a photodetector converts to voltage.
- In a **pyroelectric sensor**, thermal energy changes the *spontaneous polarization* of a crystal.
- In **LM34/LM35**, an on-chip *bandgap reference circuit* converts temperature into a precision *analog voltage output*.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Relevant to This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4\text{)}$
>
> **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
>
> **Seebeck coefficient** (for typical Chromel-Alumel): $S \approx 40 \,\mu\text{V/°C}$
>
> **Curie temperature of typical pyroelectric crystal (LiTaO₃):** $T_C \approx 618 \,\text{°C}$
>
> **LM35 sensitivity:** **+10.0 mV/°C** (calibrated directly in Celsius)
>
> **LM34 sensitivity:** **+10.0 mV/°F** (calibrated directly in Fahrenheit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermistors

### 2.1.1 Definition and Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a *non-linear, semiconductor-based passive resistor* whose **electrical resistance is a strong function of temperature**. The body is a sintered mixture of metallic oxides (Mn, Ni, Co, Fe, Cu) encapsulated in glass or epoxy.

Two principal categories exist:
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* as temperature rises.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* as temperature rises (e.g., BaTiO₃-based).

### 2.1.2 Working Principle

In NTC thermistors, as temperature rises, more charge carriers are thermally excited across the semiconductor bandgap, *lowering* resistance. The dependence is exponential:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically $298.15 \,\text{K}$, i.e., $25 \,°\text{C}$)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typically **3000 K – 5000 K**)

For greater accuracy (especially over wide ranges), the **Steinhart-Hart equation** is used (derived in Section 3):

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$$

### 2.1.3 Advantages & Limitations

| Parameter | Thermistor | RTD | Thermocouple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature Range | -100 °C to +300 °C | -200 °C to +850 °C | -270 °C to +2300 °C |
| Sensitivity | **Highest** | Medium | Low |
| Linearity | Non-linear (needs compensation) | Excellent | Requires polynomial fit |
| Self-heating | Critical issue | Minor | Negligible |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | Low |

## 2.2 Thermopile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Thermopile** is essentially *N thermocouples connected in series* (thermoelectrically, but NOT electrically with their junctions shared) to **amplify the small Seebeck EMF** of a single thermocouple. It operates on the **Seebeck Effect**.

For $N$ identical thermocouples in series:
$$V_{\text{out}} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$
where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient of the pair.

The thermopile used in non-contact IR thermometers consists of **absorbing hot junctions** (exposed to incident IR radiation) and **reference cold junctions** (mounted on a heat-sunk substrate). The temperature difference generates a measurable EMF.

## 2.3 Infrared (IR) Temperature Sensors

### 2.3.1 Physical Principle — Blackbody Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every physical body above absolute zero (0 K) emits **thermal electromagnetic radiation**. The intensity and spectral distribution of this radiation depend on the body's temperature and **emissivity** $\varepsilon$.

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**
$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

where:
- $E$ = radiant exitance (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity ($0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = absolute surface temperature (K)

**Wien's Displacement Law** (for finding peak emission wavelength):
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} \quad ; \quad b = 2.897771955 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m·K}$$

### 2.3.2 Working Principle of IR Thermometer

1. Lens (often Germanium or ZnSe) focuses IR radiation onto a detector.
2. Detector is typically a **thermopile** (passive) or a **photodiode** (active).
3. Output signal is conditioned and digitized.
4. Microcontroller applies Stefan-Boltzmann inversion:
$$T_{\text{obj}} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{K \cdot \varepsilon}}$$
where $K$ is the calibrated constant for the optics/detector system.

## 2.4 Pyroelectric Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyroelectricity** is the ability of certain crystals (e.g., **Lithium Tantalate — LiTaO₃, PZT, Triglycine Sulfate — TGS**) to generate a surface charge in response to a *change in temperature*. This arises from a change in the crystal's **spontaneous polarization** $P_s$.

The induced current is given by:
$$I_p = A \cdot p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$
where:
- $A$ = electrode area
- $p$ = pyroelectric coefficient (C/m²·K)
- $\frac{dT}{dt}$ = rate of change of temperature

> **CRITICAL KTU POINT:** A pyroelectric sensor responds only to *changes* in temperature, NOT to *static* temperature. This is why it is mainly used for **motion detection (PIR sensors)** and **infrared spectroscopy**, not steady-state temperature measurement.

The crystal loses its pyroelectric behavior above its **Curie temperature** $T_C$, above which it becomes a *paraelectric* with no net spontaneous polarization.

## 2.5 Integrated Circuit Temperature Sensors — LM34 & LM35

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LM35** and **LM34** are precision **monolithic integrated-circuit temperature sensors** from Texas Instruments whose output voltage is *linearly proportional* to temperature, with internal calibration done at the wafer level.

### 2.5.1 LM35 — Calibrated in Celsius

- **Sensitivity:** **+10.0 mV/°C** (linear)
- **Output at 0 °C:** **0 mV**
- **Output at 100 °C:** **1000 mV = 1.0 V**
- **Range:** **-55 °C to +150 °C**
- **Accuracy:** ±0.5 °C (guaranteed calibrated)
- **Operating voltage:** **4 V to 30 V**

### 2.5.2 LM34 — Calibrated in Fahrenheit

- **Sensitivity:** **+10.0 mV/°F** (linear)
- **Output at 0 °F:** **0 mV**
- **Output at 100 °F:** **1000 mV = 1.0 V**
- **Range:** **-50 °F to +300 °F** (extended to +500 °F)

### 2.5.3 Pinout (TO-92 / TO-220 packages — both share identical pinout)

1. **Pin 1:** V⁺ (Supply)
2. **Pin 2:** V_out (Analog Voltage Output)
3. **Pin 3:** GND (Ground)

### 2.5.4 Internal Block Architecture

The IC uses **bandgap reference circuits** exploiting the difference in base-emitter voltages ($\Delta V_{BE}$) of two matched BJTs operating at different current densities:

$$\Delta V_{BE} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}}\right)$$

This $\Delta V_{BE}$ is *proportional to absolute temperature (PTAT)* and is combined with a complementary-to-absolute-temperature (CTAT) voltage to produce a *Celsius-scaled* analog output.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield formulas for KTU Board Examinations — Module 3 Thermal Measurement**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NTC Thermistor | $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $R_T$, $R_0$: resistances; $\beta$: material constant; $T, T_0$: temperatures | Ω, Ω, K, K |
| 2 | Steinhart-Hart | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C[\ln(R)]^3$ | Empirical fit coefficients $A, B, C$ | K⁻¹, K⁻¹, K⁻¹ |
| 3 | Thermopile EMF | $V_{\text{out}} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$ | $N$: pairs; $S$: Seebeck coeff.; $\Delta T$: temp. difference | V, —, V/K, K |
| 4 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $E$: irradiance; $\varepsilon$: emissivity; $T$: temperature | W/m², —, K |
| 5 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}$ | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | m, m·K, K |
| 6 | Pyroelectric current | $I_p = A \cdot p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$ | $A$: electrode area; $p$: pyro coeff. | A, m², C/m²·K, K/s |
| 7 | LM35 output | $V_{\text{out}} = 10 \cdot T_{°\text{C}}$ (in mV) | Linear Celsius-scaled voltage | mV, °C |
| 8 | LM34 output | $V_{\text{out}} = 10 \cdot T_{°\text{F}}$ (in mV) | Linear Fahrenheit-scaled voltage | mV, °F |
| 9 | RTD (for comparison) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | Linear, $\alpha \approx 0.00385$ for Pt | Ω, Ω, °C⁻¹, °C |
| 10 | Scale conversion | $T_{°\text{F}} = \frac{9}{5} T_{°\text{C}} + 32$ | Conversion between scales | °F, °C |

## 2.7 Real-World Industrial Applications

- **Thermistors:** Inrush current limiting, motor winding protection, medical thermometers, automotive coolant sensors, battery-pack monitoring.
- **Thermopiles:** Ear thermometers (clinical), non-contact HVAC sensors, laser power meters, gas analyzer detection cells.
- **IR Pyrometers:** Steel/molten metal temperature, semiconductor wafer monitoring, fire detection, food industry.
- **Pyroelectric Sensors:** PIR motion detectors (security alarms, automatic doors), IR spectrometers, CO₂ laser detection.
- **LM34 / LM35:** HVAC thermostats, weather stations, battery chargers, PC thermal shutdown, embedded system protection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: NTC Thermistor Resistance Equation

### Given Empirical Behaviour

Experimentally, the conductivity of an NTC semiconductor is governed by an Arrhenius-type activation:
$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / (k_B T)}$$

Since resistance is the inverse of conductivity ($R \propto 1/\sigma$):
$$R(T) \propto e^{+E_a / (k_B T)}$$

### Step 1: Define Proportionality

$$R(T) = R_{\infty} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B T}}$$

where $R_{\infty}$ is a constant representing the resistance at theoretically infinite temperature.

### Step 2: Eliminate $R_{\infty}$ Using a Reference

At a known reference temperature $T_0$ (e.g., $25\,°\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$), the resistance is $R_0$:
$$R_0 = R_{\infty} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B T_0}}$$

Solving for $R_{\infty}$:
$$R_{\infty} = R_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_B T_0}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back

$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_B T_0}} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B T}}$$

### Step 4: Combine Exponentials

$$\begin{aligned}
R(T) &= R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Define Beta Constant

Defining $\beta = \dfrac{E_a}{k_B}$ (typically 3000–5000 K), we get the textbook form:
$$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}}$$

This is the **canonical NTC thermistor equation** used universally in industry.

## 3.2 Numerical Application — NTC Thermistor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K}$ and $\beta = 3950\,\text{K}$. Calculate its resistance at $T = 323.15\,\text{K}$ ($50\,°\text{C}$).

**Step 1: Compute $\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} &= \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \\
&= 0.0030948 - 0.0033540 \\
&= -2.5923 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Multiply by $\beta$**

$$\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-2.5923 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.02396$$

**Step 3: Apply Exponential**

$$e^{-1.02396} = 0.3592$$

**Step 4: Compute $R_T$**

$$R_T = 10\,000 \times 0.3592 \approx \mathbf{3592 \,\Omega = 3.592 \,\text{k}\Omega}$$

**Interpretation:** As expected for an NTC, resistance *dropped* from 10 kΩ to 3.59 kΩ when temperature rose by 25 °C.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Used in IR Pyrometers)

### Starting from Planck's Spectral Distribution

The spectral radiance of a blackbody per unit wavelength is:
$$B_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1}$$

The total emitted power per unit area is obtained by integrating over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$:
$$E = \int_0^{\infty} B_\lambda(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

### Substitute and Use Substitution

Let $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$ so that $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{x^2 k_B T} dx$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2hc^2}{\left(\frac{hc}{x k_B T}\right)^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^x - 1} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{x^2 k_B T}\right) dx \\
  &= \frac{2 k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx
\end{aligned}$$

### Evaluate the Integral

The integral $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$ is a known result from the Riemann zeta function ($\zeta(4) = \pi^4/90$, giving the factor of $1/15$ after dividing by 6 from the $x^3$ expansion).

### Final Result

$$E = \frac{2 \pi^4 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4$$

Defining the Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = \dfrac{2 \pi^4 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/(m}^2\text{K}^4\text{)}$:

$$\boxed{E = \sigma T^4}$$

For a *real* (non-black) body with emissivity $\varepsilon$:
$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

### Application to IR Sensor Calibration

The IR pyrometer measures $E$ via the thermopile voltage. The object temperature is recovered by:
$$\boxed{T_{\text{obj}} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{K \cdot \varepsilon}}}$$

where $K$ is a calibration constant (W/V conversion factor × geometry).

> **Industrial importance:** This 4th-root relationship is why IR thermometers are *most accurate at high temperatures*. At low temperatures, a small absolute error in $V_{\text{out}}$ translates to a much larger relative error in $T$.

## 3.4 Worked Example: LM35 Output Voltage Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An LM35 is used in a weather station. The ambient temperature is $38.4\,°\text{C}$. The sensor is powered from +5 V. Determine: (a) the analog output voltage, (b) the resolution if the ADC is 10-bit with $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\,\text{V}$.

### Part (a): Analog Output Voltage

LM35 sensitivity is **+10.0 mV/°C** (linear, calibrated in Celsius).
$$V_{\text{out}} = 10 \,\frac{\text{mV}}{°\text{C}} \times 38.4\,°\text{C}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= 384 \,\text{mV} \\
               &= 0.384 \,\text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b): ADC Resolution

ADC step size:
$$V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5}{1023} \approx 4.8876 \,\text{mV/LSB}$$

Temperature resolution:
$$\Delta T = \frac{V_{\text{LSB}}}{10 \,\text{mV/°C}} = \frac{4.8876}{10} \approx 0.4888 \,\text{°C/LSB}$$

### Part (c): Digital Output Code

$$\text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{LSB}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{384}{4.8876} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 78.57 \rfloor = 78$$

So the digital code is **78** (decimal).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in the KTU viva/exam to justify your sensor choice for a given problem.**

| Application Scenario | Best Sensor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial furnace (1200 °C, steel) | Type K/R Thermocouple or Optical Pyrometer | High range, fast response, non-contact |
| Cryogenic liquid nitrogen (-196 °C) | Platinum RTD (Pt-100) | Best linearity at low T; thermistor unusable |
| In-circuit board thermal shutdown | LM35 / LM34 | Simple analog interface, on-chip |
| Ear thermometer | Thermopile + IR optics | Contact-less, fast, clinical-grade |
| Automatic sliding door at mall | Pyroelectric (PIR) | Detects human IR *change*, not steady T |
| Battery-pack protection (laptop) | NTC Thermistor (10 kΩ) | Inexpensive, high sensitivity in 0–80 °C |
| Laser power measurement | Thermopile sensor head | Broadband, flat spectral response |
| Aircraft altitude / OAT probe | RTD (Pt-100 or Pt-1000) | Ruggedized, linear, long-term stable |

## 3.6 Comparative Performance Analysis Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 14-Mark Questions:** Compare any two sensors using a tabular matrix as shown below.

| Parameter | Thermistor | RTD (Pt-100) | Thermocouple | LM35 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Resistance change | Resistance change | Seebeck EMF | Bandgap voltage |
| Range (°C) | -100 to +300 | -200 to +850 | -270 to +2300 | -55 to +150 |
| Sensitivity | 3–5 %/°C | 0.385 %/°C | 40–60 µV/°C | 10 mV/°C |
| Linearity | Exponential (poor) | Excellent | Polynomial (fair) | Excellent |
| Accuracy | ±0.1 to ±1 °C | ±0.01 to ±0.1 °C | ±0.5 to ±2 °C | ±0.5 °C |
| Self-heating | Severe | Low | Negligible | Low |
| Cost | Very Low | High | Low | Low |
| Interface | Resistance to voltage | Resistance to voltage | Direct mV | Direct V |
| Output Type | Resistance | Resistance | Micro-volt | Analog voltage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Thermal Measurement Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Thermal Variables Measurement] --> B0[Contact Type Sensors]
    A0 --> C0[Non Contact Type Sensors]
    B0 --> B1[Liquid in Glass Thermometer]
    B0 --> B2[Bimetallic Strip]
    B0 --> B3[Thermocouple]
    B0 --> B4[RTD Pt100 Pt1000]
    B0 --> B5[NTC PTC Thermistor]
    B0 --> B6[IC Sensor LM34 LM35]
    B0 --> B7[Thermopile]
    C0 --> C1[Optical Pyrometer]
    C0 --> C2[Infrared Pyrometer]
    C0 --> C3[Pyroelectric Sensor]
    C0 --> C4[Thermal Imaging Camera]
    B6 --> B6a[LM35 Calibrated in Celsius]
    B6 --> B6b[LM34 Calibrated in Fahrenheit]
    B5 --> B5a[NTC Resistance Decreases]
    B5 --> B5b[PTC Resistance Increases]
    C2 --> C2a[Lens Optics]
    C2 --> C2b[Thermopile or Photodiode Detector]
    C3 --> C3a[LiTaO3 Crystal]
    C3 --> C3b[PZT Ceramic]
```

## 4.2 Thermopile Internal Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR1[Incident IR Radiation] --> L1[Germanium Lens]
    L1 --> J1[Hot Junction N x Thermocouples]
    J1 --> E1[EMF Source N x S x dT]
    E1 --> A1[Amplifier Stage]
    A1 --> F1[Low Pass Filter]
    F1 --> M1[Microcontroller ADC]
    M1 --> D1[Digital Temperature Display]
    C1[Heat Sink Reference Cold Junction] --> J1
    J1 -.->|Cold Junction Reference| C1
```

## 4.3 LM35 / LM34 Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[Vplus 4V to 30V] --> BGR[Bandgap Reference Cell]
    BGR --> PTAT[PTAT Circuit Delta VBE proportional to T]
    BGR --> CTAT[CTAT Circuit VBE proportional to 1 by T]
    PTAT --> SUMM[Summing Network]
    CTAT --> SUMM
    SUMM --> BUF[Output Buffer Amplifier]
    BUF --> VOUT[Vout Pin Linear 10mV per Degree]
    GND[GND Pin] --> BGR
    TEMP[Ambient Temperature at Die] --> PTAT
    TEMP --> CTAT
```

## 4.4 Pyroelectric Sensor Operation — Charge Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[IR Photons Strike Crystal] --> B0[Absorption Lattice Heating]
    B0 --> C0[Change in Spontaneous Polarization Ps]
    C0 --> D0[Surface Charge Variation dQ on Electrodes]
    D0 --> E0[Current ip A p dT by dt]
    E0 --> F0[JFET Source Follower]
    F0 --> G0[Amplifier and Window Comparator]
    G0 --> H0[Digital Motion Output Pulse]
    H0 --> I0[Motion Detection PIR Application]
```

## 4.5 Infrared Pyrometer — Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ[Target Object at Temperature Tobj] -->|Radiation| LENS[IR Focusing Lens]
    LENS --> DET[Thermopile Detector]
    DET --> AMP[Low Noise Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> COMP[Ambient Temperature Compensation]
    COMP --> CALIB[Emissivity Calibration]
    CALIB --> SQR[Fourth Root Computation by MCU]
    SQR --> DISP[Linearized Digital Display in Degree C]
```

## 4.6 NTC Thermistor — Voltage Divider Biasing Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[Vplus 5V] --> RT[Top Resistor Rref 10k]
    RT --> NODE1[Output Node Vout]
    NODE1 --> RTH[NTC Thermistor RT]
    RTH --> GND[Ground]
    NODE1 --> ADC[Microcontroller ADC Input]
    ADC --> LOOKUP[Steinhart Hart Inversion]
    LOOKUP --> DISP[Linearized Temperature Display]
```

> **Engineering Note (KTU Practical):** The output node voltage is $V_{\text{out}} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_{\text{ref}} + R_T}$. Since $R_T$ is non-linear, a Steinhart-Hart inversion in the microcontroller's lookup table is mandatory for accurate readings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **NTC** and **PTC** thermistors. State **two applications** of each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* with increase in temperature. Constructed from sintered metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co). Highly non-linear, exponential $R$–$T$ behavior. High sensitivity (~3–5 %/°C). Used in **inrush current limiters, temperature-compensation circuits, and clinical thermometers**.

> **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* with temperature (typically above a *switching temperature* $T_s$). Constructed from doped **BaTiO₃** ceramic. Exhibits a sharp resistance jump near $T_s$ (Curie point). Used in **self-resetting fuses (PPTC), motor overload protectors, and degaussing circuits**.

| Feature | NTC | PTC |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of dR/dT | Negative | Positive |
| Typical Material | Mn-Ni-Co oxides | BaTiO₃ ceramic |
| Curve | Smooth exponential | Sharp step near $T_s$ |
| Application | Inrush limiter | Resettable fuse |

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model question]**

**Q:** What is the **Steinhart-Hart equation**? Why is it preferred over the simple β-equation for precision thermistor applications?

**Model Answer:**

The **Steinhart-Hart equation** is an empirical model that relates the absolute temperature $T$ of a semiconductor thermistor to its resistance $R$ with very high accuracy (typically **±0.01 °C**) over a wide temperature range:

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$$

where $A$, $B$, $C$ are the device-specific coefficients (typically determined by a 3-point calibration at known temperatures).

**Why preferred over the simple β-equation?**

1. The simple β-model assumes a *single* activation energy $E_a$, which is only valid over narrow temperature ranges (~50 °C). Over 0–300 °C, real thermistors exhibit curvature that β cannot capture.
2. The cubic logarithmic term in Steinhart-Hart captures this non-ideality, giving **±0.01 °C accuracy** vs. **±1–2 °C** for β.
3. Steinhart-Hart is the **NIST-recommended model** for precision temperature sensing.
4. Modern microcontrollers can compute $T$ from $R$ in microseconds using only a few floating-point multiplications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **infrared (IR) pyrometer**. Derive the governing equation and discuss the role of **emissivity** in measurement accuracy.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A **thermopile detector** in an IR thermometer has **32 thermocouples** in series, each with a Seebeck coefficient of $55 \,\mu\text{V/°C}$. If the detector's hot junctions are exposed to a target at $250\,°\text{C}$ while the cold junctions are at $25\,°\text{C}$, calculate: (i) the open-circuit thermopile EMF, (ii) the output voltage after a gain stage of 100, and (iii) the corresponding temperature rise in Fahrenheit.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) IR Pyrometer — Construction and Working**

An **infrared pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measurement instrument that infers the temperature of an object from the **thermal electromagnetic radiation** it emits. The block diagram was shown in Section 4.5. The functional blocks are:

1. **Optical System (Lens/Mirror):** A Germanium (Ge) or Zinc Selenide (ZnSe) lens focuses the emitted IR radiation from the target onto the detector. The lens material is chosen because it is transparent to IR wavelengths (8–14 µm atmospheric window).

2. **IR Detector:** Typically a **thermopile** (broadband, 0.1–100 µm response) or a **photodiode** (narrowband, e.g., InGaAs for 1.6 µm or HgCdTe for 8–14 µm).

3. **Signal Conditioning:** Low-noise instrumentation amplifier (gain ~1000) and ambient temperature compensation (using an onboard RTD to subtract the sensor's own body temperature from the reading).

4. **Signal Processing:** Microcontroller computes $T$ using the Stefan-Boltzmann inversion.

5. **Display:** Linearized digital temperature readout in °C or °F.

**Governing Equation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):**

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

The radiated power density is *proportional to the 4th power* of absolute temperature. After optical collection and conversion to voltage by the thermopile:

$$V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left(T_{\text{obj}}^4 - T_{\text{ref}}^4\right)$$

Inverting:
$$T_{\text{obj}} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{K \cdot \varepsilon} + T_{\text{ref}}^4}$$

**Role of Emissivity:**

$\varepsilon$ is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real body to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature ($0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$). **Most pyrometers are pre-calibrated assuming $\varepsilon = 1$ (blackbody)**. For real surfaces, the user must input the correct $\varepsilon$ or the reading will be **erroneously low**:

| Surface | Emissivity |
|---|---|
| Human skin | 0.98 |
| Polished aluminum | 0.05 |
| Matte black paint | 0.97 |
| Glass | 0.92 |
| Stainless steel (polished) | 0.16 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that the 4th-root relationship means **a 2% error in emissivity setting can cause a ~10 °C error at 200 °C**. Always state this sensitivity in your exam answer.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Number of thermocouples $N = 32$
- Seebeck coefficient $S = 55 \,\mu\text{V/°C} = 55 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V/°C}$
- Hot junction $T_h = 250\,°\text{C}$
- Cold junction $T_c = 25\,°\text{C}$

**(i) Open-circuit thermopile EMF:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= 32 \times (55 \times 10^{-6}) \times (250 - 25) \\
               &= 32 \times 55 \times 10^{-6} \times 225 \\
               &= 32 \times 12375 \times 10^{-6} \\
               &= 396000 \times 10^{-6} \\
               &= 0.396 \,\text{V} \\
               &= 396 \,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Voltage after amplifier (Gain = 100):**

$$V_{\text{out,amp}} = G \cdot V_{\text{out}} = 100 \times 0.396 = \boxed{39.6 \,\text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert µV to V before multiplying. Marks lost here.

**(iii) Corresponding temperature rise in Fahrenheit:**

$$\Delta T_{°\text{C}} = 250 - 25 = 225\,°\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T_{°\text{F}} = \frac{9}{5} \times 225 = \boxed{405\,°\text{F}}$$

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, describe the **internal block diagram and working of LM35** integrated circuit temperature sensor. Why is it preferred over a thermistor for microcontroller-based applications?

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** An **NTC thermistor** has $R_0 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $25\,°\text{C}$ and $\beta = 3500\,\text{K}$. It is connected in series with a $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ precision resistor across a $5\,\text{V}$ supply. Calculate: (i) the thermistor resistance at $60\,°\text{C}$, (ii) the voltage across the thermistor at $60\,°\text{C}$, and (iii) the sensitivity $\left|\dfrac{dV}{dT}\right|$ at $25\,°\text{C}$ in mV/°C.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) LM35 IC Sensor**

The **LM35** is a precision Celsius-calibrated integrated-circuit temperature sensor manufactured by Texas Instruments. Its internal architecture (shown in Section 4.3) consists of:

1. **Bandgap Reference Cell:** Two matched BJTs operated at different collector current densities generate a $\Delta V_{BE}$ that is **PTAT** (proportional to absolute temperature).
2. **CTAT Circuit:** The forward $V_{BE}$ of a BJT is **complementary to absolute temperature** (~−2 mV/°C).
3. **Summing Network:** A precision on-chip resistor network adds PTAT and CTAT in the ratio that yields an output **linearly proportional to Celsius**, not Kelvin.
4. **Output Buffer:** A class-AB output stage capable of sourcing 10 mA, allowing direct ADC interfacing.

**Why preferred over thermistor for MCUs:**

| Feature | LM35 | NTC Thermistor |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Direct analog voltage (10 mV/°C) | Resistance → needs excitation & linearization |
| Linearity | **±0.25 °C linear** | Exponential → Steinhart-Hart lookup mandatory |
| Interface complexity | 3 pins, no other components | Bridge + ADC + polynomial math |
| Calibration | Factory-calibrated | User must calibrate 3 points |
| MCU code | 1 line: $T = ADC \times V_{\text{ref}} / (1024 \times 0.01)$ | 50+ lines of float math |

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K}$ ($25\,°\text{C}$)
- $\beta = 3500\,\text{K}$
- Series resistor $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- Supply voltage $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$
- Target temperature $T = 60\,°\text{C} = 333.15\,\text{K}$

**(i) Thermistor resistance at 60 °C:**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

Compute the inverse temperature difference:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} &= \frac{1}{333.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \\
&= 0.0030017 - 0.0033540 \\
&= -3.5230 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply by $\beta$:

$$\beta \cdot \Delta = 3500 \times (-3.5230 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.2330$$

Exponentiate:

$$e^{-1.2330} = 0.2913$$

Finally:

$$R_T = 5000 \times 0.2913 \approx \boxed{1456.5 \,\Omega}$$

**(ii) Voltage across thermistor (voltage divider):**

$$V_T = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R + R_T} = 5 \cdot \frac{1456.5}{5000 + 1456.5}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_T &= 5 \times \frac{1456.5}{6456.5} \\
    &= 5 \times 0.2256 \\
    &= \boxed{1.128\,\text{V}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity at 25 °C:**

The voltage across the thermistor is:
$$V_T = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R + R_T}$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $T$:
$$\frac{dV_T}{dT} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT}}{(R + R_T)^2}$$

From the β-equation:
$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta R_T}{T^2}$$

At $T = 298.15\,\text{K}$, $R_T = R_0 = 5000\,\Omega$:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT}\bigg|_{T_0} = -\frac{3500 \times 5000}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{1.75 \times 10^7}{88893.4} = -196.86 \,\Omega/\text{K}$$

Now plug in:

$$\frac{dV_T}{dT} = 5 \cdot \frac{5000 \times (-196.86)}{(5000 + 5000)^2} = 5 \cdot \frac{-9.843 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^8}$$

$$\frac{dV_T}{dT} = -4.921 \times 10^{-2} \,\text{V/K} = \boxed{-49.21 \,\text{mV/°C}}$$

The negative sign confirms NTC behavior (voltage decreases with temperature).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b)(iii):** Many students forget that sensitivity must be expressed as a **magnitude** (or correctly identify the negative sign). Also, do **not** confuse $T$ (Kelvin) with $t$ (Celsius) in the differentiation step; using °C instead of K gives a wrong answer because the β-equation requires absolute temperatures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 Thermal Measurement**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- **Temperature** is the measure of average kinetic energy of molecules; SI unit is **Kelvin (K)**.
- The **β-equation** governs NTC thermistor resistance:
  $$R_T = R_0 e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$
  - $\beta$ ranges from **3000 K to 5000 K**.
  - $T_0$ is conventionally **298.15 K** (25 °C).
- **Steinhart-Hart equation** gives superior accuracy over wide ranges:
  $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$$
- **Thermopile EMF** is **$N$ times** the single-junction thermocouple EMF:
  $$V_{\text{out}} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** (governs IR pyrometers):
  $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T}$ m·K
- **Pyroelectric current:** $I_p = A \cdot p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$ — note the $\frac{dT}{dt}$! Pyroelectric sensors respond only to **changes** in temperature.
- **LM35:** $\mathbf{+10\,\text{mV/°C}}$, calibrated in Celsius, range $-55$ to $+150\,°\text{C}$.
- **LM34:** $\mathbf{+10\,\text{mV/°F}}$, calibrated in Fahrenheit, range $-50$ to $+300\,°\text{F}$.
- **Curie temperature** of typical pyroelectric crystals: 200–700 °C — above this, spontaneous polarization vanishes.
- **Emissivity** of human skin ≈ **0.98**, polished metals ≈ **0.05–0.20**, matte paint ≈ **0.97**.

### Numerical Constants to Memorize

- Stefan-Boltzmann $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4\text{)}$
- Wien's $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}$
- Boltzmann $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$
- 0 °C = 273.15 K; 0 °F = 459.67 °R

### Common KTU Viva Questions

1. *"Why can't a pyroelectric sensor measure steady temperature?"* — Because its response is proportional to $\frac{dT}{dt}$, not $T$.
2. *"Why is LM35 called a 'Celsius-scale' sensor and not a 'Kelvin-scale' sensor?"* — Because the internal PTAT+CTAT mixing is designed so that the output crosses zero at 0 °C (273.15 K), giving direct Celsius readout.
3. *"Why does an IR pyrometer give large errors at low temperatures?"* — Because of the 4th-root relationship, small voltage errors produce large temperature errors at low $T$.
4. *"What is the major drawback of an NTC thermistor?"* — Self-heating (since current flows through it in a voltage divider, dissipating $I^2R$ power which further heats the sensor).
5. *"Why is a thermopile used inside IR thermometers instead of a single thermocouple?"* — A single thermocouple gives only ~50 µV/°C, too small to amplify cleanly. $N$ in series boosts the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For **14-mark sensor comparison questions**, always use a **table with at least 6–8 rows** (range, sensitivity, linearity, accuracy, self-heating, cost, interface, output type). Examiners award easy marks for tabular comparisons.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Imaging, Calorimetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Imaging, Calorimetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermal Imaging — Formal Definition

**Thermal Imaging (Infrared Thermography)** is a non-contact, non-destructive measurement technique that captures and visualizes the infrared (IR) radiation emitted by a body, converts it into a corresponding electrical signal using IR-sensitive transducers, and produces a two-dimensional thermal map (thermogram) where each pixel represents a temperature value at that point on the target surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Thermal imaging is the method of converting the invisible infrared radiation emitted by an object (in the wavelength range of **0.78 µm to 1000 µm**) into a visible, false-color image using specialized IR optics, focal plane array detectors, and signal processing electronics.*

Every object above **absolute zero (0 K or −273.15 °C)** emits electromagnetic radiation due to the thermal agitation of its molecules. The intensity and spectral distribution of this emitted radiation are governed by the **Planck's Radiation Law** and depend primarily on the object's **surface temperature** and **emissivity**.

### 1.1.1 Classification of the IR Spectrum (Per ISO 20473 Scheme)

| Band Designation | Wavelength Range (µm) | Typical Application |
|------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Near-Infrared (NIR) | 0.78 – 3 | Telecommunications, laser rangefinders |
| Mid-Wave Infrared (MWIR) | 3 – 6 | Military targeting, gas leak detection |
| Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) | 6 – 15 | Building diagnostics, medical imaging |
| Far-Infrared (FIR) | 15 – 1000 | Astronomical observation, spectroscopy |

Most commercial thermal cameras operate in the **MWIR (3–5 µm)** and **LWIR (8–14 µm)** atmospheric transmission windows where absorption by water vapor and CO₂ is minimal.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Seeing Heat Like the Predator":**
> Imagine you are a pit viper (like a rattlesnake) wandering in total darkness. You cannot see visible light, yet you accurately "see" the warm body of a mouse because your specialized pit organs detect the heat (infrared radiation) the mouse emits. A **thermal imaging camera is the engineering equivalent of a snake's pit organ** — it translates the invisible world of heat signatures into a visible color-coded map that the human eye can interpret.
> 
> In practical engineering terms: an electrical motor winding is overheating at 180 °C while the rest runs at 75 °C. To your naked eye, everything looks uniformly dark. A thermal camera instantly displays the hot winding as a bright yellow/white spot against a cooler blue background — saving the engineer from catastrophic failure.

## 1.2 Calorimetry — Formal Definition

**Calorimetry** is the scientific discipline concerned with the precise measurement of **heat energy (Q)** exchanged between a system and its surroundings, or the determination of physical quantities such as **specific heat capacity (c)**, **latent heat (L)**, and **heat of reaction (ΔH)** derived from such heat measurements.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Calorimetry is the experimental science of measuring the quantity of heat released, absorbed, or transferred during a physical change, chemical reaction, or phase transition, using a device called a **calorimeter**.*

The fundamental governing equation in all calorimetric analysis is:

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

where the symbols carry their standard meaning. In a perfectly insulated system, the law of energy conservation is invoked: **Heat lost by the hot body = Heat gained by the cold body**.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Banking of Heat":**
> Think of heat as **money in a savings account**. A calorimeter is the **bank vault** — perfectly insulated so that no "money" (heat) leaks in or out. When you drop a hot iron ball into a cold water beaker inside the vault, the iron "withdraws" money (loses heat) and the water "deposits" money (gains heat). The calorimeter's job is to *count* exactly how much currency changed hands by tracking the temperature shift. The bank balance (total energy) before and after must remain identical, in strict accordance with the **First Law of Thermodynamics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **S.I. Unit Conventions:**
> - Heat (Q): **Joule (J)** — the official SI unit. The older **calorie (cal)** is still widely used, where **1 cal = 4.186 J**.
> - Temperature (T): **Kelvin (K)** in absolute scientific work, or **Celsius (°C)** for practical use. Note: a *temperature difference* of 1 K is identical to a difference of 1 °C.
> - Specific heat capacity (c): **J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
> - Heat of combustion/reaction: **J/kg** or **J/mol**.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Industrial Significance

| Domain | Thermal Imaging Application | Calorimetry Application |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Power Generation** | Boiler tube inspection, turbine health | Fuel calorific value determination |
| **Petrochemical** | Refinery flare monitoring, leak detection | Crude oil heating value testing |
| **Building Sciences** | Insulation gap detection, moisture mapping | HVAC load calculations |
| **Medical** | Tumor screening, vascular imaging | Metabolic rate estimation |
| **Electronics** | PCB hot-spot identification | Chip thermal characterization |
| **Defense** | Night vision, target acquisition | Propellant heat of explosion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Infrared Radiation — Foundation of Thermal Imaging

### 2.1.1 Blackbody Radiation Laws

A **Blackbody** is an idealized, perfect absorber and emitter of radiation. Real objects (called **gray bodies** or **selective emitters**) are compared against this ideal. The behavior of blackbody radiation is described by three classical laws:

#### A) Planck's Radiation Law (Spectral Radiance)

This is the fundamental quantum-mechanical expression that gives the spectral distribution of emitted radiation as a function of wavelength and absolute temperature:

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hc}{\lambda k_{B} T}} - 1}$$

where:
- $E_{\lambda}$ = spectral emissive power (W·m⁻²·m⁻¹)
- $h$ = **Planck's constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = **speed of light = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $k_{B}$ = **Boltzmann's constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters for KTU:** Planck's law is the *master equation*. Wien's displacement law and Stefan-Boltzmann law are derived by integrating or differentiating this expression. In board exams, you may be asked to *derive* Stefan-Boltzmann or Wien's law from Planck's law.

#### B) Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength)

By differentiating Planck's equation with respect to λ and setting the result to zero, we obtain the wavelength at which emission is maximum:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** As the object's temperature rises, the peak emission shifts to *shorter* wavelengths. A piece of cold iron (~300 K) peaks around **9.66 µm** (LWIR). A hot tungsten filament (~2800 K) peaks around **1.03 µm** (NIR, glowing red).

#### C) Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Emitted Power)

Integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths yields the **total radiant exitance** (power per unit area):

$$E = \sigma \cdot T^{4}$$

For a *real* (gray) body, this is modified by emissivity ε:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^{4}$$

where **σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Because the emitted power depends on T⁴, even a small change in temperature produces a *large* change in emitted radiation. This is why thermal cameras are exquisitely sensitive — a 1% change in T translates to roughly a **4% change in detected signal**.

### 2.1.2 Emissivity (ε) — The Hidden Variable

Emissivity is the ratio of radiation emitted by a *real surface* to that emitted by a *blackbody* at the same temperature. It is a number between **0 and 1** (dimensionless).

| Surface | Approximate Emissivity (ε) |
|---------|------------------------------|
| Polished gold | 0.02 |
| Polished aluminum | 0.05 |
| Anodized aluminum | 0.85 |
| Human skin | 0.98 |
| Black paint, matte | 0.97 |
| Glass | 0.92 |
| Wood | 0.90 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often assume ε = 1 (blackbody) for all surfaces. For accurate radiometric measurement, the *correct emissivity* of the target must be input into the thermal camera software. **Wrong emissivity = wrong temperature reading.**

### 2.1.3 IR Detector Physics — How Heat Becomes Signal

A thermal imaging camera uses one of two main detector classes:

**A) Thermal Detectors (Bolometers, Thermopiles, Pyroelectric)**
- Absorb IR energy → temperature rise → change in electrical property
- Examples: **Vanadium Oxide (VOx)** and **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)** microbolometers
- Operate at **room temperature** (uncooled) — cheaper, more common
- Response time: ~10–20 ms (slower)

**B) Photon (Quantum) Detectors**
- IR photon directly excites electron across a bandgap → measurable photocurrent
- Materials: **InSb (Indium Antimonide)**, **HgCdTe (Mercury Cadmium Telluride)**, **QWIPs**
- Require **cryogenic cooling** (typically 77 K using liquid nitrogen or a Stirling cooler)
- Much faster (ns) and more sensitive, but **expensive**

### 2.1.4 Block Diagram of a Thermal Camera

1. **IR Optics (Germanium/Zinc Selenide Lenses):** Focuses incoming IR rays onto the detector array.
2. **Detector Array (Focal Plane Array — FPA):** Common formats are **320×240** to **1024×768** pixels.
3. **Read-Out Integrated Circuit (ROIC):** Multiplexes the analog signals from each pixel.
4. **Signal Processing Electronics:** Performs amplification, A/D conversion, non-uniformity correction (NUC), and bad-pixel replacement.
5. **Display:** Presents a colorized (rainbow, ironbow, grayscale) image on an LCD viewfinder.

## 2.2 Calorimetry — The Theory of Heat Accounting

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Heat Equations

| Physical Process | Governing Equation | Variables |
|------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Sensible Heat (Temperature Change) | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | m = mass, c = specific heat, ΔT = temperature change |
| Latent Heat (Phase Change) | $Q = m \cdot L$ | L = specific latent heat (J/kg) |
| Heat Transfer Rate | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | h = heat transfer coefficient |
| Electrical Heat (Resistive) | $Q = V \cdot I \cdot t = I^{2} R t$ | V, I, R, t — electrical quantities |

### 2.2.2 Conservation of Energy in a Calorimeter

For an *adiabatic* (perfectly insulated) calorimeter:

$$Q_{\text{lost by hot body}} = Q_{\text{gained by cold body}}$$

$$m_{h} c_{h} (T_{h,i} - T_{f}) = m_{c} c_{c} (T_{f} - T_{c,i})$$

In practice, the calorimeter itself absorbs some heat. The **Water Equivalent (W)** of a calorimeter is the mass of water that would absorb/give the same amount of heat as the calorimeter:

$$W = m_{\text{cal}} \cdot c_{\text{cal}}$$

The effective heat balance becomes:

$$(m + W) c_{\text{water}} (T_{f} - T_{c,i}) = m_{h} c_{h} (T_{h,i} - T_{f})$$

### 2.2.3 Heat of Combustion (Calorific Value)

The **Calorific Value (CV)** of a fuel is the heat released per unit mass (or volume) when it undergoes complete combustion. It is of two types:

- **Gross/High Calorific Value (GCV/HCV):** Heat released assuming water vapor in products condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered).
- **Net/Low Calorific Value (NCV/LCV):** Heat released assuming water vapor exits as vapor (latent heat lost).

**Approximate relationship (Dulong's formula for solid fuels):**

$$\text{GCV} = \frac{33800 \, C + 144200 \, \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9410 \, S}{100} \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Sulfur.

### 2.2.4 Types of Calorimeters

#### A) Bomb Calorimeter (Constant Volume)
- Used for solid and liquid fuels.
- Fuel placed in a sealed "bomb" (stainless steel crucible) filled with O₂ at **30 atm**.
- Ignited electrically; heat released warms surrounding water.
- **CV = (W + w)·(T₂ − T₁) / m**, where corrections for fuse wire, acid formation, and radiation are applied.

#### B) Junkers Calorimeter (Constant Pressure / Gas Calorimeter)
- Used for gaseous fuels.
- Known volume of gas is burned; the heat is absorbed by continuously flowing water.
- Measures **NCV** directly.
- **CV = m_w · c_w · (T_out − T_in) / V_gas**

#### C) Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC)
- Modern laboratory instrument.
- Measures heat flow as a function of temperature/time.
- Used for polymers, pharmaceuticals, phase transition studies.

#### D) Adiabatic Calorimeter
- Surroundings are maintained at the same temperature as the calorimeter → zero heat loss.
- Used for precise scientific work (e.g., specific heat of gases at low temperatures).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Blackbody) | $E = \sigma T^{4}$ | W/m² | Total emitted power per unit area |
| 2 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Gray Body) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | W/m² | Real object with ε < 1 |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K | Finding peak emission wavelength |
| 4 | Planck's Law | $E_{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}(e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1)}$ | W/(m²·m) | Spectral radiance |
| 5 | Sensible Heat | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | J | Heating/cooling of a body |
| 6 | Latent Heat | $Q = m L$ | J | Phase change at constant T |
| 7 | Bomb Calorimeter CV | $CV = \frac{(W+w)(T_{2}-T_{1})}{m}$ | J/kg | Calorific value of solid/liquid fuel |
| 8 | Junkers Calorimeter CV | $CV = \frac{m_{w} c_{w} (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})}{V_{\text{gas}}}$ | J/m³ | Calorific value of gaseous fuel |
| 9 | Dulong's Formula | $\text{GCV} = 33800C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9410S$ (all divided by 100) | kJ/kg | Estimating GCV from elemental analysis |
| 10 | Radiation Heat Transfer | $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$ | W | Net heat exchange between two surfaces |
| 11 | Net Radiant Power (camera) | $P_{\text{net}} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4} - (1-\varepsilon) P_{\text{env}}$ | W | Detector receives target + reflected ambient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Values to Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**
> - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$
> - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - $k_{B} = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
> - $b_{\text{Wien}} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$
> - 1 cal = 4.186 J
> - $c_{\text{water}} = 4186 \text{ J·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal Imaging:** Preventive maintenance of high-voltage transmission lines (hot joints = failure), firefighter vision through smoke, R&D of composite materials, photovoltaic panel defect detection, breast cancer screening (thermography is non-ionizing, hence safer than X-ray mammography for repeated use).
- **Calorimetry:** Quality control in coal-fired thermal power plants (daily CV tests), food industry (calorie labelling on packaged foods), pharmaceutical stability testing, rocket propellant energy content, HVAC system sizing, and energy auditing of buildings.
