## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the working principle of a C-type Bourdon tube pressure gauge. List any two materials used for its construction.*

**Model Answer:**

The C-type Bourdon tube is a curved, flattened hollow metal tube sealed at the tip and open at the socket to the process pressure. When pressure enters the tube, it tends to **change the flattened cross-section toward a circular shape**, which produces a **bending moment** that uncurls (straightens) the tube. The tip deflection is transferred via a mechanical linkage to a pointer that indicates pressure on a calibrated dial.

**Materials used:**
1. **Phosphor bronze** — for low-pressure applications (up to ~60 bar).
2. **Stainless steel / Beryllium copper / Monel / Inconel** — for high-pressure, high-temperature, and corrosive services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Principle (2 marks) + Materials (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Dead Weight Tester? Why is it called a primary standard?*

**Model Answer:**

A Dead Weight Tester (DWT) is a precision instrument used to generate a **known, highly accurate reference pressure** by applying known calibrated weights on a precision piston of known effective area, immersed in hydraulic fluid. The generated pressure is given by $P = W/A_e = mg/A_e$.

It is called a **primary standard** because it **derives pressure directly from the fundamental SI base quantities** — mass, length (area), and time (gravity) — *without* needing calibration against any other pressure instrument. The pressure value is computed from first principles, giving traceability to the SI system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Reason for being primary standard (1.5 marks).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat functional block diagram the construction and working of a C-type Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Derive the relationship between applied pressure and tip deflection.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the working principle of a Fiber Optic Pressure Sensor based on microbending. State its advantages over conventional pressure sensors.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction of C-Type Bourdon Tube (4 marks):**

The C-type Bourdon tube consists of:
1. **Bourdon Tube** — A curved, hollow metal tube of elliptical (flattened) cross-section, usually in a 250°–270° arc. Sealed at the tip, open at the socket.
2. **Socket** — The open end connects to the process line via a threaded connection.
3. **Linkage Mechanism** — A connecting link transmits the tip motion to the sector gear.
4. **Sector and Pinion** — The sector gear meshes with a small pinion on the pointer shaft, amplifying the tip movement.
5. **Pointer and Dial** — The pointer rotates over a calibrated scale, indicating pressure directly.
6. **Case and Window** — Provides mechanical protection and visibility.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

When process pressure $P$ enters the socket, it exerts a distributed force on the inner walls of the flattened tube. This produces a bending moment that tends to **uncurl** the tube (because a circular cross-section offers a more uniform stress distribution). The tip moves outward by a small displacement $\Delta r$. This displacement is amplified by the linkage and converted into a pointer rotation $\theta$ on the dial.

**Tip Deflection Relation (1 mark — qualitative):**

The tip displacement of a C-type Bourdon tube for small deflections can be approximated as:
$$\Delta r = \frac{P \cdot a^2 \cdot b \cdot R^2 \cdot \alpha}{E \cdot I}$$

where $a, b$ are the half-dimensions of the elliptical cross-section, $R$ is the radius of curvature, $\alpha$ is the angle subtended, $E$ is Young's Modulus, and $I$ is the area moment of inertia.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Fiber Optic Pressure Sensor — Microbending Principle (5 marks):**

A fiber optic pressure sensor uses a **multimode optical fiber** placed between two plates, one of which is **corrugated** (toothed). An LED injects light into one end; a photodetector measures transmitted light at the other.

**Working Steps:**
1. Light propagates through the fiber via total internal reflection.
2. Pressure $P$ applied to the corrugated plate forces the fiber into a periodic microbending pattern with spatial period $\Lambda$.
3. The bends cause coupling between guided modes and radiation modes.
4. **Light intensity at the detector decreases** as pressure increases.
5. The relationship is approximately: $I_{out} = I_{in}(1 - K P)$, where $K$ is the sensitivity constant.
6. **Matching condition** for maximum sensitivity: $\Lambda = 2a/(NA)^2$ where $a$ is the core radius and $NA$ is the numerical aperture.

**Advantages over Conventional Sensors (2 marks):**

1. **Immune to EMI/RFI** — Optical signal is not affected by electromagnetic interference.
2. **Intrinsically Safe** — No electrical energy at the sensing point; ideal for explosive/hazardous atmospheres (e.g., oil rigs, chemical plants).
3. **Suitable for High Temperature & Corrosive Environments** — Silica fiber tolerates temperatures >500°C.
4. **Small Size, Lightweight, Chemically Inert.**
5. **Can be multiplexed** — Multiple sensors on a single fiber using WDM/TDM techniques.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** *Explain the construction and working of a capacitance-type Differential Pressure Transmitter. Show how its 4–20 mA output is derived for level and flow measurement.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the principle, construction, and operation of a Dead Weight Tester. A DWT has a piston of effective area 0.5 cm². Calculate the pressure generated when a total mass of 10 kg is loaded. Take g = 9.81 m/s².* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Construction of Capacitance-Type DPT (3 marks):**

A capacitance-type DPT consists of:
1. **Two pressure capsules** — High Pressure (HP) and Low Pressure (LP), separated by a thin **sensing diaphragm**.
2. **Two fixed capacitor plates** — Mounted on either side of the diaphragm, forming two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$.
3. **Electronics housing** — Contains the oscillator, demodulator, microcontroller, and 4–20 mA current loop driver.
4. **Process connections** — ¼"NPT or flanged connections for HP and LP lines.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

When $P_H > P_L$, the diaphragm deflects toward the LP side by $\delta$. This decreases $C_1$ (gap increases) and increases $C_2$ (gap decreases). The differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2$ is proportional to $\Delta P$. The bridge/oscillator circuit converts $\Delta C$ into a voltage, which the microcontroller linearizes and outputs as 4–20 mA.

**4–20 mA Output Derivation (2 marks):**

- **For Level/Density (Linear):**
  $$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta P_{max}} \quad \text{(mA)}$$

- **For Flow (Square-Root Extraction):**
  $$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta P_{max}}} \quad \text{(mA)}$$

The square-root extraction is required because flow through an orifice is $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$, but the level/density measurement is directly proportional to $\Delta P$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Principle of Dead Weight Tester (2 marks):**

A DWT generates a known reference pressure by applying accurately known weights on a precision piston of known effective area, immersed in hydraulic fluid. The generated pressure is calculated from first principles as $P = mg / A_e$. Because mass and area can be measured with extreme accuracy (and traceable to SI standards), the DWT serves as a *primary standard* for pressure calibration.

**Construction (1.5 marks):**

1. **Piston-Cylinder Assembly** — Precision-machined with very tight clearance (typically <5 µm).
2. **Weight Carrier** — Platform on the piston to stack calibrated weights.
3. **Calibrated Weights** — Precisely known masses (often in steps).
4. **Screw Pump / Hydraulic Press** — Generates the pressure to lift the piston.
5. **Test Port** — Connection to the instrument under test.
6. **Oil Reservoir & Bleed Valves** — For filling and air removal.

**Operation (1.5 marks):**

1. Fill the system with clean hydraulic oil; bleed air.
2. Connect the instrument under calibration to the test port.
3. Place known weights on the piston.
4. Operate the screw pump until the piston **floats and rotates freely**.
5. Read the IUT indication; it should match the calculated pressure.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

**Given:**
- Effective area: $A_e = 0.5$ cm² $= 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m² $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
- Total mass: $m = 10$ kg
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Calculate force due to gravity:
$$W = m \cdot g = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate generated pressure:
$$P = \frac{W}{A_e} = \frac{98.1}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$P = 1.962 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.962 \text{ MPa} \approx 19.62 \text{ bar}$$

**Result:** $P \approx 19.62$ bar (or **1.962 MPa**).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Bourdon Tube — Don't confuse "tube straightening" with "tube bending".** Students often write "tube bends more" — it is the opposite. Pressure *uncurls* the curved tube.
> 2. **Microbending — Don't forget the matching condition.** Simply stating "light loss occurs" is incomplete. Mention the **spatial wavelength matching** condition $\Lambda = 2a/(NA)^2$ to get full marks.
> 3. **DPT — Don't forget the square-root extraction for flow.** Writing $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \Delta P/\Delta P_{max}$ for a flow application will cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **DWT — Don't use $g = 10$ m/s².** KTU expects either $g = 9.81$ or $g = 9.80665$ m/s². Using 10 is a 0.5-mark deduction in numerical problems.
> 5. **Units — Always convert cm² to m² in DWT calculations.** Forgetting unit conversion is the most common error in numerical problems.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Bourdon Tube:**
- Curved, flattened, hollow metal tube.
- Pressure → uncurling → tip deflection → mechanical amplification → pointer.
- Materials: Phosphor bronze (low P), SS/Monel/Inconel (high P/corrosive).
- Non-linear; mechanism provides linearization.
- Range: 0.5 to 7000 bar; Accuracy: ±0.1% to ±2% FS.

✅ **Fiber Optic Pressure Sensor:**
- Microbending principle: pressure → light loss.
- Matching condition: $\Lambda_{opt} = 2a / (NA)^2$.
- Advantages: EMI immune, intrinsically safe, high-temp tolerant, multiplexable.
- Output: $I_{out} = I_{in}(1 - K P)$.

✅ **Differential Pressure Transmitter:**
- Capacitance type is the industry standard.
- Two fixed plates + central sensing diaphragm → differential capacitance.
- Output: 4–20 mA (linear for level/density, square-root for flow).
- $I = 4 + 16 \cdot (\Delta P / \Delta P_{max})$ for level.
- $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / \Delta P_{max}}$ for flow.

✅ **Dead Weight Tester:**
- Primary standard for pressure calibration.
- Principle: $P = mg / A_e$ (mass, gravity, area).
- Piston must float and rotate for equilibrium.
- Accuracy: ±0.015% to ±0.05%.
- Components: Piston, cylinder, weights, screw pump, oil, test port.

✅ **Key Constants & Units:**
- 1 bar $= 10^5$ Pa $= 100$ kPa.
- $g = 9.80665$ m/s² (standard) or $9.81$ m/s² (practical).
- NA (Numerical Aperture) is dimensionless.
- 4–20 mA is the standard industrial current loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Measurement - Thermal Conductivity Gauge, Pirani Gauge
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vacuum Measurement - Thermal Conductivity Gauge, Pirani Gauge

# Vacuum Measurement: Thermal Conductivity & Pirani Gauges

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vacuum Classification (Foundation Concept)

Before diving into the gauges, it is critical to understand the vacuum regimes in which thermal conductivity gauges operate. According to the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** classification:

| Vacuum Regime | Pressure Range (Torr) | Pressure Range (Pa) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rough Vacuum** | 760 → 1 Torr | $10^{5}$ → $10^{2}$ Pa |
| **Medium Vacuum** | 1 → $10^{-3}$ Torr | $10^{2}$ → $10^{-1}$ Pa |
| **High Vacuum (HV)** | $10^{-3}$ → $10^{-8}$ Torr | $10^{-1}$ → $10^{-6}$ Pa |
| **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV)** | $< 10^{-8}$ Torr | $< 10^{-6}$ Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity Gauges (TCG)**, including the Pirani gauge, operate strictly in the **medium vacuum** range, typically from **$10^{-4}$ Torr to 1 Torr**. They are useless at atmospheric pressure and saturate below $10^{-4}$ Torr because thermal conductivity becomes independent of pressure at very low pressures.

### 1.2 Thermal Conductivity Gauge — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *Thermal Conductivity Gauge (TCG)* is a secondary vacuum measurement device that determines gas pressure by exploiting the **pressure-dependent thermal conductivity of gases** in the transitional pressure regime, where the mean free path of gas molecules is comparable to the characteristic dimensions of the gauge chamber.

### 1.3 The Pirani Gauge — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The *Pirani Gauge* (invented by **Marcello Pirani** in **1906**) is a specific, widely-used implementation of the Thermal Conductivity Gauge. It measures vacuum by sensing the **resistance change of a heated filament** whose heat loss (and thus temperature) depends on the thermal conductivity of the surrounding gas, which in turn depends on the pressure.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sweating Finger Thermometer"

Imagine you are holding a hot cup of coffee on a **humid day** versus a **dry winter day**.

- On the **humid day**, the air has many molecules close together. Heat from your finger is carried *away quickly* by these molecules colliding with it (high thermal conductivity at moderate pressure). Your finger feels cold.
- On a **dry, low-pressure day** (or in a vacuum), there are *very few molecules* to carry heat away. Your finger stays warm.

A **Pirani gauge is essentially an electrically-heated "finger"**:
1. The "finger" (filament) is heated to a fixed temperature.
2. We measure how much **electric current (or voltage)** is needed to maintain that temperature.
3. If gas pressure is **high** (more molecules) → more heat is stolen → we need **more power** to keep the filament hot.
4. If gas pressure is **low** (fewer molecules) → less heat is stolen → we need **less power**.

The amount of power required is the indirect measure of **vacuum (pressure)**.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 760 \text{ Torr} = 1.01325 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$
- **1 Torr** = $1 \text{ mmHg}$ = $133.322 \text{ Pa}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics: Why Does Thermal Conductivity Depend on Pressure?

The thermal conductivity of a gas at low pressure is governed by the **kinetic theory of gases** and the concept of **mean free path ($\lambda$)**.

### The Mean Free Path
The mean free path is the average distance a gas molecule travels between successive collisions.

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} P}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = Mean free path (m)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $d$ = Effective molecular diameter (m)
- $P$ = Pressure (Pa)

### Three Pressure Regimes

- **High Pressure Regime** ($\lambda \ll D$, where $D$ is the chamber dimension): Gas behaves as a continuous fluid. Thermal conductivity is **constant** (independent of P). → **TCG does not work here.**
- **Transitional Regime** ($\lambda \approx D$): Heat transfer depends on the number of molecules that can reach the hot surface. Thermal conductivity **becomes proportional to pressure**. → **This is the Pirani operating range.**
- **Molecular Flow Regime** ($\lambda \gg D$): Very few molecules reach the hot surface. Heat transfer becomes constant (and very small). → **TCG saturates here.**

## 2.2 Heat Transfer Mechanisms in a Pirani Gauge

The heat loss from the heated filament occurs through three parallel paths:

1. **Gas Conduction ($Q_{gas}$):** Pressure-dependent. This is the **measurand**.
2. **Conduction through Lead Wires ($Q_{lead}$):** Pressure-independent (constant loss).
3. **Radiation ($Q_{rad}$):** Pressure-independent, depends on $T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann law).

### Energy Balance Equation

$$P_{electrical} = Q_{gas} + Q_{lead} + Q_{rad}$$

In the **transitional pressure regime**:

$$Q_{gas} = K \cdot P \cdot (T_{filament} - T_{wall})$$

Where $K$ is a geometry and gas-type dependent constant.

## 2.3 Operational Modes of Pirani Gauge

The Pirani gauge can be operated in **three distinct modes**:

| Mode | What is Held Constant | What is Measured | Industrial Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Constant Voltage (CV)** | Voltage $V$ across filament | Current $I$ (or resistance $R$) | Simple, but nonlinear |
| **Constant Current (CI)** | Current $I$ through filament | Voltage $V$ (or resistance $R$) | Better linearity |
| **Constant Temperature (CT)** | Filament Temperature $T_f$ / Resistance $R_f$ | Supply Power / Voltage | **Industry Standard**, best accuracy, fastest response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** The **Constant Temperature (CT) Pirani gauge** is preferred in modern industrial applications because it offers the highest accuracy, fastest response time, and best long-term stability against ambient temperature variations.

## 2.4 Wheatstone Bridge Circuit (The Heart of Pirani Gauge)

The Pirani filament ($R_1$) is placed as one arm of a **Wheatstone Bridge**:

- $R_1$ = Pirani filament (exposed to vacuum)
- $R_2$ = Dummy/reference filament (sealed at a known reference vacuum, $\sim 10^{-4}$ Torr)
- $R_3, R_4$ = Precision fixed resistors

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

When the bridge is balanced, $V_{out} = 0$. Any pressure change alters $R_1$ (due to temperature change), unbalancing the bridge and producing a measurable $V_{out}$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean Free Path | $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} P}$ | $\lambda$ in m, $P$ in Pa |
| 2 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D}$ | $D$ = characteristic dimension |
| 3 | Operating Range | $10^{-4} \le P \le 1$ **Torr** | Medium Vacuum |
| 4 | Gas Heat Loss | $Q_{gas} = K \cdot P \cdot \Delta T$ | $K$ depends on gas & geometry |
| 5 | Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation | $Q_{rad} = \epsilon \sigma A (T_f^{4} - T_w^{4})$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| 6 | Filament Resistance (RTD) | $R_f = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T_f - T_0)]$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient |
| 7 | Bridge Balance | $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$ | For zero output voltage |
| 8 | Output Voltage (Wheatstone) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)$ | Galvanometer or instrumentation amp |
| 9 | Sensitivity Factor | $S = \frac{dV_{out}/V_{in}}{dP}$ | Higher in CT mode |
| 10 | Gas Correction Factor | $P_{effective} = P_{indicated} \times C_{gas}$ | Air = 1.0, He = 0.85, Ar = 1.4 (typical) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Semiconductor Industry:** Monitoring load-lock chambers and process tool forelines.
- **Vacuum Furnaces & Metallurgy:** Annealing, brazing, sintering.
- **Cryogenics & Space Simulation:** Testing of components in space-like environments.
- **Food Packaging:** Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) requires medium-vacuum verification.
- **Refrigeration/HVAC:** Evacuation of refrigerant lines before charging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Thermal Conductivity Depends on Pressure

We derive the heat transfer rate from a hot filament to the surrounding cold wall through a rarefied gas.

**Step 1: Define Molecular Flux**
The number of gas molecules striking a unit area per unit time (Hertz-Knudsen equation):

$$\Phi = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

*Reasoning:* A higher pressure means more molecules per unit volume, hence more molecules strike the wall per unit time.

**Step 2: Energy Carried Per Molecule**
Each molecule that strikes the hot filament leaves with an average energy corresponding to the filament's surface temperature $T_f$ (accommodation coefficient $\alpha_c \approx 1$). Each molecule arriving from the cold wall carries energy corresponding to $T_w$.

$$\Delta E_{per\,molecule} = \alpha_c \cdot \frac{1}{2} k_B (T_f - T_w)$$

**Step 3: Total Heat Flux**
Multiplying molecular flux by energy per molecule:

$$q = \Phi \cdot \Delta E = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} k_B \alpha_c (T_f - T_w)$$

**Step 4: Simplification**

$$q = \alpha_c \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} k_B (T_f - T_w)$$

After algebraic simplification (combining constants):

$$q = \alpha_c \cdot P \cdot \left( \frac{k_B}{2\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \right) (T_f - T_w)$$

$$\boxed{q = K_{gas} \cdot P \cdot (T_f - T_w)}$$

Where $K_{gas} = \dfrac{\alpha_c k_B}{2\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$ is a gas-type-dependent constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Heat flux $q$ is **linearly proportional to pressure $P$** in the transitional regime. This is the foundational equation that makes the Pirani gauge a *linear* (approximately) pressure transducer in its operating range.

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Voltage of the Pirani Wheatstone Bridge

Let us derive the output voltage when pressure changes and unbalances the bridge.

**Given:**
- Bridge arms: $R_1$ (Pirani), $R_2$ (dummy/reference), $R_3$, $R_4$ (fixed precision).
- Excitation voltage: $V_{ex}$.

**Step 1: Voltage Divider (Left Branch)**

$$V_a = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2: Voltage Divider (Right Branch)**

$$V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3: Differential Output**

$$V_{out} = V_a - V_b = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 4: When Bridge is Balanced (Reference Vacuum)**
At $P = P_{ref}$, $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_3 = R_4$, so $V_{out} = 0$.

**Step 5: Change in $R_1$ due to Pressure Change**
Let $\Delta R$ be the small change in filament resistance due to a change in pressure:

$$R_1 = R_2 + \Delta R$$

**Step 6: Substitute into Output Equation**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_2 + \Delta R}{2R_2 + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

**Step 7: Algebraic Simplification**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{2(R_2 + \Delta R) - (2R_2 + \Delta R)}{2(2R_2 + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{2R_2 + 2\Delta R - 2R_2 - \Delta R}{2(2R_2 + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R_2 + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 8: Small-Signal Approximation ($\Delta R \ll R_2$)**

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4 R_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This linear relationship between $V_{out}$ and $\Delta R$ (and thus $\Delta P$) is why the Pirani gauge provides a near-linear output in its mid-range pressures, simplifying calibration and signal conditioning in industrial instrumentation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Pirani gauge operates with a tungsten filament of $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $20\ °C$. The temperature coefficient of resistance is $\alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C. When the vacuum changes, the filament temperature rises from $20\ °C$ to $85\ °C$. Calculate the change in resistance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the RTD linear approximation:

$$R_f = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T_f - T_0)]$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $R_f$ at $85\ °C$:

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (85 - 20)]$$

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 65]$$

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 0.2925]$$

$$R_f = 129.25\ \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Change in resistance:

$$\Delta R = R_f - R_0 = 129.25 - 100 = 29.25\ \Omega$$

**Step 4:** With $V_{ex} = 6\text{ V}$ and $R_2 = 100\ \Omega$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{6 \times 29.25}{4 \times 100} = \frac{175.5}{400} = 0.439 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 0.44 \text{ V}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TC Gauge vs Other Vacuum Gauges

| Parameter | Pirani (TCG) | Thermocouple | McLeod | Ionization Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operating Range** | $10^{-4}$ – 1 Torr | $10^{-3}$ – 1 Torr | $10^{-6}$ – 10 Torr | $10^{-10}$ – $10^{-3}$ Torr |
| **Principle** | Resistance change | EMF of TC | Boyle's Law (Mercury) | Ion current |
| **Type** | Indirect (thermal) | Indirect (thermal) | **Primary (Absolute)** | Indirect (ionization) |
| **Accuracy** | ±10–20% | ±15–25% | ±1–5% (highest) | ±10–30% |
| **Gas Type Dependence** | Yes (gas-specific cal.) | Yes | **No (absolute)** | Yes |
| **Response Time** | Fast (~100 ms) | Slow (~1 s) | Very Slow | Moderate |
| **Industrial Use** | Forelines, load locks | Rough vacuum | Calibration (lab) | HV/UHV systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Pirani Gauge Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vacuum Chamber] -->|Low P gas| nodeB[Pirani Filament]
    nodeB -->|Heat Loss varies| nodeC[Filament Temp T_f]
    nodeC -->|Resistance Change| nodeD[Wheatstone Bridge]
    nodeD -->|Differential Voltage| nodeE[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    nodeE -->|Conditioned Signal| nodeF[Microcontroller ADC]
    nodeF -->|Calibrated Curve| nodeG[Digital Display Pressure]
    nodeH[Reference Filament Sealed at P_ref] -->|Compensation| nodeD
    nodeI[Heater Control Loop CT Mode] -->|Maintains T_f Constant| nodeB
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Wheatstone Bridge in Pirani Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Excitation Voltage V_ex] --> node2[Top of Bridge]
    node2 --> node3[Left Arm: R1 Pirani Filament]
    node2 --> node4[Right Arm: R3 Precision Resistor]
    node3 --> node5[Junction Point A]
    node4 --> node6[Junction Point B]
    node5 --> node7[Output V_out to Amplifier]
    node6 --> node7
    node3 --> node8[Bottom Arm R2 Dummy Sealed]
    node4 --> node9[Bottom Arm R4 Precision]
    node8 --> node10[Ground]
    node9 --> node10
    node7 --> node11[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    node11 --> node12[Display / Output]
```

## 4.3 Operational Flow: Constant Temperature Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Vacuum Applied]) --> heat[Heat Filament to Set Point T_set]
    heat --> sensor[Temperature Sensor Reads T_f]
    sensor --> compare{T_f vs T_set}
    compare -->|Lower| increase[Increase Heating Power]
    compare -->|Higher| decrease[Decrease Heating Power]
    compare -->|Equal| stable[Stable State]
    increase --> sensor
    decrease --> sensor
    stable --> measure[Measure Applied Power]
    measure --> convert[Power Proportional to Pressure]
    convert --> display([Display Pressure in Torr / Pa])
```

## 4.4 Physical Construction Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gasport[Vacuum Port Inlet] --> housing[Stainless Steel Housing]
    housing --> filblock[Filament Mounting Block]
    filblock --> fwire[Tungsten or Platinum Filament]
    filblock --> leads[Four Electrical Feedthroughs]
    fwire -->|Resistance changes| leads
    leads --> cable[Shielded Cable to Bridge Circuit]
    housing --> refchamber[Reference Vacuum Chamber]
    refchamber --> dfil[Dummy Filament Sealed at 10^-4 Torr]
    dfil -->|Temperature Compensated Reference| cable
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the working principle of a Pirani gauge. Mention its operating range and list any two advantages.*

**Model Answer:**
The Pirani gauge operates on the principle that the **thermal conductivity of a gas varies with pressure** in the medium vacuum range. It consists of a heated filament placed inside the vacuum system. Heat loss from the filament to the surrounding gas depends on the number of gas molecules (i.e., pressure). A change in pressure alters the filament's temperature and hence its electrical resistance, which is measured using a Wheatstone bridge circuit.

**Operating Range:** $10^{-4}$ Torr to 1 Torr (medium vacuum).

**Advantages:**
1. Continuous, real-time pressure indication.
2. Compact, robust, and inexpensive compared to ionization gauges.
3. No high-voltage or ionizing radiation hazard.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Mean Free Path. How does it relate to the operating principle of a thermal conductivity gauge?*

**Model Answer:**
**Mean Free Path ($\lambda$):** The average distance a gas molecule travels between two successive collisions with other molecules.

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^{2} P}$$

In a thermal conductivity gauge, when the mean free path of gas molecules is **comparable to the gap between the hot filament and the cold wall** (transitional regime), the rate of heat transfer from the filament becomes **directly proportional to pressure**. When $\lambda$ is much smaller than the gap (high pressure), heat transfer is independent of pressure (gauge saturates). When $\lambda$ is much larger than the gap (very low pressure), again heat transfer is constant. Hence, the gauge is effective only in the transitional regime.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Pirani gauge used for vacuum measurement. Discuss the role of the Wheatstone bridge and the reference (dummy) filament. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a Pirani gauge Wheatstone bridge and show that for small resistance changes, the output is approximately linear with pressure change. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Construction:**
1. **Filament:** A thin wire of **tungsten** or **platinum** is mounted inside a chamber connected to the vacuum system. It is typically $50\text{–}100\ \mu m$ in diameter.
2. **Reference Filament:** A duplicate filament sealed in a small envelope evacuated to a known reference pressure ($\sim 10^{-4}$ Torr) provides compensation for ambient temperature fluctuations.
3. **Wheatstone Bridge:** The two filaments form two arms of a bridge along with two precision fixed resistors.
4. **Housing:** Stainless steel with a standard vacuum flange (KF / CF) for connection.

**Working:**
- The filament is heated by passing a current through it.
- At a reference (low) pressure, the bridge is balanced. The filament temperature is $T_{ref}$.
- As the system pressure increases, more gas molecules collide with the filament, **increasing convective heat loss**.
- This cools the filament, reducing its temperature and hence its electrical resistance ($R_f = R_0[1+\alpha\Delta T]$).
- The resistance change unbalances the bridge, producing an output voltage $V_{out}$.
- $V_{out}$ is calibrated against known pressures to provide the pressure reading.

**Role of Wheatstone Bridge:**
Converts the small resistance change into a measurable differential voltage with high common-mode rejection and temperature compensation via the dummy arm.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

From our derivation in Section 3.2:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R_2 + \Delta R)}$$

For small $\Delta R$:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4 R_2}$$

Now, the resistance change $\Delta R$ is related to temperature change $\Delta T$, which in turn is related to pressure change $\Delta P$. Since the heat loss is proportional to pressure in the transitional regime:

$$\Delta T \propto \Delta P \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta R \propto \Delta T$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \propto \Delta P}$$

This confirms the **linear approximation** of the Pirani gauge output over its mid-range pressures.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the three different modes of operation of a Pirani gauge (constant voltage, constant current, and constant temperature). Compare their relative merits. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** A Pirani gauge has a tungsten filament of resistance $100\ \Omega$ at $20\ °C$. The temperature coefficient is $4.5 \times 10^{-3}/°C$. The bridge excitation voltage is $6\ \text{V}$ and the dummy arm resistance is also $100\ \Omega$. If the filament temperature rises to $90\ °C$ due to a change in vacuum, calculate (i) the change in filament resistance and (ii) the approximate output voltage. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Mode 1 — Constant Voltage (CV):**
- A fixed voltage is applied across the filament.
- Pressure change → temperature change → resistance change → current change (measured).
- *Limitation:* Filament temperature is not regulated; output is highly nonlinear at extremes.

**Mode 2 — Constant Current (CI):**
- A fixed current is maintained.
- Pressure change → temperature change → resistance change → voltage change (measured).
- *Limitation:* Slow response; filament can burn out at low pressure (overheating).

**Mode 3 — Constant Temperature (CT) — Industry Standard:**
- An electronic feedback loop maintains the filament at a constant resistance (i.e., constant temperature).
- The electrical power required to maintain this temperature is measured.
- *Merits:* Highest accuracy, fastest response, best stability, widest usable range, protects filament.

| Mode | Accuracy | Response | Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CV | Low | Medium | Poor |
| CI | Medium | Slow | Medium |
| **CT** | **High** | **Fast** | **Excellent** |

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $T_0 = 20\ °C$, $\alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}/°C$, $T_f = 90\ °C$, $V_{ex} = 6\ \text{V}$, $R_2 = 100\ \Omega$.

**(i) Change in Resistance:**

$$R_f = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T_f - T_0)]$$

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (90 - 20)]$$

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 70]$$

$$R_f = 100 [1 + 0.315] = 131.5\ \Omega$$

$$\Delta R = 131.5 - 100 = 31.5\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4 R_2} = \frac{6 \times 31.5}{4 \times 100}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.4725\ \text{V} \approx 0.47\ \text{V}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Constant Voltage vs Constant Current vs Constant Temperature modes** — examiners frequently set 3-mark questions testing this distinction. State clearly *what is held constant* and *what is measured* in each.
> 2. **Always convert units properly:** $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.322 \text{ Pa}$. Mixing units is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Pirani is NOT an absolute gauge.** It must be calibrated against a primary standard (e.g., McLeod gauge). Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks in descriptive questions.
> 4. **Operating range is $10^{-4}$ to 1 Torr**, not the full vacuum range. Writing "measures all vacuum levels" is incorrect.
> 5. **In derivation questions**, do not skip algebraic steps. Examiners award step-marks (method marks) generously in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Pirani Gauge** is a **Thermal Conductivity Gauge (TCG)** operating on the principle of pressure-dependent thermal conductivity of gases.
- [ ] **Operating Range:** $10^{-4}$ Torr to 1 Torr (Medium Vacuum only).
- [ ] **Transducer Element:** Tungsten or Platinum filament whose resistance is temperature-dependent.
- [ ] **Heat Loss Equation:** $Q_{gas} = K \cdot P \cdot (T_f - T_w)$ — **linearly proportional to pressure** in the transitional regime.
- [ ] **Mean Free Path:** $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$ — must be comparable to chamber dimension for gauge to work.
- [ ] **Three Operating Modes:** Constant Voltage, Constant Current, **Constant Temperature (preferred)**.
- [ ] **Wheatstone Bridge:** Used to convert resistance change to voltage; balanced at reference vacuum.
- [ ] **Reference (Dummy) Filament:** Sealed at $10^{-4}$ Torr to compensate for ambient temperature variations.
- [ ] **Output Voltage (small-signal):** $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4 R_2}$.
- [ ] **Gauge is GAS-DEPENDENT:** Calibration differs for Air, He, Ar, N₂. Use a gas correction factor.
- [ ] **Limitations:** Not absolute (requires calibration); saturates above 1 Torr and below $10^{-4}$ Torr.
- [ ] **Advantages:** Compact, rugged, continuous output, no high voltage, no mercury (unlike McLeod).
- [ ] **Constant Temperature Mode** is the industry standard — remember its advantages for KTU descriptive answers.
- [ ] **Filament Material:** Tungsten (most common) or Platinum — high temperature coefficient of resistance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Measurement - Electromagnetic Flow Meters, Ultrasonic Flow Meters, Thermal Mass Flow Meters, Flow Transmitter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow Measurement - Electromagnetic Flow Meters, Ultrasonic Flow Meters, Thermal Mass Flow Meters, Flow Transmitter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electromagnetic Flow Meter (EMF / Magmeter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** An Electromagnetic Flow Meter is a volumetric flow measuring device that operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of flow, an EMF (voltage) is induced across two electrodes mounted on the pipe wall, which is directly proportional to the average velocity of the fluid.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine pushing a metal rod through a magnetic field (like a magnet's gap). As the rod moves, electrons in it experience a force and accumulate at one end, creating a measurable voltage. Now replace the rod with a flowing conductive liquid (water with minerals, slurries, acids) — the same physics applies. The faster the liquid flows, the more voltage appears at the electrodes. The pipe itself doesn't need to be conductive — only the fluid must be.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants and Metrics:**
> - Magnetic flux density **B** is typically **0.1 to 1 Tesla** (often 0.5 T in industrial units).
> - Minimum fluid conductivity required: **≥ 5 μS/cm** (water is ~50 μS/cm; deionized water fails).
> - Standard accuracy class: **±0.5% to ±1% of rate**.

---

### 1.2 Ultrasonic Flow Meter (USM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** An Ultrasonic Flow Meter measures fluid velocity by transmitting and receiving high-frequency acoustic pulses (typically **0.5 to 10 MHz**) through the flowing medium. The two dominant principles are **(a) Transit-Time (Time-of-Flight)** and **(b) Doppler Shift** methods.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Picture two people standing on opposite banks of a flowing river throwing a ball to each other. The person throwing downstream gets the ball back faster (current helps); the upstream thrower struggles (current resists). The difference in travel times reveals the current speed. Ultrasonic meters do exactly this with sound waves instead of balls.

> [!NOTE]
> Two operation modes:
> - **Transit-Time (Most Accurate):** Used for **clean liquids** like water, oil, chemicals.
> - **Doppler Shift:** Used for **dirty/slurry fluids** with suspended particles or bubbles that act as reflectors.

---

### 1.3 Thermal Mass Flow Meter (TMFM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A Thermal Mass Flow Meter measures the **mass flow rate directly** (not volumetric) by introducing a known amount of heat into the flowing stream and measuring the resulting temperature difference. It is based on the convective heat transfer principle and is independent of fluid density and (to a large extent) pressure.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a hairdryer. The air that flows over the heating coil takes heat away — more air = more cooling. If you measure how much the heating coil cools down for a given electrical power input, you can figure out how much air is passing by. A thermal flow meter works the same way: heat a sensor, see how much it cools due to flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Application:** Almost exclusively used for **gases** — compressed air, natural gas, nitrogen, argon, and biogas. Best suited for low-to-medium flow rates in clean gases.

---

### 1.4 Flow Transmitter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A Flow Transmitter is an electronic instrument that accepts the **low-level signal** (millivolts, frequency, RTD/thermocouple readings) from a flow sensor (primary element) and converts it into a **standardized industrial output signal**, typically a **4–20 mA current loop** with optional digital communication protocols like **HART, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, or PROFIBUS**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of the transmitter as a **universal translator** for a process plant. The flow sensor speaks a "weak whisper" (millivolts), but the control room's PLC and DCS need a "standardized, strong, noise-resistant language" (4–20 mA current). The translator (transmitter) boosts the whisper into the strong standard language, so the message travels thousands of meters of cable without losing meaning. It also performs **linearization, temperature compensation, and square-root extraction** for differential pressure-based flow meters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern "Smart" Transmitters** offer:
> - Digital trim and remote calibration
> - Self-diagnostics
> - Multi-variable sensing (flow + pressure + temperature)
> - HART/Fieldbus communication (bi-directional)

---

### 1.5 Section 1 — Quick Conceptual Map

| Device | Measures | Working Principle | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electromagnetic Flow Meter | Volumetric flow of conductive liquids | Faraday's Law of Induction | mV → mA via transmitter |
| Ultrasonic Flow Meter | Volumetric flow of clean or dirty fluids | Transit-Time / Doppler Effect | Frequency / mA |
| Thermal Mass Flow Meter | Mass flow of gases | Convective Heat Transfer | mV / RTD → mA |
| Flow Transmitter | Not a sensor — a signal conditioner | Current loop & digital comm. | 4–20 mA + HART |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Theory

**Operating Principle — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction:**

When a conductor of length **L** moves with velocity **v** perpendicular to a magnetic flux density **B**, the induced EMF is:

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

For a circular pipe of diameter **D**, the conductive fluid itself acts as the moving conductor spanning the electrode gap **D**:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Step-by-step logic for the volumetric flow relationship:**

1. The electrodes are placed diametrically opposite, so the conductor length = pipe diameter **D**.
2. The fluid velocity **v** at any point varies across the pipe (laminar flow has parabolic profile), but the EMF is proportional to the **average velocity** because the magnetic field and geometry produce an averaging effect — this is a unique advantage of EMF.
3. The volumetric flow rate **Q** is the average velocity times the cross-sectional area:
$$Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$
4. Solving for **v** and substituting:
$$v = \frac{E}{B \cdot D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{\pi D}{4B} \cdot E$$
5. This shows that **Q is directly proportional to E** — no square-root extraction is required (unlike orifice plates). This is a major KTU-favorite point.

**Constructional Details:**

- **Liner:** Insulating material (PTFE, rubber, ceramic) inside the pipe — electrically isolates the fluid from the metal body.
- **Coils:** Generate the magnetic field — usually pulsed DC (to avoid polarization of electrodes).
- **Electrodes:** Two (sometimes three for ground reference) — made of stainless steel, Hastelloy, or platinum.
- **Housing:** Protects the assembly; classified as IP67/IP68 for outdoor/underground use.

**Real-World Utility:** Used extensively in **water treatment plants, chemical industries, pulp & paper, food & beverage, mining slurries, and pharmaceutical** applications. The only limitation is that the fluid **must be electrically conductive**.

---

### 2.2 Ultrasonic Flow Meter — Theory

#### 2.2.1 Transit-Time Method (Time of Flight)

Two transducers (T₁, T₂) are mounted on the outside of the pipe, each acting as both transmitter and receiver. The sound path length is **L**, the sound velocity in the static fluid is **c**, the fluid velocity along the sound path is **v_f**, and the angle of the sound beam with respect to the pipe axis is **θ**.

- **Downstream time** (sound travels with the flow):
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$$
- **Upstream time** (sound travels against the flow):
$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta}$$

The **time difference** Δt = t₂ − t₁ reveals the flow:

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta} - \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta} = \frac{2 L v_f \cos\theta}{c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

Since **v_f ≪ c** (fluid velocity is much less than sound speed in fluid), the term $v_f^2 \cos^2\theta$ is negligible compared to **c²**:

$$\boxed{\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L v_f \cos\theta}{c^2}}$$

Solving for **v_f**:

$$v_f \approx \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$$

Then volumetric flow:

$$Q = v_f \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Transit-Time is so popular in KTU questions:** The math is elegant, the principle is intuitive, and the formula is easy to derive during exams. Also, transit-time is **bidirectional** — sign of Δt tells you flow direction.

#### 2.2.2 Doppler Shift Method

When the transmitted frequency **f₀** hits moving particles (bubbles, dirt, solids), the reflected frequency **f_r** is shifted by an amount:

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 v_f f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v_f = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Doppler meters **require** reflective particles (≥ 100 μm, ≥ 25 ppm concentration). They are unsuitable for clean liquids or pure gases.

**Real-World Utility:** Transit-time meters dominate clean liquid applications (water, oil, LNG). Doppler meters dominate **wastewater, slurries, and pulp stock** in paper mills. Clamp-on versions are popular for retrofit and troubleshooting because they don't require pipe cutting.

---

### 2.3 Thermal Mass Flow Meter — Theory

A heater (sensor 1) is placed in the flow stream, and a temperature sensor (sensor 2) is placed downstream. A second temperature sensor (sensor 3) is placed upstream to compensate for inlet temperature variations. Heat added **P** is related to mass flow by King's law (derived empirically, valid for forced convection):

$$\frac{P}{\Delta T} = A + B \cdot q_m^n$$

Where:
- **A** = conductive heat losses (constant, depends on construction)
- **B** = convective coefficient constant (depends on geometry, fluid properties)
- **n** = exponent, typically **0.5 to 0.7** (≈ 0.5 for laminar flow)
- **q_m** = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- **ΔT** = temperature difference between downstream and upstream sensors

**Two Operational Modes:**

1. **Constant Power Mode:** P is held constant; ΔT varies inversely with q_m.
$$q_m = f\!\left(\frac{1}{\Delta T}\right)$$
2. **Constant Temperature Difference Mode:** ΔT is held constant (by varying P); P is directly proportional to mass flow.
$$P \propto q_m$$

The second mode is more common in modern instruments because it offers better linearity and faster response.

**Real-World Utility:** Thermal mass flow meters are the **de-facto standard for compressed air and natural gas custody transfer** in semiconductor fabs, pharmaceutical plants, and biogas plants. Their biggest advantage is direct mass flow measurement without needing **P** and **T** compensation.

---

### 2.4 Flow Transmitter — Theory

A flow transmitter performs three primary functions:

1. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies weak sensor signals (mV from EMF, RTD resistance from thermal, frequency from ultrasonic).
2. **Linearization:** Many flow sensors (orifice, venturi with DP) have a **square-root** relationship:
$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$
The transmitter often extracts the square root to give a linear 4–20 mA vs. flow output.
3. **Communication:** Generates a **4–20 mA** current proportional to flow and overlays a digital HART signal (Bell 202 standard at 1200 bps).

**Current Loop Equation:**

$$I_{out} = 4 \text{ mA} + \left(\frac{Q - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}}\right) \times 16 \text{ mA}$$

Where:
- **Q_min** = flow at 4 mA (often zero, but can be elevated for reverse-flow suppression)
- **Q_max** = flow at 20 mA (full-scale)

**HART Protocol (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer):**
- Master-slave protocol, 1200 bps.
- Up to 15 slave devices on a single twisted pair.
- Uses Frequency Shift Keying (FSK): 1200 Hz = "1", 2200 Hz = "0".
- Allows remote configuration, calibration, and diagnostics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Question:** "Why is 4 mA used as the lower limit and not 0 mA?"
> **Answer:** 4 mA is the **live zero** — it powers the transmitter electronics, allows distinction between a "true zero reading" and a "broken wire / open circuit" (which would give 0 mA). This is a key KTU viva and exam point.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the following formulas are **high-frequency exam topics** for KTU 2024 scheme Module 3 of PERUT527.

| # | Topic | Formula | Description | Units |

| 4 | Ultrasonic (TT) | $t_1 = \dfrac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$ | Downstream transit time | s |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Output Voltage and Volumetric Flow Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Physics:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states that the EMF induced in a conductor of length **L** moving with velocity **v** perpendicular to a magnetic field **B** is **E = B · L · v** volts.

**Step 1 — Identify the Conductor in an EMF Pipe**

In an electromagnetic flow meter, the conductive fluid itself acts as the moving conductor. The fluid spans the distance between two diametrically opposite electrodes, which equals the pipe diameter **D**. So we replace **L** with **D**:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Relate Velocity to Volumetric Flow**

By continuity equation, the volumetric flow **Q** equals the average velocity times the pipe cross-sectional area **A**:

$$Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Average Velocity**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back into the EMF Equation**

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4B \cdot Q}{\pi D}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Q in Terms of E (Final Form)**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$$

This is the **final KTU-standard form** of the EMF volumetric flow equation. It is **linear in E** — the EMF is a true linear-output flow meter (no square-root extraction needed). The sensitivity of the meter to flow is:

$$S_{EMF} = \frac{dQ}{dE} = \frac{\pi D}{4B}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

Given: Pipe diameter **D = 0.1 m** (100 mm), Magnetic flux density **B = 0.5 T**, Measured EMF **E = 2.5 mV = 2.5 × 10⁻³ V**.

Find: (a) Average fluid velocity, (b) Volumetric flow rate in L/s.

**(a) Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{E}{B \cdot D} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 0.1} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Volumetric Flow:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B} = \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 0.5} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/s (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.3927 \text{ L/s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ultrasonic Transit-Time Flow Velocity

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Physics:** Sound travels through a stationary fluid at speed **c**. When the fluid is moving with velocity **v_f** at angle **θ** to the sound path, the effective sound speed along the path is **c + v_f cos θ** (downstream) and **c − v_f cos θ** (upstream).

**Step 1 — Define the Path Geometry**

The two transducers are mounted on opposite sides of the pipe, separated by an axial distance **L** along the pipe wall. The sound path makes an angle **θ** with the pipe axis (typical values: 30° to 60°).

**Step 2 — Write the Downstream Transit Time**

When sound travels **with** the flow (downstream), the effective speed is greater:

$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Upstream Transit Time**

When sound travels **against** the flow (upstream), the effective speed is less:

$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Time Difference**

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta} - \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Combine Over a Common Denominator**

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{(c + v_f \cos\theta) - (c - v_f \cos\theta)}{(c - v_f \cos\theta)(c + v_f \cos\theta)}$$

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{2 v_f \cos\theta}{c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Approximation v_f ≪ c**

The term $v_f^2 \cos^2\theta$ is on the order of 10⁻⁴ times smaller than $c^2$ (sound in water is ~1500 m/s; typical process flows are < 5 m/s). So:

$$c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta \approx c^2$$

This gives:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L v_f \cos\theta}{c^2}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for v_f (Final Form)**

$$\boxed{v_f = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}}$$

**Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

Given: Path length **L = 0.5 m**, Angle **θ = 45°**, Sound speed in water **c = 1480 m/s**, Time difference **Δt = 50 ns = 50 × 10⁻⁹ s**.

Find: Volumetric flow in a pipe of diameter **D = 0.2 m**.

**Step 8a — Calculate v_f:**

$$v_f = \frac{(1480)^2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.5 \times \cos 45°}$$

$$v_f = \frac{2.1904 \times 10^6 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{1.0 \times 0.7071}$$

$$v_f = \frac{0.10952}{0.7071} = 0.1549 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 8b — Volumetric Flow:**

$$Q = v_f \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 0.1549 \times \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^2}{4} = 0.1549 \times 0.03142 = 4.866 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 4.866 \text{ L/s}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Mass Flow Meter — Direct Mass Flow from Heat Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Physics:** First law of thermodynamics — the electrical power **P** supplied to the heater equals the heat absorbed by the fluid plus the conductive losses. The convective heat loss is proportional to mass flow to some power **n** (empirically determined).

**Step 1 — Heat Balance on the Sensor**

$$P = P_{conduction} + P_{convection} = P_{conduction} + h \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T$$

Where **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), **A_s** is the sensor surface area, and **ΔT** is the temperature rise of the fluid due to the heater.

**Step 2 — Use Empirical Correlation (King's Law)**

For forced convection over a cylinder or wire, the heat transfer coefficient is empirically found to follow:

$$h = A' + B' \cdot q_m^n$$

Where **A'** and **B'** are constants depending on geometry and fluid properties, and **n** ≈ 0.5 (laminar) to 0.7 (turbulent).

**Step 3 — Combine Into King's Law Form**

$$P = (A + B \cdot q_m^n) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Two Operating Modes**

**Mode 1 — Constant Power (P fixed):** Rearranged:

$$\Delta T = \frac{P}{A + B \cdot q_m^n}$$

As **q_m** increases, **ΔT** decreases. The instrument measures **ΔT** and infers **q_m**. Non-linear output requires linearization in the transmitter.

**Mode 2 — Constant ΔT (Closed Loop):** The bridge circuit varies **P** to maintain **ΔT** constant:

$$P = (A + B \cdot q_m^n) \cdot \Delta T_{const}$$

If **A ≪ B · q_m^n** (at sufficient flow), then:

$$P \approx B \cdot q_m^n \cdot \Delta T_{const} \Rightarrow q_m = \left(\frac{P}{B \cdot \Delta T_{const}}\right)^{1/n}$$

This mode is **self-validating**: zero flow corresponds to a known power, providing automatic zero stability.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

Given: A thermal mass flow meter has constants **A = 0.05**, **B = 0.8**, **n = 0.5**. Operating in constant ΔT mode with **ΔT = 10 K**. The power measured is **P = 2.4 W**.

Find: Mass flow rate in kg/s.

$$P = (A + B \cdot q_m^n) \cdot \Delta T$$

$$2.4 = (0.05 + 0.8 \cdot q_m^{0.5}) \times 10$$

$$0.24 = 0.05 + 0.8 \cdot q_m^{0.5}$$

$$0.19 = 0.8 \cdot q_m^{0.5}$$

$$q_m^{0.5} = 0.2375$$

$$q_m = 0.0564 \text{ kg/s} = 56.4 \text{ g/s}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Flow Transmitter 4–20 mA Mapping

**Step 1 — Define the Linear Mapping**

The transmitter linearly maps the measured flow **Q** to current **I** between two endpoints:

- At **Q = Q_min** (zero flow typically): **I = 4 mA**
- At **Q = Q_max** (full scale): **I = 20 mA**

**Step 2 — Use Two-Point Linear Equation**

$$I = I_{min} + (I_{max} - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{Q - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}}$$

$$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{Q - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Example**

A flow transmitter is calibrated for **Q_min = 0 L/min** and **Q_max = 1000 L/min**. If the current reads **12 mA**, what is the flow?

$$12 = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{Q - 0}{1000 - 0}$$

$$8 = 16 \cdot \frac{Q}{1000}$$

$$Q = 500 \text{ L/min} \quad \text{(i.e., 50\% of full scale)}$$

This linear 50% → 12 mA relationship (because 4 + 0.5 × 16 = 12) is the **classic KTU question** in flow transmitter modules.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva & Short-Answer Favorite)

| Feature | Electromagnetic (EMF) | Ultrasonic (Transit-Time) | Thermal Mass (TMFM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | Volumetric flow | Volumetric flow | **Mass flow directly** |
| **Fluid Type** | Conductive liquids only | Clean liquids or dirty w/ particles | Gases (sometimes liquids) |
| **Min. Conductivity** | ≥ 5 μS/cm | N/A | N/A |
| **Pressure Drop** | **None** (no obstruction) | **None** (clamp-on or wetted sensors) | Small (capillary) |
| **Moving Parts** | **None** | **None** | **None** |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.5% to ±1% | ±1% to ±2% | ±1% to ±2% |
| **Turndown Ratio** | 100:1 | 100:1+ | 10:1 to 50:1 |
| **Bidirectional?** | Yes (with bipolar output) | Yes (Δt sign indicates direction) | No (typically) |
| **Calibration Drift** | Very low | Very low | Higher (sensor fouling) |
| **Output** | mV → 4–20 mA | Frequency / time → 4–20 mA | mV / RTD → 4–20 mA |
| **Cost** | High | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Best Applications** | Water, slurries, chemicals | Water, oil, LNG, retrofit | Compressed air, natural gas |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conductive Fluid Inlet] --> B[Magnetic Coil Assembly]
    B --> C[B Field Lines Perpendicular to Flow]
    C --> D[Fluid Flows Through Insulated Liner]
    D --> E[Two Electrodes on Pipe Wall]
    E --> F[Induced EMF E = B·D·v]
    F --> G[Low Noise Preamplifier]
    G --> H[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    H --> I[Microcontroller with Linearization]
    I --> J[Current Loop Output 4 to 20 mA]
    I --> K[HART Modem and Digital Output]
    J --> L[PLC or DCS in Control Room]
    K --> L
    M[Pulsed DC Excitation Supply] --> B
```

### 4.2 Ultrasonic Flow Meter — Transit-Time Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Measurement Cycle] --> B[Transducer T1 Sends Pulse Downstream]
    B --> C[Transducer T2 Receives Pulse]
    C --> D[Record Downstream Time t1]
    D --> E[Transducer T2 Sends Pulse Upstream]
    E --> F[Transducer T1 Receives Pulse]
    F --> G[Record Upstream Time t2]
    G --> H[Compute Delta t = t2 minus t1]
    H --> I{Direction of Flow}
    I -->|Delta t greater than 0| J[Forward Flow]
    I -->|Delta t less than 0| K[Reverse Flow]
    J --> L[Compute v_f using c^2 Delta t over 2L cos theta]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Multiply by Cross Sectional Area to get Q]
    M --> N[Output 4 to 20 mA plus HART]
```

### 4.3 Thermal Mass Flow Meter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Inlet] --> B[Inlet Temperature Sensor T3]
    B --> C[Heater Sensor T1 with Constant Power]
    C --> D[Downstream Temperature Sensor T2]
    D --> E[Bridge Circuit Computes Delta T = T2 minus T3]
    E --> F[Closed Loop Controller Adjusts Heater Power P]
    F --> G[P is Proportional to Mass Flow qm]
    G --> H[Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    H --> I[Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Output 4 to 20 mA plus HART]
    K[Reference Sensor] --> E
```

### 4.4 Flow Transmitter — Generic Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Flow Sensor] --> B[Millivolt or Frequency or RTD Input]
    B --> C[EMI and RFI Filtering]
    C --> D[Preamplifier with High CMRR]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Microprocessor with Linearization]
    F --> G[Square Root Extraction if Needed]
    G --> H[PID or Characterization Block]
    H --> I[Digital to Analog Converter]
    I --> J[Voltage to Current Converter]
    J --> K[4 to 20 mA Output to Field]
    F --> L[HART Modem FSK 1200 bps]
    L --> M[Same Two Wires Superimposed on Loop]
    F --> N[Display and Local Configuration Keys]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and worth 3 marks. Answers should be concise (3–4 sentences) with one supporting equation or diagram where appropriate.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define an Electromagnetic Flow Meter. State any two advantages and one major limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An Electromagnetic Flow Meter (EMF) is a volumetric flow measuring device that operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field **B** perpendicular to the flow direction, an EMF is induced across two electrodes: **E = B · D · v**, where **D** is pipe diameter and **v** is average velocity.

**Advantages (any 2):**
- No moving parts → high reliability, low maintenance.
- No pressure drop (no obstruction in flow path).
- Bidirectional measurement; linear output (no square-root extraction).
- Independent of viscosity, density, and flow profile.

**Limitation:**
- Can only measure **electrically conductive fluids** (conductivity ≥ 5 μS/cm). Non-conductive fluids like oils, hydrocarbons, and pure gases cannot be measured.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between Transit-Time and Doppler methods of ultrasonic flow measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Transit-Time | Doppler Shift |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Measures time difference of sound pulses traveling with and against flow | Measures frequency shift of sound reflected from particles |
| Fluid requirement | **Clean, homogeneous** liquids | Must contain **suspended particles or bubbles** (≥ 100 μm, ≥ 25 ppm) |
| Formula | $v_f \approx \dfrac{c^2 \Delta t}{2L \cos\theta}$ | $v_f = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ |
| Accuracy | Higher (±1%) | Lower (±2–5%) |
| Applications | Water, oil, LNG | Wastewater, slurries, pulp stock |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions are 14 marks each with **internal choice** (either-or). Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Both questions should be prepared; only one is answered in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Electromagnetic & Thermal Flow Meters**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of an Electromagnetic Flow Meter. Derive the relationship between induced EMF and volumetric flow rate. State two industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a Thermal Mass Flow Meter with a suitable diagram. State King's law and explain constant-temperature-difference mode of operation.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle:**

The electromagnetic flow meter (EMF) works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A pair of coils generates a magnetic field **B** perpendicular to the direction of fluid flow. Two electrodes are mounted diametrically opposite on the inside of the pipe wall. When a conductive fluid flows through this field, a voltage **E** is induced across the electrodes proportional to the average velocity **v** of the fluid.

**Diagram (Schematic):**

```
           Magnetic Coils (N and S)
          +---+         +---+
          |   |   B →   |   |
          |   |         |   |
          +---+         +---+
                ↓ Fluid Flow ↓
   =======================================
   |        Insulated Liner (PTFE)      |
   =======================================
    E (+)                            (- E)
    Electrode                         Electrode
```

**Derivation:**

The induced EMF by Faraday's law is:

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

In an EMF pipe, the conductor length = pipe diameter **D**:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

From (2), $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$. Substituting into (1):

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4B \cdot Q}{\pi D}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}}$$

This is a **linear** relationship between Q and E — a major advantage over differential pressure flow meters.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Water and wastewater treatment plants** — measure raw water, treated water, and sludge flow.
2. **Chemical process industries** — measure acid, alkali, and slurry flows.
3. **Food and beverage industry** — milk, juice, beer (sanitary designs with stainless steel and PTFE liners).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Working of Thermal Mass Flow Meter:**

A Thermal Mass Flow Meter consists of three sensors inserted into the flow stream:
- **T1**: Heater sensor (energized electrically with power P).
- **T2**: Temperature sensor downstream.
- **T3**: Temperature sensor upstream (for inlet temperature compensation).

**Diagram:**

```
        ┌──── Flow Direction ────┐
        │                        │
   [T3] │   [T1 Heater]   [T2]   │
    ↑   │      P (W)         ↑   │
   T_in │                   T_out│
        └────────────────────────┘
        ΔT = T2 - T3 measured
```

**Heat is transferred from T1 to the fluid by forced convection**. The amount of heat carried away depends on the mass flow rate. Two operational modes are used:

**King's Law:**

$$\frac{P}{\Delta T} = A + B \cdot q_m^n$$

Where:
- **A** = conductive heat losses (no flow term)
- **B** = convective coefficient
- **n** ≈ 0.5 (laminar) to 0.7 (turbulent)
- **q_m** = mass flow rate

**Constant Temperature Difference Mode:**

A bridge circuit measures the temperature difference ΔT = T2 − T3. If ΔT deviates from a setpoint, the controller adjusts the heater power **P** to bring ΔT back to the setpoint. At equilibrium:

$$P = (A + B \cdot q_m^n) \cdot \Delta T_{set}$$

If A is negligible compared to B·q_m^n:

$$P \approx B \cdot q_m^n \cdot \Delta T_{set} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_m = \left(\frac{P}{B \cdot \Delta T_{set}}\right)^{1/n}$$

**Advantages of Constant ΔT Mode:**
- Linear output: P is directly proportional to q_m.
- Self-validating zero (when flow is zero, P equals a known baseline).
- Better long-term stability and faster response.

**Application:** Compressed air, natural gas, hydrogen, argon in industrial gas panels.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Ultrasonic Flow Meter & Flow Transmitter**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Transit-Time method of ultrasonic flow measurement with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for fluid velocity in terms of measured time difference.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the function of a Smart Flow Transmitter. Discuss the 4–20 mA current loop and HART protocol. A transmitter is calibrated for 0–2000 L/min. What current corresponds to 750 L/min?

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Transit-Time Method:**

In the transit-time method, two ultrasonic transducers are mounted on the outside of the pipe, each acting as both transmitter and receiver. The acoustic path between them makes an angle **θ** with the pipe axis and has path length **L** measured along the beam.

**Block Diagram:**

```
         +----------------------+
         |    Process Pipe      |
         |                      |
  (T1) =====\              /====== (T2)
  Tx/Rx       \            /
                \  θ  θ  /
                 \  |  /
                  \ | /
                   \|/
                    X  (Sound Path, Length L)
                   /|\
                  / | \
                 /  |  \
                /    \____ Flow Direction
```

**Working:**

1. T1 transmits a pulse downstream. T2 receives it. Time = **t₁**.
2. After a short delay, T2 transmits upstream. T1 receives it. Time = **t₂**.
3. The difference **Δt = t₂ − t₁** is computed.

**Derivation:**

- Downstream (sound travels with flow):
$$t_1 = \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$$

- Upstream (sound travels against flow):
$$t_2 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta}$$

- Time difference:
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{L}{c - v_f \cos\theta} - \frac{L}{c + v_f \cos\theta}$$

Combining over common denominator:

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{2 v_f \cos\theta}{c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

Since **v_f ≪ c**:

$$c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta \approx c^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L v_f \cos\theta}{c^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_f = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}}$$

**Advantages:** Non-intrusive (clamp-on version), no pressure drop, bidirectional, suitable for clean and dirty liquids.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Smart Flow Transmitter:**

A flow transmitter is a microprocessor-based field device that:
1. Receives the low-level signal (mV, RTD, frequency) from a flow sensor.
2. Performs **signal conditioning, linearization, square-root extraction, and temperature/pressure compensation**.
3. Outputs a **standardized 4–20 mA** analog signal proportional to flow, optionally with **digital HART communication** on the same two wires.

**4–20 mA Current Loop:**

- The transmitter regulates current in the loop, not voltage. Voltage varies (typically 12–24 V) depending on loop resistance.
- 4 mA = live zero (also powers the transmitter electronics).
- 20 mA = full-scale flow.
- 0 mA = fault condition (broken wire, open circuit).

**Linear Mapping Equation:**

$$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{Q - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}}$$

**HART Protocol:**

- **H**ighway **A**ddressable **R**emote **T**ransducer.
- Digital communication superimposed on the 4–20 mA loop using **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying):
  - "1" → 1200 Hz
  - "0" → 2200 Hz
- Baud rate: 1200 bps (Bell 202 standard).
- Up to **15 slave devices** on a single twisted pair.
- Allows **bi-directional** communication: read process variable, perform remote calibration, run diagnostics, and change configuration.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: **Q_min = 0 L/min**, **Q_max = 2000 L/min**, **Q = 750 L/min**.

$$I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{750 - 0}{2000 - 0} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.375 = 4 + 6.0 = 10.0 \text{ mA}$$

**Answer:** The current corresponding to 750 L/min is **10.0 mA**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams — Flow Measurement Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in EMF derivation:** Always state B in Tesla, D in meters, v in m/s. Carrying units and writing the final answer with units is worth **half a mark** in valuation.
> 2. **Sign convention in transit-time:** Students often forget that **Δt = t₂ − t₁** (upstream − downstream), not the other way around. The sign of Δt indicates flow direction.
> 3. **Approximation in transit-time:** Forgetting to apply **v_f ≪ c** approximation will lead to a complex denominator $c^2 - v_f^2 \cos^2\theta$ and you'll lose **2 marks** for not simplifying.
> 4. **Mixing volumetric and mass flow:** Thermal mass flow meters measure **mass**, not volume. Do not say "volumetric flow" in the answer — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions:** A labeled diagram is worth **2–3 marks** in KTU ESE. Always draw one, even if not explicitly asked.
> 6. **Forgetting the "live zero" concept in 4 mA loop:** A 0 mA is **not** "zero flow" — it's a fault. **4 mA is the live zero**. This is a very common short-answer trap.
> 7. **HART frequencies swapped:** Always remember: **1200 Hz = "1"** (binary 1) and **2200 Hz = "0"** (binary 0). Many students reverse these.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Flow Measurement (Advanced)**

**Electromagnetic Flow Meter (EMF):**
- Principle: **Faraday's Law of Induction**.
- Key equation: $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ → $Q = \dfrac{\pi D}{4B} \cdot E$ (linear in E).
- Requires **conductive fluid** (≥ 5 μS/cm).
- **No pressure drop**, **no moving parts**, **bidirectional**.
- Uses **pulsed DC excitation** to avoid electrode polarization.
- Cannot measure hydrocarbons, oils, or gases (non-conductive).

**Ultrasonic Flow Meter (USM):**
- Two methods: **Transit-Time** (clean fluids) and **Doppler** (dirty fluids with particles).
- Transit-Time formula: $v_f = \dfrac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$.
- Doppler formula: $v_f = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$.
- **Non-intrusive** clamp-on version available.
- Typical accuracy: **±1% to ±2%**.
- Bidirectional (sign of Δt or Δf gives direction).

**Thermal Mass Flow Meter (TMFM):**
- Measures **mass flow directly** (no need for P, T compensation).
- **King's Law**: $\dfrac{P}{\Delta T} = A + B \cdot q_m^n$.
- **Constant ΔT mode** is preferred (linear output, self-validating zero).
- Primarily used for **gases** (compressed air, natural gas, biogas, N₂, Ar).
- Sensitive to **sensor fouling** — needs clean gas.

**Flow Transmitter:**
- Standard output: **4–20 mA current loop** (live zero = 4 mA).
- 0 mA indicates **fault**, not zero flow.
- Linear mapping: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{Q - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}}$.
- **HART protocol**: FSK at 1200 bps; "1" = 1200 Hz, "0" = 2200 Hz.
- Up to **15 HART devices** per loop.
- Performs **linearization, square-root extraction, and P/T compensation** in smart transmitters.
- Used with **DP transmitters, EMF, USM, TMFM, Coriolis, vortex, and turbine** meters.

**Cross-Comparison Golden Rule (Viva):**
- **For liquids — conductive → EMF**
- **For liquids — clean → Ultrasonic Transit-Time**
- **For liquids — dirty/slurry → Ultrasonic Doppler**
- **For gases → Thermal Mass Flow Meter**
- **For mass flow — independent of P, T → Coriolis or Thermal**
- **For steam, high temp → Vortex Shedding**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the system, **write the key equation**, and **state at least one limitation and one application** in long-answer questions. KTU 2024 valuation rewards structured, complete answers with 80%+ marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humidity Measurements - Definitions and Units, Psychrometer, Dew Point Hygrometer, Electrical Resistive Hygrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Humidity Measurements - Definitions and Units, Psychrometer, Dew Point Hygrometer, Electrical Resistive Hygrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Humidity (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Humidity** is defined as the measure of the amount of water vapor present in a gas mixture (typically air). It is a critical process variable in industries such as **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning)**, **pharmaceuticals**, **textile manufacturing**, **food processing**, **paper production**, and **meteorology**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Humidity is the concentration of water vapor in a gaseous environment, expressed either as a mass per unit volume (absolute), a partial pressure ratio (relative), or a mass per unit mass of dry air (specific humidity).

### 1.2 Fundamental Terms and Definitions

| Term | Formal Definition | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|------|-------------------|--------|--------------|
| **Absolute Humidity** | Mass of water vapor per unit volume of moist air | $\rho_v$ | $\text{g/m}^3$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| **Specific Humidity** | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air | $q$ | $\text{kg/kg}$ of dry air |
| **Relative Humidity (RH)** | Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor to saturation pressure at same temperature | $\phi$ | % |
| **Dew Point Temperature** | Temperature at which air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure | $T_{dp}$ | °C or K |
| **Mixing Ratio** | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air | $w$ | $\text{kg/kg}$ dry air |
| **Wet Bulb Temperature** | Temperature a parcel of air would have if cooled adiabatically to saturation by evaporation | $T_w$ | °C |

### 1.3 Standard Units Used in KTU Board Exams

- **Relative Humidity:** Percentage (%), dimensionless ratio.
- **Absolute Humidity:** $\text{grains/ft}^3$, $\text{g/m}^3$, or $\text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Dew Point:** Degrees Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K).
- **Vapor Pressure:** Pascals (Pa), millibars (mbar), or $\text{mm of Hg}$.
- **Humidity Ratio / Mixing Ratio:** $\text{g/kg}$ of dry air or $\text{lb/lb}$ of dry air.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sponge and the Water Bucket"**
> 
> Imagine the air as a **sponge** that can soak up water vapor.
> - **Absolute Humidity** = "How much water is currently in the sponge?"
> - **Relative Humidity** = "How full is the sponge compared to its maximum capacity at this temperature?"
> - **Dew Point** = "The temperature at which the sponge starts dripping (saturates)."
> - **Specific Humidity** = "Mass of water per mass of dry sponge material."
> 
> A hot sponge can hold more water than a cold one. This is why a summer day with 90% RH feels more "humid" than a winter day at 90% RH — the **absolute water content** is much higher in summer.

### 1.5 Why Humidity Measurement is Critical in Industry

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug stability and tablet coating require precise RH.
- **Semiconductors:** Wafer fabrication demands < 1% RH contamination control.
- **Textiles:** Yarn breakage and static electricity depend on moisture content.
- **HVAC:** Human thermal comfort and energy efficiency optimization.
- **Food Storage:** Prevents mold growth and desiccation.
- **Meteorology:** Weather prediction and climate modeling.

### 1.6 The Saturation Curve (Phase Diagram Concept)
