# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between RS-232 and RS-485 communication standards.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **RS-232** is a **single-ended, point-to-point** standard with maximum cable length of **15 m** and maximum speed of **20 kbps**, supporting only 1 driver and 1 receiver. It uses ±12 V signal levels and is used for short-distance PC-to-device links.
- **RS-485** is a **differential, multi-drop** standard with maximum cable length of **1200 m** and maximum speed of **10 Mbps**, supporting up to **32 drivers and 32 receivers** (64 nodes). It uses ±1.5 V to ±6 V differential signal levels and is used in industrial networks.
- RS-485 provides **much better noise immunity** and is the physical layer of industrial protocols like Modbus RTU and Profibus-DP.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a Fieldbus? List any four advantages of fieldbus over conventional 4–20 mA analog signaling.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **Fieldbus** is a digital, bidirectional, multi-drop, serial communication network used to interconnect field devices (sensors, actuators, controllers) in industrial automation.

**Four advantages of Fieldbus over 4–20 mA:**
1. **Reduced wiring** — One twisted pair carries many process variables, instead of one pair per signal.
2. **Bidirectional** — Field devices can both send and receive data (4–20 mA is one-way).
3. **Distributed control** — Control loops can be executed inside field devices, reducing DCS load.
4. **Diagnostic information** — Field devices can transmit calibration, health, and predictive maintenance data.
5. (Bonus) **Interoperability** — Devices from different vendors can communicate on the same bus.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **RS-485 standard** in detail. Discuss its electrical characteristics, topology, and advantages over RS-232. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Describe the **Modbus protocol** with reference to its architecture, frame format, and different variants (RTU, ASCII, TCP). (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- **Definition:** RS-485 is a **TIA/EIA-485** standard for **balanced differential** serial communication.
- **Electrical Characteristics:** Differential signaling between two lines A (−) and B (+); logic 1 when V_A − V_B < −200 mV; logic 0 when V_A − V_B > +200 mV; common-mode range −7 V to +12 V.
- **Topology:** Multi-drop, up to **32 drivers and 32 receivers** on a single bus; **120 Ω termination** at both ends.
- **Performance:** Up to **10 Mbps** at short distances; up to **1200 m** at low speeds; equation $L \times B \le 10^8$ bps·m.
- **Advantages over RS-232:** (1) Differential signaling gives excellent noise immunity; (2) Multi-drop capability (RS-232 is point-to-point only); (3) Much longer distance (1200 m vs 15 m); (4) Higher speed (10 Mbps vs 20 kbps); (5) Supports up to 64 nodes vs 2 nodes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
- **Definition:** Modbus is an open, master-slave protocol developed by Modicon in 1979.
- **Architecture:** **Master (client) initiates**, slave (server) responds. Up to **247 slaves** on a bus.
- **Variants:**
  - **Modbus RTU:** Binary, compact, uses **CRC-16** error check. Most common. Runs on RS-485.
  - **Modbus ASCII:** Human-readable, uses **LRC** checksum. Slower.
  - **Modbus TCP:** Runs over Ethernet on **port 502**, uses **MBAP header** instead of address + CRC.
- **RTU Frame Format:** `[Address (1) | Function (1) | Data (N) | CRC (2)]` with **3.5 character silent intervals** as frame delimiters.
- **Common Function Codes:** 01 (Read Coils), 03 (Read Holding Registers), 05 (Write Single Coil), 06 (Write Single Register), 16 (Write Multiple Registers).

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **Profibus protocol** with its variants, physical layer, frame structure, and access method. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Write a detailed note on **Foundation Fieldbus (FF)**. Compare Foundation Fieldbus H1 and HSE. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- **Definition:** Profibus (Process Field Bus) is an open, vendor-independent fieldbus standardized in **IEC 61158** and **IEC 61784**, governed by **PI (Profibus International)**.
- **Variants:**
  - **Profibus-DP (Decentralized Periphery):** High speed up to 12 Mbps, used for factory automation with RS-485 physical layer.
  - **Profibus-PA (Process Automation):** 31.25 kbps, **Manchester Bus Powered (MBP)**, intrinsically safe, used in process industries.
  - **Profibus-FMS:** Older, replaced by DP and PROFINET.
- **Physical Layer:** DP uses **RS-485** (twisted pair, 9-pin D-sub), PA uses **MBP** per IEC 61158-2.
- **Frame Format:** `[SD | DA | SA | FC | DU | FCS | ED]` — Start delimiter, destination, source, function code, data unit, frame check sequence, end delimiter.
- **Access Method:** **Token Passing** among masters in a logical ring, plus **Master-Slave** for slaves of each master. Token has a maximum hold time.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
- **Definition:** Foundation Fieldbus is an open fieldbus developed by the **Fieldbus Foundation** for process automation, standardized in **IEC 61158** and **ISA 50.02**.
- **H1 (Low Speed):** 31.25 kbps, bus-powered, intrinsically safe, used to connect field instruments. Uses Manchester coding (IEC 61158-2). Up to 32 devices per segment.
- **HSE (High Speed Ethernet):** 100 Mbps, runs on standard Ethernet (TCP/IP), used to connect H1 segments to host systems and to interconnect controllers.
- **Key Concept — Function Blocks:** Foundation Fieldbus supports **distributed control** by allowing function blocks (PID, AI, AO) to be executed in field devices.
- **LAS (Link Active Scheduler):** Centralized scheduler that controls deterministic bus access using CD (Compel Data) and TD (Time Distribution) messages.
- **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | H1 | HSE |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | 31.25 kbps | 100 Mbps |
| Medium | Twisted pair (bus-powered) | Standard Ethernet |
| Topology | Bus, tree, star | Star (Ethernet switches) |
| Use | Field device connection | Host and controller backbone |
| Standard | IEC 61158-2 | IEC 61158-3 to 6 |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing RS-232 with RS-485** — RS-232 is single-ended, ±12 V, 15 m, point-to-point. RS-485 is differential, ±1.5–6 V, 1200 m, multi-drop. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Writing "32 nodes" for RS-485** — Strictly, RS-485 supports **32 drivers and 32 receivers (64 nodes)** OR **32 unit loads**. The "32 devices" simplification is only correct for 1-UL devices.
> 3. **Forgetting termination** — In RS-485 diagrams, students often forget the **120 Ω termination resistors** at both ends. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 4. **Mixing up Profibus variants** — Profibus-DP = RS-485, fast. Profibus-PA = Manchester, 31.25 kbps, intrinsically safe. Students frequently swap them.
> 5. **Modbus CRC vs Modbus TCP** — Modbus TCP has **no CRC** (TCP handles it) and uses an **MBAP header** instead of the slave address. Many students incorrectly include CRC in Modbus TCP frames.
> 6. **Foundation Fieldbus ≠ Profibus** — They are different protocols! FF uses the LAS scheduler; Profibus uses token passing. Examiners love to test this distinction.
> 7. **Skipping the access method** — When asked about a protocol, always mention **how devices access the bus** (master-slave, token passing, CSMA/CD, LAS scheduling).
> 8. **No units in numericals** — Always write units (m, kbps, V, Ω) in your final answers; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **RS-423** is a **single-ended (unbalanced)** standard supporting **1 driver and 10 receivers** up to **1200 m** at **100 kbps** max.
- [x] **RS-485** is the **industrial workhorse** with **differential signaling**, **32 ULs**, **1200 m**, and **10 Mbps** max. Termination = **120 Ω** at both ends.
- [x] **RS-485 vs RS-423:** Differential vs single-ended; multi-drop vs limited multi-drop; better noise immunity for RS-485.
- [x] **Fieldbus** = digital, bidirectional, multi-drop industrial network replacing 4–20 mA.
- [x] **Profibus** is governed by PI; variants are **DP (12 Mbps, RS-485)**, **PA (31.25 kbps, MBP, Ex-safe)**, and legacy **FMS**. Access method = **Token Passing + Master-Slave**.
- [x] **Modbus** is an **open master-slave** protocol by Modicon; **RTU = binary + CRC-16**, **ASCII = LRC**, **TCP = port 502 + MBAP header, no CRC**.
- [x] **Foundation Fieldbus** supports **distributed control** via downloadable function blocks. H1 = 31.25 kbps, HSE = 100 Mbps Ethernet. **LAS** controls deterministic bus access.
- [x] **Physical layer commonality:** Both **Modbus RTU** and **Profibus-DP** use **RS-485** as the physical layer.
- [x] **Typical baud-rate × cable-length product** for RS-485: $L \times B \le 10^8$ bps·m.
- [x] **Application domains:** Modbus (universal/SCADA), Profibus (factory + process), Foundation Fieldbus (refineries/chemical/process industries).
- [x] **Frame delimiters:** Modbus RTU uses **3.5 character silent intervals**; Profibus uses **SD/ED**; FF uses Manchester coding and LAS scheduling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question says "compare" — always end with a **summary table** showing parameters side by side. KTU examiners reward structured answers with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation Diagrams - Instruments Labeling, ISA Standards, Data Sheets, P&ID Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT527 - MEASUREMENTS AND INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Instrumentation Diagrams - Instruments Labeling, ISA Standards, Data Sheets, P&ID Diagrams ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Instrumentation Diagrams** are standardized graphical documents used in process and industrial engineering to communicate the design, operation, and maintenance requirements of measurement and control systems. The **Instrument Society of America (ISA)** — now **International Society of Automation** — publishes the governing standards, primarily **ISA-5.1** for instrumentation symbols and identification, and **ISA-5.2** for binary logic diagrams. **Key diagram types in industrial practice:** 1. **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)** — The master engineering document that integrates process equipment, piping, and the complete instrument loop architecture. 2. **Instrument Loop Diagram (ILD)** — Detailed wiring/signal diagram of a single control loop. 3. **Data Sheets** — Tabular specification documents for individual instruments. 4. **Hookup Drawings** — Installation drawings showing physical mounting of instruments. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Analogy > [!NOTE] > Think of a chemical plant as a **city**, the process piping as its **road network**, and the instruments as the **traffic signals, CCTV cameras, and control rooms**. A **P&ID** is the *city planning blueprint* that shows every signal light, every sensor, every controller, and which road (pipe) it monitors. The **ISA standard** is the *universal language* (like road signs) — it ensures that an engineer in Kerala can read a P&ID designed in Germany without confusion. Without this standard, a `PT` could mean "Pressure Transmitter" to one person and "Power Transformer" to another — chaos! ### 1.3 Why Standards Are Mandatory | Aspect | Without Standard | With ISA Standard | |---|---|---| | **Cross-plant communication** | Inconsistent symbols | Universal recognition | | **Safety** | Misread signals → accidents | Clear hazard identification | | **Procurement** | Ambiguous specs | Precise instrument ordering | | **Maintenance** | Guesswork | Loop drawings are self-explanatory | | **Regulatory compliance** | Fails audits | Meets OSHA, IEC, ISO norms | The **ISA-5.1** standard uses a **tag number system** that uniquely identifies every instrument in the plant with letters and numbers. The first letter indicates the *measured variable*, while subsequent letters describe *function, readout, and output*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 ISA-5.1 Instrument Identification — The Tagging System Every instrument on a P&ID receives a unique alphanumeric tag composed of **4 functional letters** and a **loop number** (e.g., `FT-101`, `LIC-205`, `PDV-303`). **Structure of an Instrument Tag:** $$\text{Tag} = \underbrace{LE}_{\text{Function Letters (1-4)}} - \underbrace{101}_{\text{Loop Number}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **first letter** identifies the *measured variable* (Pressure, Temperature, Flow, etc.). Letters 2, 3, 4 are *function modifiers* (Indicate, Record, Control, Transmit, Valve, etc.). The numeric suffix identifies the *control loop* the instrument belongs to. ### 2.2 ISA-5.1 Letter Definitions (Authoritative Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The first letter designates the MEASURED VARIABLE, not the instrument itself.** A "PT" (Pressure Transmitter) may be measuring the *level* of a tank if preceded by "L" — no, strictly: the *first letter* always governs the variable. Memorize this rule for KTU exams. #### Table 2A — First Letter (Measured Variable) | Letter | Measured Variable | KTU Memory Aid | |---|---|---| | A | Analysis | **A** for chemical **A**nalysis | | B | Burner, Combustion | **B**urning fuel | | C | Conductivity | **C**onductivity of water | | D | Density, Specific Gravity | **D**ense liquids | | E | Voltage (EMF) | **E**lectromotive force | | F | Flow Rate | **F**lowing fluid | | G | Gauging, Dimensional | **G**eometry measurement | | H | Hand (manually initiated) | **H**uman operated | | I | Current (Electrical) | **I** = current symbol | | J | Power | **J**oules per second | | K | Time, Schedule | **K**lock (German) | | L | Level | **L**iquid level | | M | Moisture, Humidity | **M**oisture content | | N | User-defined | Reserved | | O | User-defined | Reserved | | P | Pressure, Vacuum | **P**ressure gauge | | Q | Quantity, Totalization | **Q**uantity counter | | R | Radiation | **R**adioactive | | S | Speed, Frequency, RPM | **S**peed of shaft | | T | Temperature | **T**hermometer | | U | Multivariable | **U**nique combo | | V | Vibration, Viscosity | **V**ibration sensor | | W | Weight, Force | **W**eight on scale | | X | Unclassified | **X** = unknown | | Y | Event, State, Presence | **Y**es/No relay | | Z | Position, Dimension | **Z**-axis position | #### Table 2B — Succeeding Letters (Function Modifiers) | Letter | Function | KTU Memory Aid | |---|---|---| | A | Alarm | **A**larm light | | C | Controller | **C**ontrol loop brain | | E | Element (Primary Sensor) | **E**ye of the loop | | I | Indicator | **I**ndicate on panel | | K | Control Station (Manual) | Hand **K**nob | | L | Light (Low/High) | Indicator **L**amp | | R | Recorder | **R**ecord on chart | | S | Switch, Safety | **S**witch contact | | T | Transmitter | **T**ransmit signal | | V | Valve, Damper, Louver | **V**alve actuator | | Z | Driver, Actuator | Final control element | ### 2.3 Instrument Line Symbols (ISA 5.1) The **type of line** connecting an instrument to the process defines the **signal type**: $$\text{Signal Type Mapping} = \begin{cases} \text{Solid line} & \rightarrow \text{Pneumatic / Hydraulic signal} \\ \text{Dashed line} & \rightarrow \text{Electrical signal} \\ \text{Double dashed} & \rightarrow \text{Capillary / Thermocouple} \\ \text{Double solid} & \rightarrow \text{Mechanical link} \end{cases}$$ | Bubble/Balloon Location | Meaning | |---|---| | Inside process area, capital letters | **Field-mounted, primary location** | | Outside process area, capital letters | **Control room, primary location** | | Lowercase letters in bubble | **Auxiliary/secondary location** | | Slash through bubble (`/`) | **Distributed Control System (DCS) shared display** | | Horizontal line through bubble | **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** | ### 2.4 P&ID Component Classification A P&ID integrates **five distinct categories** of information: 1. **Process Equipment** — Vessels, columns, reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors 2. **Piping** — Process lines, utility lines, drains, vents, with size and material 3. **Instrumentation** — All sensing, control, and final control elements 4. **Control Loops** — Signal flow from sensor → transmitter → controller → valve 5. **Process Information** — Design pressures, temperatures, flow rates, materials > [!NOTE] > A P&ID is the **only document** that shows ALL these elements simultaneously. That's why it's called the *engineer's master document*. ### 2.5 Data Sheets — Instrument Specifications Data sheets are **tabular documents** that accompany P&IDs. They provide all the engineering, mechanical, and process information required to **procure, install, and operate** each instrument. **Standard data sheet sections (as per ISA-20):** | Section | Content | |---|---| | **General** | Tag number, service, manufacturer, model, area classification | | **Process** | Measured variable range, normal/operating/min/max values | | **Performance** | Accuracy (±% of span), repeatability, response time | | **Mechanical** | Materials of wetted parts, process connections, weight | | **Electrical** | Supply voltage, signal output (4-20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus) | | **Environmental** | Ambient temperature, humidity, ingress protection (IP) rating | | **Testing** | Calibration certificates, NDT requirements | > [!IMPORTANT] > For **KTU exams**, expect a data sheet question where you must select appropriate instrument specifications given a process scenario. The exam tests whether you can match **process range** to **instrument range** and **accuracy** requirements. ### 2.6 The 4-20 mA Current Loop — Implicit Formula in P&IDs Since most P&ID instruments use 4-20 mA signalling, the **engineering conversion formula** is fundamental: $$P_{\text{measured}} = P_{\text{LRV}} + \left( \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \right) \times (P_{\text{URV}} - P_{\text{LRV}})$$ Where: - $I$ = Loop current in **mA** - $P_{\text{LRV}}$ = Lower Range Value (corresponds to 4 mA) - $P_{\text{URV}}$ = Upper Range Value (corresponds to 20 mA) - $P_{\text{measured}}$ = Current physical measurement **Live zero principle:** The **4 mA** corresponds to 0% of span (not 0 mA), enabling **open-circuit fault detection** — if the controller reads 0 mA, the wire is broken! ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refineries & Petrochemicals**: P&IDs are mandated by law (OSHA 1910.119 PSM standard) - **Power Plants**: Used to coordinate DCS operations across boilers, turbines - **Pharmaceuticals**: FDA requires P&IDs as part of cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) documentation - **Water Treatment**: Help trace chemical dosing loops and SCADA integration - **KTU Employability**: Graduate engineers in instrumentation, chemical, and mechanical roles routinely read, design, and revise P&IDs in plant engineering firms like L&T, Honeywell, Emerson, ABB, and TechnipFMC ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: ISA Tag Decoding **Problem Framework:** Decode the instrument tag `TIC-203` on a P&ID. **Step 1 — Split the tag into functional and numeric components:** $$\text{TIC-203} = \underbrace{\text{T}}_{\text{Letter 1}} + \underbrace{\text{I}}_{\text{Letter 2}} + \underbrace{\text{C}}_{\text{Letter 3}} - \underbrace{\text{203}}_{\text{Loop Number}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply ISA-5.1 rule (first letter = measured variable):** From Table 2A: `T` = **Temperature** Therefore the instrument **measures TEMPERATURE**. **Step 3 — Decode Letter 2 (modifier function):** From Table 2B: `I` = **Indicator** Therefore the instrument **INDICATES** (displays) the value on a panel. **Step 4 — Decode Letter 3 (modifier function):** From Table 2B: `C` = **Controller** Therefore the instrument is a **CONTROLLER** that receives input and produces an output to a final control element. **Step 5 — Decode the loop number:** `203` indicates this instrument belongs to **control loop number 203** in the plant's numbering scheme. **Step 6 — Construct the full descriptive meaning:** > [!NOTE] > **Final Decoded Description:** *TIC-203 is a **Temperature Indicating Controller** belonging to **Loop 203** of the plant. It receives a temperature signal (likely from TT-203), compares it to a setpoint, displays the value, and outputs a corrective signal to a Temperature Control Valve (TCV-203).* ### 3.2 Worked Example: 4-20 mA Signal Conversion **Given:** A differential pressure transmitter (PT-101) has a calibrated range of **0-500 kPa**. The loop current reads **12 mA**. Find the measured pressure. **Step 1 — Identify the parameters:** $$P_{\text{LRV}} = 0 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{\text{URV}} = 500 \text{ kPa}, \quad I = 12 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the 4-20 mA conversion formula:** $$ P_{\text{measured}} = P_{\text{LRV}} + \left( \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \right) \times (P_{\text{URV}} - P_{\text{LRV}}) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$ P_{\text{measured}} = 0 + \left( \frac{12 - 4}{16} \right) \times (500 - 0) $$ **Step 4 — Simplify the bracket:** $$ P_{\text{measured}} = 0 + \left( \frac{8}{16} \right) \times 500 $$ **Step 5 — Compute final value:** $$ P_{\text{measured}} = 0.5 \times 500 = 250 \text{ kPa} $$ **Verification:** At 12 mA, we are exactly at **50% of span** (since 12 mA is 8 mA above 4 mA, out of 16 mA span). 50% of 500 kPa = 250 kPa. ✓ ### 3.3 Span, Range, and Turndown Analysis **Definitions (formally derived):** $$\text{Static Pressure Span} = P_{\text{URV}} - P_{\text{LRV}}$$ $$ \text{Turndown Ratio (TDR)} = \frac{P_{\text{URV}}}{P_{\text{LRV}}} \quad \text{(when LRV is not zero, e.g., absolute pressure)} $$ **Why Turndown matters:** Instruments with high turndown (e.g., 100:1) can measure both tiny and large flow rates with acceptable accuracy. Orifice plates have limited turndown (~3:1), while Coriolis meters achieve 100:1. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instrument Tag → Function Mapping | Tag | 1st Letter (Variable) | 2nd Letter | 3rd Letter | Decoded Function | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | PT-101 | P (Pressure) | T (Transmit) | — | Pressure Transmitter | Line pressure sensing | | TT-205 | T (Temperature) | T (Transmit) | — | Temperature Transmitter | Reactor temperature | | FT-310 | F (Flow) | T (Transmit) | — | Flow Transmitter | Orifice plate DP cell | | LT-115 | L (Level) | T (Transmit) | — | Level Transmitter | Tank level | | TIC-203 | T (Temperature) | I (Indicate) | C (Control) | Temperature Indicating Controller | Jacketed reactor | | LCV-401 | L (Level) | C (Control) | V (Valve) | Level Control Valve | Surge drum drain | | PDS-507 | P (Pressure) | D (Differential) | S (Switch) | Pressure Differential Switch | Filter clogging alarm | | FQI-220 | F (Flow) | Q (Quantity) | I (Indicate) | Flow Totalizer Indicator | Custody transfer | | XV-610 | X (Unclassified) | V (Valve) | — | On/Off Solenoid Valve | Emergency shutdown | | EAL-708 | E (Voltage) | A (Alarm) | L (Light) | Voltage Alarm Light | Generator trip | | KIC-901 | K (Time) | I (Indicate) | C (Control) | Time Indicating Controller | Batch process timer | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** You will be asked to identify the meaning of tags like `FE-101`, `RC-202`, `LIC-103`, etc. Always start with the **first letter** — it dictates the **measured variable**. Subsequent letters are *function modifiers only*. ### 3.5 Comprehensive Derivation: Loop Number Assignment **The loop number follows plant-area conventions:** $$ \text{Loop Number} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{Area}} \underbrace{01}_{\text{Loop in area}} $$ **Example Conventions in KTU-relevant industry practice:** | Area | Range | Plant Section | |---|---|---| | 1xx | 100-199 | Feed / Raw material handling | | 2xx | 200-299 | Reactor / Conversion section | | 3xx | 300-399 | Separation / Distillation | | 4xx | 400-499 | Storage / Tank farm | | 5xx | 500-599 | Utilities (steam, water, air) | | 6xx | 600-699 | Product finishing | | 9xx | 900-999 | Safety / ESD / Fire protection | > [!IMPORTANT] > Loop numbers are *not random* — they follow the **process flow direction** (upstream → downstream). This helps engineers immediately locate the instrument on the P&ID. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Typical Control Loop on a P&ID ```mermaid flowchart LR proc1[("PROCESS
VARIABLE")] sens["PRIMARY
SENSOR E"] xmit["TRANSMITTER T"] ctrl["CONTROLLER C
I = Indicate
R = Record"] fcvalve["FINAL CONTROL
ELEMENT V
VALVE/DAMPER"] out1[("ACTUATED
PROCESS CHANGE")] dcs1["DCS DISPLAY
Slash through bubble"] alarm1["ALARM A
High / Low"] pan1[("CONTROL ROOM
UPPERCASE")] proc1 --> sens sens --> xmit xmit --> ctrl ctrl --> fcvalve fcvalve --> out1 xmit -.-> dcs1 ctrl -.-> alarm1 ctrl --> pan1 ``` ### 4.2 ISA Instrument Tag Decoding Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start1["INSTRUMENT TAG
Example TIC 203"] split1["SPLIT INTO COMPONENTS"] let1["FIRST LETTER
Measured Variable"] letn["SUBSEQUENT LETTERS
Function Modifiers"] loop1["NUMERIC SUFFIX
Loop Number"] start1 --> split1 split1 --> let1 split1 --> letn split1 --> loop1 let1 --> var1["P Pressure"] let1 --> var2["T Temperature"] let1 --> var3["F Flow"] let1 --> var4["L Level"] let1 --> var5["A Analysis"] let1 --> var6["E Voltage"] let1 --> var7["S Speed"] letn --> fn1["I Indicate"] letn --> fn2["R Record"] letn --> fn3["C Control"] letn --> fn4["T Transmit"] letn --> fn5["A Alarm"] letn --> fn6["V Valve"] letn --> fn7["S Switch"] letn --> fn8["E Element"] loop1 --> l1["100s Raw Material"] loop1 --> l2["200s Reactor"] loop1 --> l3["300s Separation"] loop1 --> l4["500s Utilities"] loop1 --> l5["900s Safety ESD"] ``` ### 4.3 ISA Line Convention Reference Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR bubble1(("○")) bubble2(("◇")) bubble3(("⬡")) bubble4(["☐"]) procNode["PROCESS
PIPING"] bubble1 ---|"SOLID LINE
Pneumatic Signal"| procNode bubble2 -.->|"DASHED LINE
Electrical Signal"| procNode bubble3 == "DOUBLE DASH
Capillary" ==> procNode bubble4 ~~~ "DOUBLE SOLID
Mechanical Link" ~~~ procNode bubble1 -->|"Capital letters
Field mounted"| loc1["FIELD LOCATION"] bubble2 -->|"Capital letters
Control room"| loc2["CONTROL ROOM"] bubble3 -->|"Lowercase
Auxiliary"| loc3["AUXILIARY PANEL"] bubble4 -->|"Slash through
DCS"| loc4["DCS SHARED DISPLAY"] ``` ### 4.4 P&ID Component Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph pidBlock ["P&ID MASTER DOCUMENT - ISA 5.1 COMPLIANT"] blockA["PROCESS EQUIPMENT
Vessels, Pumps, Heat Exchangers"] blockB["PIPING NETWORK
Process Lines, Utility Headers"] blockC["INSTRUMENTATION LAYER
Field Sensors, Transmitters, Valves"] blockD["CONTROL LAYER
Controllers, DCS, PLC, Indicators"] blockE["DATA LAYER
Data Sheets, Hookup Drawings, ILD"] end blockA --- blockB blockB --- blockC blockC --- blockD blockD --- blockE pidInfo["STANDARD REFERENCES
ISA 5.1 Symbols and Identification
ISA 5.2 Binary Logic
ISA 5.3 P&ID Drafting
ISA 20 Instrument Data Sheets"] blockE --> pidInfo ``` ### 4.5 Data Sheet Sectional Structure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD ds["INSTRUMENT DATA SHEET"] s1["GENERAL INFO
Tag, Service, Area Class"] s2["PROCESS DATA
Range, Units, Operating Values"] s3["PERFORMANCE
Accuracy, Repeatability"] s4["MECHANICAL
Materials, Connections"] s5["ELECTRICAL
Supply, Output Signal"] s6["ENVIRONMENTAL
IP Rating, Temperature"] s7["TESTING
Calibration, NDT"] ds --> s1 ds --> s2 ds --> s3 ds --> s4 ds --> s5 ds --> s6 ds --> s7 ``` > [!NOTE] > Mermaid diagrams above provide a **functional architecture view** of the P&ID, instrument tagging system, line conventions, and data sheet structure. They are designed to render cleanly in GitHub, GitLab, and most Mermaid v10+ viewers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** *Explain the ISA-5.1 instrument tagging system. Decode the instrument tag `PT-101` and describe its function in a process plant.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **ISA-5.1 standard** (now also adopted as **ANSI/ISA-5.1-2009**) provides a uniform system for identifying instruments on P&IDs through alphanumeric tags. Each tag has **functional letters** (1 to 4 letters) and a **loop number** (numeric suffix). **Decoding `PT-101`:** - **First letter `P`** → Measured variable = **Pressure** (from ISA-5.1 first-letter table) - **Second letter `T`** → Function modifier = **Transmitter** (sends a standardized signal) - **Loop number `101`** → Belongs to **control loop 101**, located in area 100 (raw material handling section, per typical plant numbering) **Function:** `PT-101` is a **Pressure Transmitter** in loop 101. It senses the process pressure, converts it into a standardized analog signal (typically **4-20 mA** with **HART** protocol), and transmits it to the controller, indicator, or DCS. It serves as the *primary measurement device* in the pressure control loop. --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q2. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between P&ID and PFD. List at least four components typically shown on a P&ID.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | PFD (Process Flow Diagram) | P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Shows overall process flow | Shows detailed piping + instrumentation | | **Detail Level** | Simplified, conceptual | Engineering-grade, detailed | | **Instruments** | NOT shown | Shown with ISA tags and bubbles | | **Piping** | Major lines only | All lines including drains, vents | | **Equipment** | Block representation | Detailed mechanical symbols | | **Use** | Conceptual design, operator overview | Construction, commissioning, maintenance | **Four typical P&ID components:** 1. **Process equipment** — pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, vessels, columns 2. **Piping** — process lines, utility lines, drains, vents (with size and material specifications) 3. **Instruments** — sensors, transmitters, controllers, indicators (with ISA tags) 4. **Control valves and actuators** — final control elements with actuation details 5. **Safety devices** — relief valves, rupture discs, ESD systems --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 - Past Paper Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the ISA-5.1 standard for instrumentation symbols. Describe the various instrument line symbols and balloon conventions used in P&IDs with neat diagrams.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction to ISA-5.1:** The **ISA-5.1 standard** (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification) is the universally accepted standard for depicting instruments and their interconnections on P&IDs and other engineering drawings. It was developed by the **Instrument Society of America** (now International Society of Automation) and ensures that engineers worldwide can interpret instrumentation diagrams without ambiguity. **Step 2 — Instrument Balloons (Bubbles):** Instruments are represented by **circles** (balloons) with attached alphanumeric tags. Key conventions: - **Plain circle** with capital letters → Field-mounted primary instrument - **Circle with a horizontal line through it** → Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) based instrument - **Circle with a diagonal slash (/)** → Distributed Control System (DCS) shared display instrument - **Lowercase letters inside the circle** → Auxiliary or secondary location (e.g., computer-based) - **No circle / Square instead** → Computer, PLC, or DCS mounted instrument **Step 3 — Line Symbol Conventions:** The line connecting an instrument to the process represents the **signal type**: | Line Type | Meaning | |---|---| | **Solid line** | Pneumatic, hydraulic, or capillary signal | | **Dashed line** | Electrical signal (4-20 mA, digital) | | **Dotted line** | Electromagnetic, sonic, or wireless signal | | **Double solid line** | Mechanical linkage | | **Double dashed line** | Capillary tube (for filled-system thermometers) | **Step 4 — Location Identification:** - **All capital letters** in the tag → Primary location (field or main control room) - **Lowercase letters** → Auxiliary location - **Bubble with no letters** → Instrument exists but is unclassified **Step 5 — Two-letter combinations example:** A typical **PT** (Pressure Transmitter) symbol on P&ID: - **Circle with "PT"** inside → Pressure transmitter in field - **Circle with "PT" and slash** → Pressure transmitter on DCS display - **Circle with "PT" and horizontal line** → Pressure transmitter inside PLC **Conclusion:** ISA-5.1 unifies the language of instrumentation engineering, enabling safe and efficient plant operation. --- **(b) (7 Marks)** *A control loop uses a temperature transmitter with a calibrated range of 100°C to 300°C. The loop current is measured as 14 mA. Calculate the measured temperature. Also, explain the concept of "live zero" in the 4-20 mA standard.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify given parameters:** $$T_{\text{LRV}} = 100^\circ \text{C}, \quad T_{\text{URV}} = 300^\circ \text{C}, \quad I = 14 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the 4-20 mA linear conversion formula:** $$ T_{\text{measured}} = T_{\text{LRV}} + \left( \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \right) \times (T_{\text{URV}} - T_{\text{LRV}}) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$ T_{\text{measured}} = 100 + \left( \frac{14 - 4}{16} \right) \times (300 - 100) $$ **Step 4 — Compute numerator of the fraction:** $$ T_{\text{measured}} = 100 + \left( \frac{10}{16} \right) \times 200 $$ **Step 5 — Calculate the bracket value:** $$ T_{\text{measured}} = 100 + 0.625 \times 200 $$ **Step 6 — Final result:** $$ T_{\text{measured}} = 100 + 125 = 225^\circ \text{C} $$ **Verification:** 14 mA is 10 mA above the zero (4 mA). Span is 16 mA. So percentage = 10/16 = 62.5%. Temperature span = 200°C. So 62.5% of 200°C = 125°C above the LRV of 100°C = 225°C. ✓ **Explanation of "Live Zero":** > [!NOTE] > The **4-20 mA standard** uses 4 mA (not 0 mA) as the zero point. This is called the **"live zero"** principle. Its advantages: > 1. **Open-circuit detection:** If the wire breaks or the instrument loses power, the current drops to 0 mA, which is *outside* the valid 4-20 mA range, immediately alerting the controller to a **fault condition**. > 2. **Transmitter powering:** The 4 mA minimum provides enough current to **power the transmitter's internal electronics** through the loop itself (2-wire loop-powered design). > 3. **Signal integrity:** Zero current (0 mA) is reserved exclusively as a **fault signal**, eliminating ambiguity between "zero reading" and "broken wire". --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) (7 Marks)** *List and explain the components of a typical P&ID with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of P&IDs in process plant operations.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of P&ID:** A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed engineering schematic that depicts the interconnection of process equipment, piping, instrumentation, and control systems in an industrial plant. It is governed by **ISA-5.1** and **ISA-5.3** standards. **Step 2 — Major Components of a P&ID:** | Component | Description | |---|---| | **1. Process Equipment** | Vessels, reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, filters, mixers (shown with standardized mechanical symbols) | | **2. Piping Network** | All process lines, utility lines, drains, vents, bypasses, with size, material, and service tags | | **3. Instrumentation** | All sensors, transmitters, controllers, indicators, recorders (shown as ISA balloons with tags) | | **4. Control Valves** | Final control elements including globe valves, butterfly valves, control valves with pneumatic/electric actuators | | **5. Safety Devices** | Pressure Relief Valves (PRV), Rupture Discs, Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves, alarms | | **6. Utility Connections** | Steam, cooling water, instrument air, electrical supplies | | **7. Annotations** | Process conditions (P, T, Flow), equipment numbers, line numbers, special notes | **Step 3 — Block Diagram of P&ID Architecture:** The P&ID document integrates five information layers: - **Process Layer** → Equipment and pipes - **Instrumentation Layer** → Field devices - **Control Layer** → Controllers and DCS - **Safety Layer** → Alarms, trips, interlocks - **Information Layer** → Data sheets, hookups **Step 4 — Role of P&ID in Plant Operations:** 1. **Design and Engineering** — Used by process engineers to design control strategies 2. **Construction** — Acts as the master reference for pipe fabricators and instrument fitters 3. **Commissioning** — Guides loop checking, instrument calibration, and signal tracing 4. **Operations** — Operators use it for startup, shutdown, and emergency response 5. **Maintenance** — Maintenance crews use it to locate valves, instruments, and isolation points 6. **Safety and HAZOP** — Essential for Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) studies 7. **Regulatory Compliance** — Required by OSHA, EPA, and PESO for audit purposes 8. **Training** — Used for training new operators and engineers **Conclusion:** The P&ID is the **single most important document** in any process plant. A well-maintained P&ID is the *backbone of plant integrity*. --- **(b) (7 Marks)** *With reference to a data sheet for a pressure transmitter, list the typical sections and explain the importance of each. Why is the "accuracy" specification critical in instrument selection?* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Data Sheet Purpose:** An instrument data sheet is a **detailed technical specification document** that provides all information needed for procurement, installation, calibration, and operation of a single instrument. The standard format is given by **ISA-20**. **Step 2 — Typical Data Sheet Sections (Tabulated):** | Section | Content | Importance | |---|---|---| | **1. General** | Tag number, service description, P&ID reference, area classification (hazardous/non-hazardous), manufacturer, model | Enables **unambiguous identification** and procurement | | **2. Process Conditions** | Measured variable, units, range (LRV, URV), normal operating value, design pressure/temperature, fluid properties | Ensures the instrument **survives process conditions** and is **sized correctly** | | **3. Performance** | Accuracy (% of span or % of reading), repeatability, linearity, hysteresis, response time, stability | Guarantees the instrument **meets measurement quality requirements** | | **4. Mechanical** | Body material, wetted parts material, process connection type/size, weight, mounting style | Ensures **compatibility with process fluid** and mechanical installation | | **5. Electrical** | Power supply voltage, signal output type (4-20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus), cable entry, electrical classification | Defines **integration with control system** | | **6. Environmental** | Ambient temperature limits, humidity range, IP/NEMA rating, vibration tolerance | Ensures the instrument operates in **field conditions** | | **7. Testing and Calibration** | Factory calibration, NDT requirements, witness testing, certificate requirements | Ensures **traceable quality assurance** | | **8. Accessories and Spares** | Manifolds, mounting brackets, calibration certificates, spare parts list | Supports **installation and lifecycle** | **Step 3 — Critical Importance of Accuracy:** **Accuracy** is defined as the **maximum deviation of the measured value from the true value**, expressed as a percentage of span or reading. **Why Accuracy is critical:** 1. **Process Control Quality** — Inaccurate measurement → incorrect control action → poor product quality 2. **Safety** — Pressure transmitters on reactors/boilers must be accurate to **prevent overpressure accidents** 3. **Economy** — For *custody transfer* (oil & gas pipelines), ±0.1% accuracy can mean **millions of dollars** difference annually 4. **Regulatory Compliance** — Environmental emissions monitoring requires **legally defensible** accuracy 5. **Calibration Interval** — Lower accuracy drift extends calibration intervals, **reducing maintenance cost** **Mathematical Representation:** $$ \text{Inaccuracy} = \pm \frac{a}{100} \times \text{Span} $$ **Example:** A pressure transmitter with **±0.075% accuracy** and span of **0-1000 kPa** has: $$ \text{Inaccuracy} = \pm \frac{0.075}{100} \times 1000 = \pm 0.75 \text{ kPa} $$ This is acceptable for **custody transfer** but might be insufficient for **high-precision reactor control**, where ±0.04% would be required. **Step 4 — Conclusion:** The data sheet is a **legally binding technical document** that bridges engineering design, procurement, and operations. Accuracy selection must be **justified economically and safely**, not arbitrarily. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU P&ID / ISA Tagging Questions:** > > 1. **Reversing the First Letter Rule** — Students often write "PT = Power Transformer" instead of "Pressure Transmitter". The **first letter ALWAYS defines the measured variable** (Power = `J` in ISA notation, not `P`). Losing 1-2 marks per question. > > 2. **Confusing P&ID with PFD** — The P&ID is NOT a process flow diagram. It must show **all instruments with ISA tags**, **all pipes**, and **all control valves**. Drawing an instrument without a tag = 1 mark lost. > > 3. **Forgetting the "Live Zero"** — Many students write 0-20 mA instead of **4-20 mA** in signal conversion problems. The 4 mA live zero is a fundamental principle. > > 4. **Unit Errors in Conversions** — Mixing kPa, bar, psi, and mmH₂O without proper conversion factor. Always use **SI units** unless specifically asked otherwise. > > 5. **Omitting Data Sheet Sections** — A "list the data sheet sections" question expects at least **6-8 sections**. Writing only 3 will lose 4-5 marks. > > 6. **Ignoring ISA 5.1 Symbol Conventions** — Drawing a control valve as a butterfly (mechanical symbol) instead of a **bubble with `V`** in the function letter is a common error. > > 7. **Bubble Location Misinterpretation** — A bubble with **capital letters** does NOT mean "field"; it means "primary location" which could be **control room or field**. The location is indicated by **a line connecting to the process**, not the case of letters. > > 8. **Not Drawing Line Symbols** — Students often omit the **dashed line for electrical signals** vs **solid line for pneumatic** in P&ID loop drawings. Always specify the line type. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Instrumentation Diagrams: Top Revision Points** - ✅ **ISA-5.1** is the governing standard for **instrument symbols and identification** - ✅ Every instrument tag has **functional letters** (1-4) + **loop number** - ✅ **First letter** = Measured variable. **Subsequent letters** = Function modifiers - ✅ Common tags: `PT` (Pressure Tx), `TT` (Temperature Tx), `FT` (Flow Tx), `LT` (Level Tx), `TIC` (Temp Indicating Controller), `LCV` (Level Control Valve) - ✅ **Solid line** = Pneumatic, **Dashed line** = Electrical, **Dotted line** = Wireless/EM - ✅ **Capital letters in bubble** = Primary location, **Lowercase** = Auxiliary - ✅ **Slash through bubble** = DCS display, **Horizontal line** = PLC - ✅ **4-20 mA** is the industry-standard current signal with **live zero** at 4 mA - ✅ **P&ID integrates** equipment, piping, instrumentation, control, and safety - ✅ **Data sheets** contain 8 sections: General, Process, Performance, Mechanical, Electrical, Environmental, Testing, Accessories - ✅ **Accuracy** is expressed as **±% of span or % of reading** - ✅ **Loop numbering** typically follows process flow (100s raw, 200s reactor, 300s separation, 500s utilities, 900s safety) - ✅ **PFD ≠ P&ID** — PFD is simplified; P&ID is detailed - ✅ **ISA-5.2** governs binary logic diagrams - ✅ **ISA-20** governs data sheets - ✅ Conversion formula: $P = P_{\text{LRV}} + \left( \frac{I-4}{16} \right) \times (P_{\text{URV}} - P_{\text{LRV}})$ - ✅ **Turndown Ratio** = $\frac{\text{URV}}{\text{LRV}}$ (for non-zero LRV instruments) - ✅ Mermaid diagrams show **functional architecture**; real P&IDs use **standardized CAD symbols** - ✅ For KTU exams: **always state the standard** (e.g., "As per ISA-5.1...") when answering P&ID questions > **END OF KTU 2024 MODULE 4 NOTES — INSTRUMENTATION DIAGRAMS**
